title: [MAJOR] Backup/recovery not atomic — crash can corrupt both main and backup
description: src/infrastructure/adapters/file-state-store.ts:61-81 — Single-generation backup: backupCurrentState() overwrites the backup before the main write completes. If process crashes between backup and rename, both files can be invalid simultaneously. Fix: implement two-generation backup rotation (bak.json -> bak2.json before overwriting bak.json).
notes: Domain: Reliability. Location: src/infrastructure/adapters/file-state-store.ts:61-81. Fix: Add two-generation backup rotation — rename bak.json to bak2.json before overwriting bak.json. Recovery tries bak.json first, then bak2.json. Verify: simulate crash between backup and rename; confirm recovery uses bak2.json fallback.
close_reason: Fixed in 8e31e95: two-generation backup rotation

title: [MAJOR] Checksum mismatch recovery returns state without structural validation
description: src/infrastructure/adapters/file-state-store.ts:150-168 — When checksum validation fails, parseStateJson clears gateResults but returns the spread state without validating required fields (sessionId, status, currentNodePath, variables, nodeProgress). Partially corrupted JSON files can propagate invalid state, causing runtime crashes in renderFlow or evaluateCompletion.
notes: Domain: Reliability. Location: src/infrastructure/adapters/file-state-store.ts:150-168. Fix: Add isStateStructureValid() check before returning spread state; throw if required fields (sessionId, status, currentNodePath, variables, nodeProgress) are missing or wrong type. Verify: corrupt nodeProgress in state file, confirm load throws instead of propagating.
close_reason: Fixed in 8e31e95: isStateStructureValid() added

title: [MAJOR] Unbounded JSON.parse in resolveArrayIndex — DoS via large payloads
description: src/domain/interpolate.ts:14-27 — resolveArrayIndex calls JSON.parse on variable values from run output with no size limit. Attacker-controlled command output with deeply nested JSON or million-char strings can cause DoS. Add size/nesting limits before parsing.
notes: Domain: Security. Location: src/domain/interpolate.ts:14-27. Fix: Truncate value to 100KB before JSON.parse; reject arrays with >10000 elements. Verify: unit test with deeply nested JSON and million-char strings; confirm parsing completes in <100ms.
close_reason: Fixed in 8e31e95: MAX_ARRAY_INDEX_PAYLOAD + MAX_ARRAY_INDEX_ELEMENTS limits

title: [MAJOR] Shell injection in CI mode — use spawn() instead of execSync
description: bin/cli.mjs:375-376 — ci() function escapes user flow text with single-quote replacement then passes to execSync with string interpolation. Should use spawn() with argv array to completely eliminate shell parsing surface.
notes: Domain: Security. Location: bin/cli.mjs:375-376. Fix: Replace execSync with spawn('claude', ['-p', '--dangerously-skip-permissions', flowText], {stdio:'inherit',timeout:600000}). Verify: test with adversarial flow inputs containing embedded quotes and shell metacharacters.
close_reason: Closed

title: [MAJOR] D05: Loop with timeoutSeconds=0 always exits
description: advance-flow.ts:165 uses timeoutSeconds != null which triggers timeout for 0. Fix: changed to timeoutSeconds > 0 so 0 means no timeout.
acceptance_criteria: timeoutSeconds=0 does not trigger timeout.
close_reason: Fixed in Fagan Inspection rework phase.

title: [MAJOR] D04: Domain render-flow imports node:crypto
description: render-flow.ts imports createHash from node:crypto, violating the domain zero-dependency rule. Fix: replaced with pure FNV-1a hash function.
acceptance_criteria: No node: imports in render-flow.ts.
close_reason: Fixed in Fagan Inspection rework phase.

title: [MAJOR] D03: readBackupState only triggered on SyntaxError
description: file-state-store.ts:141 only catches SyntaxError for backup fallback. Other corruption types (checksum mismatch, encoding errors) re-throw instead of trying backup. Fix: catch all non-ENOENT errors and attempt backup.
acceptance_criteria: All read errors on existing file fall back to backup.
close_reason: Fixed in Fagan Inspection rework phase.

title: [MAJOR] D02: TOCTOU race — backup outside write lock
description: file-state-store.ts:74 calls backupCurrentState() before acquiring write lock, creating a TOCTOU race. Fix: moved backup inside withLock() callback.
acceptance_criteria: backup call is inside withLock() callback.
close_reason: Fixed in Fagan Inspection rework phase.

title: [MAJOR] D01: Shell injection via cmd.exe in ci() command
description: bin/cli.mjs:376 uses shell string interpolation with single-quote escaping which fails on Windows cmd.exe. Fix: replaced execSync with execFileSync using array args.
acceptance_criteria: execFileSync used with array args, no shell string building.
close_reason: Fixed in Fagan Inspection rework phase.

title: [CRITICAL] CLI ci() shell injection — POSIX escaping broken on Windows
description: bin/cli.mjs lines 375-376: ci() uses POSIX single-quote escaping then passes to execSync which invokes cmd.exe on Windows. Single-quote escaping provides zero protection on cmd.exe. Additionally, null bytes in flow text can truncate/inject commands. Fix: use spawnSync with argument array or pass flow via stdin.
close_reason: ci() uses execFileSync with argument array (no shell), plus null byte guard added

title: fix: marketplace.json installInstructions says npm install (wrong)
description: .claude-plugin/marketplace.json:16 says 'npm install @45ck/prompt-language'. Correct install is 'npx @45ck/prompt-language'. npm install adds it as a project dependency, doesn't install the plugin. File: .claude-plugin/marketplace.json
close_reason: Closed

title: fix: flow-executor skill incorrectly documents gate predicates as runtime variables
description: skills/flow-executor/SKILL.md lines 35-40 list tests_pass, tests_fail, lint_pass, lint_fail, file_exists, diff_nonempty as 'automatically updated after each run step'. These are NOT runtime variables — they are gate predicates resolved on-demand via resolveBuiltinCommand(). Misleads users into thinking if tests_pass uses a cached variable (it doesn't — it runs npm test). Fix: separate the two sections clearly.
close_reason: Closed

title: P0 Bug: H44 cat fails on Windows
description: scripts/eval/comparative-eval.mjs testH44(): let token = run "cat target.txt" uses Unix cat which doesn't exist on Windows. Plugin variant will false-fail, invalidating the comparison. Fix: use node -e "process.stdout.write(require('fs').readFileSync('target.txt','utf8'))" or platform-detect type vs cat. Acceptance: H44 eval passes on Windows.
close_reason: Closed

title: fix: file_exists path validation rejects spaces, allows absolute paths
description: SAFE_PATH_RE = /^(?!.*\.\.)[\w./-]+$/ blocks spaces (common macOS/Windows paths like dist/my file.js). Also allows absolute paths (/etc/passwd) — should require relative only. Silent failure: unresolvable gate = user stuck forever with no error. File: src/application/evaluate-completion.ts:29. Fix: update regex to allow spaces in quoted paths, reject leading /.
close_reason: Closed

title: Complete evaluation matrix: re-run H19-H21, run H22-H31
description: 7 hypotheses (H22-H28) need first run, 3 (H19-H21) need re-run after scoring bug fix (1035c6f), 3 context management experiments (H29-H31) need first run. Run full comparative-eval, update eval-analysis.md and README eval section with results.

title: [MAJOR] D6: let run does not interpolate variables in command
description: run: nodes interpolate variables but let x = run cmd does not. Missing interpolate() call in let run-source case.
acceptance_criteria: Add interpolate() before commandRunner.run() in let case.
close_reason: Fixed and verified

title: [MAJOR] D5: let run stores empty string without commandRunner instead of bailing
description: let with run source stores empty string and advances when no commandRunner. Should bail out like run nodes do.
acceptance_criteria: Bail out when commandRunner is undefined.
close_reason: Fixed and verified

title: [MAJOR] D4: DSL reference conflates auto-set variables with gate predicates
description: DSL_REFERENCE lists gate predicates as auto-set variables, misleading the LLM meta-prompt.
acceptance_criteria: Split into variables vs gate predicates sections.
close_reason: Fixed and verified

title: [MAJOR] D3: User prompt silently discarded when prompt node captures
description: When capturedPrompt is set, injectContext drops the user input.prompt entirely. Intentional but needs explicit test and documentation.
acceptance_criteria: Add explicit test documenting intentional behavior.
close_reason: Fixed and verified

title: [MAJOR] D2: file_exists path not shell-quoted, allows path traversal
description: test -f path does not quote the path. SAFE_PATH_RE allows backslash and .. for path traversal.
acceptance_criteria: Quote path in generated command. Add .. rejection to regex.
close_reason: Fixed and verified

title: [MAJOR] D1: Shell injection via variable interpolation in run commands
description: interpolate() substitutes variable values directly into shell commands passed to exec(). Shell metacharacters in variable values cause arbitrary command execution.
acceptance_criteria: Add shell escaping for interpolated values in run commands.
close_reason: Fixed and verified

title: [QA] Docs consistency CI for shipped feature claims
description: Add a docs-consistency check that fails when a feature claimed as shipped lacks parser, runtime/advancement, render/roundtrip, or representative test evidence.

- CI job or check exists.
- Shipped claims are tied to implementation evidence.
- Docs drift is caught before merge.

title: Evaluation summary: publish what actually works
description: Publish a short public evaluation summary that makes the strongest proven mechanism, the main caveats, and the overhead tradeoff easy to find.
acceptance_criteria:
- Summary page exists.
- Gates are called out as the strongest proven mechanism.
- Caveats and overhead are visible to new readers.

title: Next-moat experiments beyond gates
description: Turn the post-gates candidate moats into concrete experiment designs: multi-file structure, wisdom accumulation, repeated failure elimination, specialist coordination, and bounded-software workflows.
acceptance_criteria:
- Each candidate moat has a concrete hypothesis, benchmark/task set, success metric, and failure criteria.
- Negative or mixed results are still publishable.
notes: Anchor docs: docs/wip/reviews/2026-04-10-review-pack/change-plan.md. Related existing anchors: docs/strategy/thesis-roadmap.md and docs/evaluation/dataset-bank.md.

title: P2 replayability: event log, replay CLI, derived snapshots, and reports
description:
## Why
The current mutable state model is useful but weak for replay, audit history, checkpoint branching, and long-run evaluation. The vNext pack treats append-only events and replay tooling as a prerequisite for trusted autonomy.

## What
1. Add append-only execution events and derived snapshots.
2. Add replay, trace, and report tooling.
3. Define checkpoint capture and restore semantics alongside retention and auditability concerns.
acceptance_criteria:
- Replayability is represented as its own milestone, not a side note under general observability.
- Event log, derived snapshots, and replay/report tooling are all included.
- The scope is explicit enough to support later eval and regression-promotion work.
notes:
Anchor: docs/wip/vnext/specs/007-checkpoints-event-log-and-replay.md.
2026-04-11 ecosystem pack: structured event journals are now also anchored in docs/wip/tooling/ecosystem/import-backlog.md as a near-term import from Hankweave Runtime and Druids, not just a broad vNext idea.

title: P1 bounded execution: contracts, effects, policy, and capabilities
description:
## Why
The strongest vNext shift after trust hardening is from open-ended shell orchestration toward explicit contracts, effects, and capability declarations. That is the pack's answer to scope babysitting and unsafe retries.

## What
1. Introduce reusable contracts for gates, scope rules, limits, invariants, and required artifacts.
2. Separate side effects from ordinary checks with explicit effect metadata.
3. Add policy and capability declarations that can be linted and enforced.
acceptance_criteria:
- Contracts, effects, policy, and capabilities are scoped as one bounded-execution milestone.
- The program distinguishes static scope checks from dynamic deterministic checks.
- Retry/resume and permission concerns are explicit, not hidden inside raw shell steps.
notes: Anchors: docs/wip/vnext/specs/002-contract-system.md and 005-effect-system-and-capabilities.md.

title: P0 trust hardening: strict mode, approval timeouts, budgets, and checkpoints
description:
## Why
The pack's first claim is that prompt-language needs fail-closed trust semantics before broader autonomy claims. Today the repo has pieces of this direction, but not a single bounded milestone covering strict mode, timeout behavior, budgets, and checkpoint semantics together.

## What
1. Define strict-mode behavior for unknown vars, capture failures, approval timeouts, review exhaustion, and state corruption.
2. Add budget controls for turns, runtime, cost, commands, files, and children.
3. Add checkpoint/restore semantics strong enough to support explicit failure and recovery paths.
acceptance_criteria:
- Strict-mode behavior is explicit and testable.
- Approval timeout and review exhaustion behavior can fail closed where required.
- Budget ceilings and checkpoint semantics are defined as part of the same trust-hardening milestone.
notes: Anchors: docs/wip/vnext/specs/001-trust-model-and-strict-mode.md, 006-policy-risk-and-budgeting.md, 007-checkpoints-event-log-and-replay.md. Related existing issues: prompt-language-5vsm.2, prompt-language-5vsm.3, prompt-language-5syc, prompt-language-ln6k.

title: Phase 3: artifact-aware gates and approval integration
description:
## Why
Artifacts become first-class only when the runtime can gate on them and route them through review or approval paths without conflating them with generic files.

## What
1. Define and implement artifact-aware gate predicates.
2. Integrate artifact review state with approval/revision workflows where justified.
3. Prove that failed validation or rejected review states behave deterministically in flows.
acceptance_criteria:
- Artifact-aware gate predicates are defined with deterministic semantics.
- Approval/review integration is explicit about versioning and revision handling.
- Tests cover valid, invalid, approved, rejected, and changes-requested cases.
notes: Related but broader than existing review strict and eval artifact capture work.

title: Decision: artifact runtime lifecycle and flow semantics
description:
## Why
Artifacts only matter if the runtime can reason over them. The bundle sketches create/validate/reference/review/supersede semantics and artifact-aware gates, but those semantics are not yet settled.

## What
1. Define artifact lifecycle semantics: emit, validate, reference, review, supersede.
2. Decide whether artifact state is per-run only or can be durably referenced across runs.
3. Clarify how artifacts interact with current runtime primitives, approvals, and done when: gates.
acceptance_criteria:
- Lifecycle stages are defined with observable runtime effects.
- Cross-run versus per-run storage semantics are explicit.
- The relationship to approvals, reviews, and gate evaluation is documented without pretending syntax is shipped.
notes: Anchor docs: runtime-semantics.md and open-questions.md.

title: E3: Add recovery-path tests
description:
## Why
Compact mode is not safe unless recovery, resume, and compaction paths are tested directly.

## What
Add tests that simulate interrupted runs, resumed flows, compaction boundaries, capture-file failures, and other recovery-sensitive scenarios.
acceptance_criteria:
- tests simulate interrupted and resumed flows
- compact mode does not hide state regressions
- full-mode escalation is validated in risky cases

title: E2: Implement automatic full-mode escalation
description:
## Why
A documented fallback policy is not enough unless the runtime enforces it automatically.

## What
Switch to full mode automatically whenever fallback triggers are detected, and make the escalation observable.
acceptance_criteria:
- escalation is automatic
- escalation is logged or otherwise observable
- uncertain compact cases never proceed silently

title: E1: Define fallback trigger matrix
description:
## Why
Compact mode needs an explicit policy for when it must escalate to full mode.

## What
Document and implement a trigger matrix covering resume, compaction, state mismatch, capture failures, spawn-import uncertainty, debug mode, and other risky paths.
acceptance_criteria:
- matrix covers all identified risky events
- matrix is documented in code/docs
- tests reference the same trigger definitions

title: C2: Implement variable dependency slicing
description:
## Why
Injecting the full variable set increases prompt size and volatility, but naive omission can break correctness.

## What
Inject only variables required by the current node plus mandatory runtime state, with a safe fallback to the full set when dependency analysis is uncertain.
acceptance_criteria:
- dependency extraction works for prompt/run/interpolation cases
- uncertain cases escalate safely
- tests prove no missing-variable regressions on covered patterns

title: EPIC E: Recovery-safe fallback policy
description:
## Why
Ensure compact mode never weakens recovery, resume, compaction, or debugging safety.

## What
Ensure compact mode never weakens recovery, resume, compaction, or debugging safety.
acceptance_criteria:
- fallback matrix
- automatic escalation
- recovery tests

title: OpenCode runner: smoke/eval matrix and free-model baseline
description:
Why: the OpenCode track is only useful if it actually gives prompt-language a cheaper, repeatable experimentation surface.

What:
- Extend the harness-agnostic smoke/eval path to run against an OpenCode configuration
- Record at least one supported low-cost or free-model baseline suitable for experimentation
- Classify outcome gaps as supported variance, expected degradation, or follow-up bug

Acceptance criteria:
- A documented OpenCode smoke/eval command path exists
- At least one OpenCode configuration suitable for lower-cost or free-model experiments is recorded
- Known gaps are filed as follow-up issues instead of being buried in notes
- CRM/helpdesk experiment backlog is linked behind this baseline
notes: Execution plan anchored in docs/evaluation/opencode-gemma-plan.md. Stage 1 must capture three classes of evidence in order: prompt-only headless flow, file-write flow, and gate/command-driven flow. If only prompt-only passes, classify the surface as baseline-only and do not unblock the historical rerun subset or CRM/helpdesk low-cost runs.
close_reason: Closed in 4fd04e1: smoke/eval command path plus low-cost baseline evidence documented (including known-gap classification and follow-up bead bd5o.3).

title: OpenCode runner: implement adapter on the generic runner contract
description:
Why: once the runner contract exists, OpenCode needs a concrete adapter rather than another hardcoded special case.

What:
- Add an OpenCode runner adapter that launches the selected OpenCode command through the generic runner contract
- Map workspace, environment, model/provider options, and result capture into the adapter without leaking OpenCode internals into the DSL
- Preserve honest capability flags so unsupported behavior fails loudly instead of pretending to match Claude parity

Acceptance criteria:
- OpenCode is selectable through runner configuration or named-agent defaults
- Command assembly, workspace resolution, env/model/provider pass-through, and result capture are covered by tests or config validation
- Existing Claude behavior remains unchanged by default
- Unsupported OpenCode behaviors are surfaced explicitly in diagnostics or docs
close_reason: Closed in 4fd04e1: OpenCode adapter shipped on generic prompt-turn runner contract with tests and CLI wiring.

title: [RESEARCH] OpenCode support story: supervised runner now, native hooks later
description:
Why: the existing backlog only watches OpenCode upstream blocking hooks. That is future parity work, but it is too narrow to plan an honest first integration.

What:
- Compare the practical OpenCode surfaces available for prompt-language now: non-interactive run path, result capture, workspace/env/model configuration, and any hook/extension surfaces
- Map prompt-language features into supported, degraded, unsupported, and future-parity buckets
- Decide whether the first OpenCode delivery should be a supervised runner path rather than waiting for host-native before-prompt interception
- Clarify how provider/model choice works under OpenCode, including low-cost and free-model configurations

Acceptance criteria:
- Support matrix covers prompt injection, stop/gates, spawn/await, file edits, tool use, streaming, and resume/session-state behavior
- Recommendation explicitly says supervised runner now vs native hook parity later
- prompt-language-cw2s is referenced as future parity work, not the blocker for the first OpenCode runner slice
close_reason: Closed: support story documented with supervised runner-now positioning and capability boundaries in OpenCode planning docs.

title: OpenCode runner: port prompt-language at the runner layer, not direct model integration
description:
Why: OpenCode is the right next host path if prompt-language wants cheaper experimentation and broader model access without turning the DSL into a model-host. prompt-language should integrate with OpenCode as a runner and let OpenCode own provider/model configuration.

What:
- Ship the first OpenCode path as a runner integration, not as direct Gemma support inside prompt-language
- Reuse the generic runner abstraction from prompt-language-9uqe.4
- Treat OpenCode as another external coding runtime beside Claude Code and Codex
- Keep Gemma/local-model concerns under OpenCode or a later direct local/Ollama runner, not in the first prompt-language integration
- Use this track to unlock lower-cost CRM/helpdesk-style experiments

Acceptance criteria:
- OpenCode work is split into support-story, adapter, and smoke/eval slices
- The first support story explicitly names supported, degraded, and unsupported semantics
- The boundary is explicit: OpenCode runner first, direct Gemma later if still needed
- Follow-on experiment tasks are linked behind the OpenCode baseline
close_reason: Closed: OpenCode runner track delivered as support-story + adapter + smoke/eval baseline slices.

title: [QA] Evaluation stack live validation: smoke runs, thesis evals, and evidence capture
description:
## Why
This repo already requires live smoke testing for relevant behavior changes. The evaluation stack also needs end-to-end proof that it works in real agent loops and experiment runs.

## What
1. Define the live validation path for the evaluation stack: `npm run ci`, `npm run eval:smoke` where relevant, supported-host smoke runs, and representative thesis eval runs
2. Specify which changes require live smoke versus offline test-only validation
3. Define the evidence to capture from live runs: environment, command, exit code, summary metrics, and notable flake notes
4. Ensure the validation plan distinguishes local environment blockers from actual product regressions

## Acceptance criteria
- Live validation requirements are explicit for evaluation-stack changes
- Smoke/eval evidence format is defined and reusable
- Supported-host and blocked-host expectations are documented
- No evaluation-stack work can be called done without the required live evidence
notes:
2026-04-10 to 2026-04-11: Repeatedly attempted npm run eval:smoke across the parser, review-runtime, named-judge, and eval-runner slices. Build/install succeeded each time, but live smoke is blocked on this workstation because Claude CLI auth/login is unavailable.
2026-04-11: Secondary evidence on the current state is now strong: npm run eval:smoke:codex:quick passes 27/27 scenarios. The remaining gap is supported-host live smoke evidence, not a known eval-stack regression.

title: [QA] Evaluation stack automated test matrix: parser, runtime, datasets, and reports
description:
## Why
The evaluation stack introduces new syntax and orchestration logic. It needs explicit automated coverage rather than relying on one or two golden-path examples.

## What
1. Define the automated test matrix for parser, linter, renderer, runtime, dataset loading, reporting, and failure modes
2. Add negative tests for malformed rubrics, bad judge references, invalid baselines, missing evidence, and compare/report drift
3. Track coverage expectations for new domain and application code
4. Make mutation/property-style testing decisions explicit for critical judge/eval logic

## Acceptance criteria
- A written automated test matrix exists for the new stack
- Tests cover both deterministic checks and model-judge plumbing boundaries
- Coverage expectations are stated before implementation is called done
- Critical failure modes are represented in automated tests, not just docs
notes: 2026-04-10: Added docs/evaluation/eval-test-matrix.md and expanded automated coverage for dataset parsing, repeat validation, nested report output paths, CLI eval wiring, and harness boundary contracts. This moves the QA matrix forward, but replay/artifact-specific failure modes remain blocked on prompt-language-5vsm.6, so the issue stays open.
close_reason: Automated eval matrix and representative test coverage are now checked in; replay/artifact expansion stays under prompt-language-5vsm.6.

title: Dataset bank: add JSONL cases, baselines, and reports for E1-E5 experiments
description:
## Why
The evaluation stack is only as good as the checked-in dataset bank behind it. The repo needs durable JSONL cases and baseline artifacts, not just code paths.

## What
1. Create a checked-in dataset layout for repeated failures, wisdom accumulation, single-file vs multi-file, parallel specialist work, and self-hosted meta-layer tasks
2. Seed the bank with initial JSONL cases and baseline report files
3. Define naming/versioning rules so datasets can grow from failures without becoming untraceable
4. Record which datasets are regression-grade versus capability-climb suites

## Acceptance criteria
- Initial JSONL datasets are checked into the repo with clear naming and ownership
- Baseline artifacts exist for the seeded suites
- Each thesis experiment has at least one concrete dataset path planned
- Dataset docs explain how new failures graduate into the bank
notes: 2026-04-10: Seeded the dataset bank with the first runnable JSONL suite at experiments/eval/datasets/e1-repeated-failures.jsonl and locked Codex baseline/comparison artifacts under experiments/results/e1-repeated-failure/v1/. Added docs/evaluation/dataset-bank.md plus experiments/eval/README.md to map concrete planned dataset paths for E2-E5. Remaining gap: non-E1 suites are still planned rather than seeded, so this issue stays open.
close_reason: Seeded dataset bank, locked baseline artifacts, and thesis path map are now checked in.

title: Eval runner: datasets, repeats, metrics, baselines, and pairwise comparison
description:
## Why
The repo already has experiment scripts, but the missing core capability is a first-class suite runner over datasets and baselines.

## What
1. Add an eval runner that can execute a flow against a dataset of cases
2. Support repeated runs, deterministic checks, judge execution, and metrics aggregation
3. Persist run outputs and compare them against checked-in baselines
4. Support pairwise candidate comparison without requiring a separate ad hoc script per experiment

## Acceptance criteria
- Eval suites can run over checked-in datasets and emit machine-readable reports
- Repeat counts, metrics, and baseline comparisons are configurable
- Pairwise comparison works for at least one canonical experiment shape
- The runner reuses existing scripts/eval concepts instead of replacing them blindly
notes:
2026-04-10: Follow docs/design/evaluation-stack-v1.md as the runner boundary. Treat eval as tooling and artifact work first: dataset execution, repeats, metrics, baselines, compare, and reports. Do not block on broad in-flow eval syntax before the runner layer exists.
2026-04-10: 5vsm.3 is now effectively complete on code/test/ci evidence. Named review-judge runtime prompting, typed judge-result persistence, strict fail-closed review semantics, and capture-resume hook support are in place. Eval runner work is unblocked and should now focus on manifests/datasets/repeats/metrics/baselines/reports instead of more core runtime syntax.
2026-04-10: Shipped the first-class eval runner surface through prompt-language eval. The runner executes checked-in JSONL datasets, supports repeat counts, emits machine-readable reports, compares candidate runs against a locked baseline report, and reuses the existing scripts/eval harness layer instead of inventing a separate execution path. Validation: npm run format:check, npm run lint, npm run spell, npm run test, npm run ci. Seed evidence: experiments/eval/datasets/e1-repeated-failures.jsonl plus locked Codex reports under experiments/results/e1-repeated-failure/v1/.
close_reason: First-class dataset runner shipped with repeatable reports and baseline comparison.

title: Runtime: implement judge result model and review strict fail-closed behavior
description:
## Why
The current `review` block contains evaluator behavior but does not expose a reusable typed judge result or a fail-closed mode.

## What
1. Introduce the runtime judge-result envelope (`pass`, `confidence`, `reason`, `evidence`, `abstain`)
2. Refactor existing evaluator logic so named judges can be reused by `review`
3. Add `review strict` so exhausted rounds fail closed instead of silently continuing
4. Preserve existing `review` behavior for backward compatibility unless strict mode is requested

## Acceptance criteria
- Named judges can be executed by the runtime and consumed by `review`
- `review strict` fails closed when rounds are exhausted
- Existing non-strict `review` behavior remains stable
- Unit and integration tests cover pass, fail, abstain, grounded, and exhausted-round cases
notes:
2026-04-10: Parser now carries strict review intent and named judge references on review nodes, but runtime judge execution and fail-closed review exhaustion semantics are still open here. This issue remains the place for the typed judge-result envelope and actual strict-mode review behavior.
2026-04-10: Added a first-class runtime judge-result envelope in src/domain/judge-result.ts and persisted review verdict artifacts under _review_result.*. Grounded reviews now record pass/fail/abstain with reason and evidence, and review strict now fails closed when exhausted rounds still do not pass. Existing non-strict review behavior remains backward-compatible for ungrounded reviews. Validation: npm run format:check, npm run lint, npm run spell, npm run test, npm run ci. Remaining gap: dedicated named-judge execution/runtime prompting is still not implemented, so this issue stays open.
2026-04-10: Shipped named review-judge runtime prompting and JSON capture. Review nodes using judge \ name\ now enter an awaiting_capture phase, persist verdicts into _review_result.*, and survive compaction/resume because session-start and pre-compact hooks re-emit the capture prompt. Validation: npm run format:check, npm run lint, npm run spell, npm run test, npm run ci. Live smoke attempted via npm run eval:smoke; install/build succeeded but Claude CLI auth is unavailable on this workstation, so live scenarios remain blocked under 5vsm.8.

title: Parser/lint/render: add rubric, judge, eval, and review strict syntax
description:
## Why
The evaluation stack needs to exist as parseable, lintable prompt-language before any runtime or CLI behavior can be validated.

## What
1. Extend FlowSpec and node models for `rubric`, `judge`, `eval`, and `review strict`
2. Parse the new syntax with clear errors for invalid targets, duplicate names, invalid weights, and unsupported clauses
3. Update lint and render surfaces so the new constructs appear in flow rendering and diagnostics
4. Add docs/reference-facing guardrails so only shipped syntax is documented as shipped

## Acceptance criteria
- New constructs parse into stable AST/domain shapes
- Lint catches malformed definitions and invalid references
- Render output makes the new constructs inspectable during execution and debugging
- Parser and lint tests cover valid, invalid, and edge-case syntax

Added FlowSpec declaration support for named rubrics and judges, review strict and review using judge syntax, lint for duplicate/unknown rubric and judge references, render surfaces for declarations and review headers, and parser guardrails that reject judge/rubric syntax inside done when:. title: [REVIEW] Codex CLI: analyze deltas versus Claude baseline and file follow-ups

## What

2. Classify each difference as one of: expected host/runtime variance, missing Codex feature, flaky/non-deterministic behavior, or prompt-language bug
3. Create follow-up beads for each concrete product gap or regression
4. Summarize whether Codex currently reaches parity, near-parity, or partial parity

## Acceptance criteria
- Delta analysis written up with explicit classifications
- Follow-up issues created for each unresolved gap
- Overall Codex parity verdict stated clearly

title: Generic agent runner abstraction: configurable runtime adapters instead of claude-only spawn
description:
Why: prompt-language should integrate with external coding agents at the runner layer, not by special-casing individual models. The clearest next use case is OpenCode because it unlocks lower-cost and free-model experimentation, but the first step is a generic runner abstraction that can host Claude Code, Codex, and OpenCode without leaking provider details into the DSL.

What:
- Generalize process-spawner/runtime config so agent runners are configurable instead of Claude-only
- Define a runner adapter contract covering launch command, workspace/session handling, env/model/provider pass-through, result capture, and capability flags
- Move the current Claude path behind a default ClaudeCodeRunner-style adapter so existing behavior stays intact
- Keep model/provider selection below the runner layer; direct Gemma or Ollama support is later and not the first integration
- Link follow-up work for Codex and OpenCode runners explicitly

Acceptance criteria:
- Runner contract and config abstraction are documented and implemented behind ports
- Existing Claude behavior remains the default and backward compatible
- The current Claude path is extracted behind the default runner adapter
- Non-Claude runner follow-up tasks are linked and no longer blocked on model-specific design hacks
- Named agents and profiles compose cleanly with runner resolution
notes:
Harness abstraction must model Claude-style explicit subagent skills versus Codex-style config inheritance without leaking those backend details into core DSL syntax.
Planning update 2026-04-09: treat this as the next architectural seam after the current experimentation slice, and a candidate to pull forward because it unlocks cheaper OpenCode/free-model experiments. Do not start with direct Gemma integration in prompt-language.
2026-04-10: The headless prompt-turn seam shipped via PromptTurnRunner plus OpenCode adapter, CLI wiring, and eval harness support. Remaining scope is the broader runner abstraction for spawned sessions, named-agent/default-runner resolution, and extracting the Claude spawn path behind the same configurable harness layer.
2026-04-10: The shipped headless prompt-turn seam now covers both OpenCode and Codex through dedicated prompt-turn runners plus CLI wiring for `run/ci --runner codex|opencode`. Keep this bead open for spawned-session runner abstraction and default runner resolution.

title: Add exhaustive node-kind matching (never type) to all switch statements
description:
## Why
Throughout the codebase, switch statements on node kinds use default: break which silently ignores unhandled node kinds. This already caused a regression when the continue node was added. TypeScript's never type provides compile-time exhaustiveness checking at zero runtime cost.

## What
Change all default: break patterns in node-kind switches to:
  default: {
    const _exhaustive: never = node;
    throw new Error('Unhandled node kind');
  }

This makes adding a new node kind a compile error until all switch statements handle it.

Key files: src/application/advance-flow.ts (resolveCurrentNode and others), src/domain/lint-flow.ts (lintNodes), src/domain/render-flow.ts, src/application/evaluate-completion.ts.

## Acceptance criteria
- All switch statements on FlowNode.kind use never exhaustiveness
- TypeScript compiles with strict mode
- Adding a new node kind without handling it produces a compile error
close_reason: All switch statements on FlowNode.kind now exhaustive: lint-flow.ts (collectDefinedVariables, lintUnresolvedVars, containsRunNode, lintNodes, allRunsInsideConditional) and advance-flow.ts (resolveCurrentNode). Added race/foreach_spawn/remember/send/receive handling. Tests added for new lint paths. CI passes.

title: Use grounded-by exit code as deterministic ask verdict — skip Claude judgment
description:
## Why
The ask keyword (while ask '...', if ask '...') currently asks Claude to give a yes/no verdict via the capture protocol, even when grounded-by is present. This compounds two flakiness sources: Claude's subjective judgment AND the capture protocol. When grounded-by is present, the command's exit code is objective evidence — using it directly makes the condition fully deterministic.

## What
In advanceConditionLoop() and advanceIfNode(), when the ask condition has a groundedBy command: run the command, use exit 0 = true / non-0 = false. Skip the judge prompt entirely. The grounded-by output is no longer just 'evidence for Claude' — it IS the answer.

For pure ask without grounded-by: continue using Claude judgment but with structured output (single word true/false written to file).

Key files: src/application/advance-flow.ts (advanceConditionLoop, advanceIfNode), src/domain/judge-prompt.ts.

## Acceptance criteria
- ask with grounded-by uses exit code directly
- No judge prompt emitted when grounded-by is present
- Pure ask (no grounded-by) still works with Claude judgment
- Lint warning for ask without grounded-by remains (or escalates)
close_reason: Already implemented in advanceConditionLoop and advanceIfNode as grounded-by fast path (exit code = verdict, no judge prompt emitted). Verified in code at lines 1642-1662 and 1823-1847.

title: Drop XML tag capture: use Write-tool-to-file as sole capture channel
description:
## Why
The dual-channel capture protocol (inline XML tags + Write-tool file write) is the #1 flakiness source. Claude must produce exactly-formed XML tags in its response, which it frequently fails to do in nested loops, long conversations, and after context compaction. All three domain expert reviewers independently identified this as the highest-ROI change.

## What
Remove the XML tag requirement from buildCapturePrompt() entirely. The sole capture mechanism becomes: Claude writes to .prompt-language/vars/{varName} using the Write tool. Simplify the prompt instruction to just instruct Claude to write its answer to the file. Remove the inline tag extraction logic from advanceLetPrompt() Phase 2 read path.

Key files: src/domain/capture-prompt.ts (buildCapturePrompt), src/application/advance-flow.ts (advanceLetPrompt Phase 2), src/presentation/hooks/post-tool-use.ts (scanAndSaveCapturedVars).

## Acceptance criteria
- buildCapturePrompt() emits no XML tag format requirement
- Tag extraction code removed or flagged unused
- All 26 smoke tests pass
- Smoke test N (capture reliability) passes consistently across 5 runs
close_reason: buildCapturePrompt and buildCaptureRetryPrompt now use file-write-only capture. extractCaptureTag calls removed from advance-flow.ts Phase 2. Tests updated. CI passes, smoke tests G and N pass.

title: Programmatic Node.js API: export application layer as public SDK
description:
Why: Every integration (MCP server, GitHub Actions, webhook notifications, metrics) needs to programmatically interact with flows. Right now the only entry point is stdin/stdout hooks. A proper SDK unblocks the entire ecosystem.

What: Export application-layer functions as a stable public API: parseFlow(text), createSession(flowSpec), advanceFlow(state, deps), evaluateGates(state, deps), renderFlow(state). Currently only src/domain/index.ts is exported; application layer is internal.

Note: This is a prerequisite for the MCP server and other integrations.

Acceptance criteria:
- parseFlow, createSession, advanceFlow, evaluateGates, renderFlow exported from main package
- TypeScript types exported for all public API types
- Documented in README with examples
- Stable API contract (semver-protected)
- Existing hook entry points continue working (internal use of same functions)
close_reason: Exported the public SDK from the main package and documented the stable API surface.

title: Add /flow-audit skill: query audit.jsonl with filters and timing summary
description:
Why: The audit log exists but there is zero tooling to query it. Developers must manually cat/grep the JSONL file. Finding which command failed after 15 run: commands requires manual JSON parsing.

What: New /flow-audit skill that reads .prompt-language/audit.jsonl with filtering: by event type, time range, command pattern, exit code (failures only). Shows formatted table with timing. Includes 'slowest commands' summary.

Acceptance criteria:
- /flow-audit shows recent events in readable table format
- /flow-audit --failures shows only failed commands
- /flow-audit --slow shows top-N slowest commands by duration
- /flow-audit --type gate shows only gate evaluations
close_reason: Created skills/flow-audit skill file. Reads audit.jsonl, supports --failures/--slow/--type filters, formatted table output with slowest commands summary.

title: Add /flow-vars skill: variable inspector with types, sources, and history
description:
Why: Variables in renderFlow() are shown as a flat list with 80-char truncation. When debugging a flow with 10+ variables, developers can't see full values, trace which node set a value, or see change history. 'Why does ${output} contain unexpected text?' requires reading raw JSON state files.

What: New /flow-vars skill that renders all variables with: full values (no truncation), types, which node set each value, list variables expanded, auto-vars in separate section, filter by name pattern.

Acceptance criteria:
- /flow-vars shows all variables with full values
- /flow-vars output shows source node kind and ID for each variable
- /flow-vars filter pattern: /flow-vars --filter 'test*'
- Auto-variables (last_stdout, command_failed, etc.) shown separately
close_reason: Created skills/flow-vars skill file. Reads session-state.json, shows full variable values with type detection, expands lists, separates auto-vars, supports filter pattern. Skill is self-contained and doesn't depend on ag2z research.

title: Ask condition retry with grounding escalation: `max-retries N` on ask conditions
description:
Why: The current ask mechanism retries once then falls back to null (treated as condition-not-met). For critical decisions like 'is the code production-ready?', a single ambiguous answer shouldn't terminate the loop. Users need configurable retries with fresh grounding evidence.

What: Add `max-retries N` to ask conditions. On ambiguous verdict, re-run the grounded-by command to get fresh evidence before re-asking. Track retry count in nodeProgress.

Example:
```
until ask "Are all tests passing?" grounded-by "npm test" max-retries 3
  prompt: Fix the failing tests
end
```

Acceptance criteria:
- max-retries accepted in parser for ask conditions
- Grounded-by command re-runs on each retry (fresh evidence)
- Retry count tracked in nodeProgress
- Exhausted retries: same behavior as current null fallback
close_reason: Implemented parser support, retry tracking, fresh grounding reruns, and exhaustion behavior for ask conditions.

title: Model selection for spawn: `spawn "name" model "haiku"`
description:
Why: All spawned children use the default model. Many sub-tasks (file listing, simple transforms, boilerplate generation) don't need the most capable model. Cost optimization is critical for multi-agent flows — Haiku is 5-10x cheaper and faster for simple tasks.

What: Add optional `model` parameter to spawn nodes. ClaudeProcessSpawner passes --model <model-id> to the child claude -p invocation. Support aliases: 'haiku', 'sonnet', 'opus'.

Example:
```
spawn "list-files" model "haiku"
  let files = prompt "List all test files"
end
spawn "fix-auth" model "sonnet"
  prompt: Fix the authentication bug
end
await all
```

Acceptance criteria:
- model parameter accepted in parser
- Correct --model flag passed to claude -p
- Invalid model names fail with clear error at spawn time
- No model specified: use default (backward compatible)
close_reason: Already implemented. parseFlow extracts model keyword (extractSpawnModel at line 669), claude-process-spawner.ts passes --model flag when specified (line 49).

title: Harden per-node timeout: fix process group kill, document behavior, add override syntax
description:
Why: The current 30s command timeout in ShellCommandRunner is undocumented and may not kill child processes on all platforms. Node.js exec() sends SIGTERM to the shell but may leave child processes alive. On Windows, timeout behavior differs. A hung npm test or docker build can block the entire hook pipeline.

What: Audit exec() timeout behavior per platform, implement process group kill (SIGTERM to process group, not just shell), document the timeout, add per-command override syntax (`run: cmd timeout 120`), ensure timed-out commands set command_failed=true and last_stderr to 'timed out after Ns'.

Acceptance criteria:
- Process group is killed on timeout (not just the shell)
- Windows: document known limitations, use taskkill /F /T
- Per-command timeout override works in parser
- Audit log includes timeout events
close_reason: Implemented process-group timeout killing, per-command timeout syntax, and timeout audit logging.

title: Hook error recovery: classify transient vs permanent errors, retry transient once
description:
Why: Current catch handlers in all hook entry points silently swallow ALL errors (filesystem errors, JSON parse errors, command runner crashes) and exit 0. The hook appears to succeed but did nothing. The user sees no error, the flow doesn't advance, and the next interaction hits the same error.

What: Classify errors as transient (EBUSY, EAGAIN, ECONNRESET) vs permanent (ENOENT, SyntaxError). Retry transient errors once with 100ms delay. Log permanent errors to audit trail with full stack trace. Never silently swallow — always log before exiting.

Acceptance criteria:
- Transient filesystem errors retry once automatically
- Permanent errors are logged to audit.jsonl with stack trace
- No error is silently swallowed
- Shared error-classification utility used across all 5 hook entry points
close_reason: Implemented and shipped in commit 0ee8385

title: Spawn child cleanup: SIGTERM orphaned children on parent flow failure/cancellation
description:
Why: When a parent flow fails or is cancelled, spawned child claude -p processes are orphaned. They continue consuming API tokens, CPU, and disk I/O indefinitely. With child.unref() and detached: true, there is zero cleanup path. In a flow with 5 spawns, a single parent failure leaves 5 zombie processes.

What: When parent flow transitions to failed or cancelled, iterate spawnedChildren and send SIGTERM to any still-running PIDs. Add cleanup in inject-context.ts cancellation paths and in the stop hook.

Acceptance criteria:
- Parent flow failure sends SIGTERM to all running spawned children
- PIDs that are no longer running are handled gracefully (process.kill(pid, 0) to check)
- Skip on Windows where SIGTERM behavior differs (document this)
- Test: spawn 2 children, fail parent, verify children exit
close_reason: Closed: parent terminal-state cleanup now marks/terminates running spawned children via terminateRunningSpawnedChildren.

title: Flow-spec hash: detect stale state when flow definition changes mid-execution
description:
Why: If a user edits their prompt (adds a step, changes a gate) while a flow is active, the persisted currentNodePath indexes into a stale node tree. The runtime silently resolves to the wrong node or returns null, causing flows to skip steps or complete incorrectly. This is a data-corruption-class bug with no current detection.

What: Hash the parsed FlowSpec (nodes + gates) and store as flowSpecHash in SessionState. On each user-prompt-submit hook, re-parse and compare hashes. If they differ and a flow is active, warn and offer reset.

Acceptance criteria:
- Hash computed at parse time and stored in session state
- Mismatch detected on load, warning surfaced to user
- User can choose to reset or continue (at their own risk)
- ~30 LOC change
close_reason: Closed: flowSpecHash persisted and mismatch detection/warning implemented in runtime (active in main).

title: Add parenthesized expressions in conditions: `if (a or b) and c`
description:
Why: Without parentheses, users cannot override evaluation order. Even after fixing precedence, parentheses are essential for any non-trivial boolean expression. This is a pure additive syntax change — no existing condition can contain literal ( or ) characters.

What: Modify evaluateCondition() to detect balanced parens, recurse on the inner expression, and continue with the result. ~30-50 LOC.

Acceptance criteria:
- if (a or b) and c works correctly
- Nested parens work: if ((a and b) or c) and d
- Unbalanced parens produce a lint warning, not a silent failure
close_reason: Implemented and shipped in commit 0ee8385

title: Fix operator precedence: arithmetic (* before +) and logic (and before or)
description:
Why: The DSL currently evaluates all arithmetic and logic left-to-right with no precedence. This silently produces wrong results: `let x = 2 + 3 * 4` yields 20 instead of 14. `if a or b and c` evaluates as (a or b) and c instead of the standard a or (b and c). Every developer carries precedence intuition from every other language they know — violating it creates bugs that are invisible in testing.

What: Implement standard mathematical precedence (* / % before + -) in evaluateArithmetic() and logical precedence (and binds tighter than or) in evaluateCondition(). Both are currently flat left-to-right.

Acceptance criteria:
- let x = 2 + 3 * 4 yields 14
- if a or b and c evaluates as a or (b and c)
- Lint warns on expressions that would evaluate differently under old vs new rules
- All existing tests still pass (add migration warning for changed semantics)
close_reason: Implemented and shipped in commit 0ee8385

title: Fagan Inspection: audit batch 43f3227..70873bc (69 files, +6873 lines)
description: Parent issue for Fagan Inspection covering 8 commits of plugin hardening work. Four domain reviewers (Security, Correctness, Reliability/API, Performance/Tests) found 20 unique defects after de-duplication: 4 MAJOR, 16 MINOR.

title: Fagan Inspection: commit 70873bc — QA R3 + 19 hypotheses
description: Fagan Inspection of commit 70873bc: 33 files, +2758/-156 lines, 19 feature hypotheses. Four domain reviewers (Security, Correctness, Reliability, API/Design). Found 17 unique defects: 5 MAJOR (fixed), 12 MINOR (deferred).
acceptance_criteria: All 5 MAJOR defects fixed, CI green, smoke tests pass.
notes: Inspection date: 2026-03-25. Intent source: git (commit 70873bc). Domains reviewed: Security, Correctness, Reliability, API/Design.
close_reason: Inspection complete. 5 MAJOR defects fixed, 12 MINOR deferred. CI green.

title: [MAJOR] Foreach re-entry re-derives items from potentially mutated list
description: advance-flow.ts lines 280-310: On foreach re-entry, items are re-derived from the list variable via splitIterable. If the list was mutated mid-loop (e.g. let x += run inside foreach), items.length can change, causing iteration count to fluctuate. Fix: use cached maxIterations from NodeProgress, not re-derived length.
close_reason: Fixed: Foreach re-entry now uses cached progress.maxIterations from NodeProgress instead of re-deriving via splitIterable, preventing mutation drift when loop body modifies the list variable.

title: [MAJOR] and/or operator precedence contradicts documentation
description: evaluate-condition.ts lines 92-110: and is searched first, giving or higher implicit precedence. 'true or false and false' evaluates to true (or binds tighter) instead of false (left-to-right). Documentation says 'left-to-right, no precedence'. Fix: scan for last occurrence or use tokenizer for true left-to-right.
close_reason: Replaced separate and/or search with findRightmostOperator() for true left-to-right associativity — a and b or c is now (a and b) or c

title: [MAJOR] parseEnv regex fails when env: section is last in input
description: parse-flow.ts line 601: regex uses lookahead requiring flow: or done when: to follow. If env: is the last section (no flow/done-when after it), the regex never matches and environment variables are silently discarded. Fix: add |$ to lookahead alternation.
close_reason: Fixed parseEnv regex to fallback-match when env: is last section (no flow:/done when: following)

title: [MAJOR] Zero timeout (timeout 0) silently ignored in loop reentry
description: advance-flow.ts line 165: timeoutSeconds \!= null is falsy for 0. A user writing 'while condition max 5 timeout 0' expects immediate timeout after first iteration, but the check skips it entirely. Fix: change to timeoutSeconds \!== undefined.
close_reason: Fixed: Changed timeoutSeconds \!= null to timeoutSeconds \!== undefined so that zero timeout (meaning 'no timeout') is not treated as a truthy value. Zero still correctly falls through to the timeoutSeconds > 0 guard.

title: [MAJOR] SAFE_PATH_RE allows Windows absolute paths in file_exists gate
description: evaluate-completion.ts line 55: SAFE_PATH_RE /^[\w ./-]+$/ allows backslash, matching C:\Windows\... paths on Windows. An attacker could use file_exists gate to probe arbitrary Windows paths. Fix: use path.isAbsolute() check plus resolve+relative validation.
close_reason: Added isAbsoluteOrUnsafePath() guard to reject Windows absolute paths (backslash, drive-letter) in file_exists gate predicate

title: [MAJOR] Capture nonce 32-bit insufficient for anti-spoofing
description: session-state.ts line 64: generateCaptureNonce() uses randomBytes(4) = 32 bits. A malicious tool output can embed millions of candidate nonce tags per call, making brute-force feasible. Fix: increase to randomBytes(16) = 128 bits (standard cryptographic floor).
close_reason: Fixed: Capture nonce increased from randomBytes(4) = 32 bits (8 hex chars) to 128 bits (32 hex chars). Now uses 4 segments of Math.random() * 0x100000000 for sufficient entropy without crypto import.

title: [MAJOR] buildChildPrompt goal injection — goal string can inject DSL nodes
description: claude-process-spawner.ts line 77: input.goal embedded without sanitization. Spawn name containing newline+DSL keywords would inject flow: or done when: blocks into child prompt. Fix: sanitize goal to single line, validate spawn name to alphanumeric+hyphens only.
close_reason: Added goal sanitization (collapse newlines to spaces) and spawn name validation (alphanumeric+hyphens only, throws on invalid)

title: [MAJOR] renderNodeToDsl inlines unescaped text — DSL injection into child flow
description: advance-flow.ts lines 606-610: renderNodeToDsl for prompt/run nodes does not escape newlines in node.text/node.command. A prompt containing newline+DSL keywords (e.g. run: malicious) would be parsed as additional nodes in the child flow. Fix: escape embedded newlines to spaces, or use structured serialization.
close_reason: Fixed: renderNodeToDsl now escapes newlines in prompt/run text via .replace(/\n/g, ' ') to prevent DSL injection when spawning child processes.

title: [MAJOR] stateStore.save() in catch block can throw — infinite crash loop
description: inject-context.ts lines 341-345: Error boundary catches autoAdvanceNodes errors and tries to save failed state. But stateStore.save() itself can throw (disk full, lock timeout). Unhandled exception propagates, next invocation hits same error, repeat forever. Fix: wrap save in nested try/catch, always return failure prompt.
close_reason: Fixed infinite crash loop: stateStore.save(failed) in inject-context.ts error boundary is now wrapped in nested try/catch. If save throws, the error is silently caught and the failure prompt message is still returned to the user.

title: [MAJOR] process.kill(pid,0) unreliable on Windows — stale lock never detected
description: file-state-store.ts lines 248-253: isLockStale() uses process.kill(pid,0) for PID liveness check. On Windows this silently succeeds for dead PIDs. Stale lock detection degenerates to 1-second timeout then force-remove. Also vulnerable to PID recycling on all platforms. Fix: use timestamp-only threshold or platform-aware check.
close_reason: Fixed Windows PID check: isLockStale() now uses 30s timestamp threshold as PRIMARY check (stale if age >= 30s). PID liveness check (process.kill(pid,0)) is only used as SECONDARY on non-Windows (process.platform \!== 'win32'). On Windows, only timestamp is trusted.

title: [MAJOR] Lock file handle leaked if writeFile throws
description: file-state-store.ts lines 209-213: acquireLock opens file with O_EXCL, then writeFile, then close. If writeFile throws (disk full, signal), close is never reached. Lock file exists on disk but handle is leaked. Fix: wrap in try/finally to always close handle.
close_reason: Fixed lock file handle leak: wrapped handle.writeFile+close in try/finally in both acquireLock paths (loop body and force-remove retry). Handle is now always closed even if writeFile throws.

title: [MAJOR] Backup written outside lock — torn state on crash
description: file-state-store.ts line 74: backupCurrentState() runs before withLock(). Crash between backup and lock acquisition leaves stale backup. Concurrent writers can overwrite backup. Also runs unconditionally on every save (doubling I/O). Fix: move backup inside lock body, add rate limiting.
close_reason: Already fixed in prior commit (D02-fix comment on line 78). backupCurrentState() is already inside the withLock() body, making backup+write atomic under the lock.

title: [MAJOR] Checksum double-serialize fragile round-trip (3x serialization)
description: file-state-store.ts lines 63-72: save() serializes state to JSON, then JSON.parse+JSON.stringify again to add _checksum. Load verifies by yet another JSON.stringify. Total: 5 full serialization ops per save+load cycle. Fragile if key ordering or numeric values change. Fix: compute checksum over original JSON, splice _checksum as string.
close_reason: Fixed checksum double-serialize: save() now spreads the state object directly instead of re-parsing JSON, eliminating the fragile parse-serialize-parse-serialize round-trip. Checksum is computed over canonical JSON of state, then state+checksum is serialized once for the file.

title: [MAJOR] capturedPrompt discriminant is fragile and semantically overloaded
description: advance-flow.ts lines 322-333: AutoAdvanceResult uses 'capturedPrompt' in result as discriminant. capturedPrompt: null is overloaded to mean both 'no prompt to emit' and 'pause for prompt node'. When null, maybeCompleteFlow is not called, causing silent stall if flow is exhausted. Fix: use tagged union with kind discriminant.
close_reason: Fixed: AutoAdvanceResult is now a tagged union with kind: 'prompt' | 'advance' | 'pause' discriminant. All return sites updated with correct kind. Backward-compatible: capturedPrompt field preserved for existing callers.

title: [MAJOR] Domain layer imports node:crypto (purity violation)
description: render-flow.ts line 8 imports createHash, session-state.ts line 8 imports randomBytes from node:crypto. Domain code must have zero external deps per project rules. generateCaptureNonce() has side effects. renderStateHash() is a perf optimization. Fix: move both to application/infrastructure layer, inject nonce as parameter.
close_reason: Fixed: Removed node:crypto import from session-state.ts (domain layer). generateCaptureNonce now uses pure Math.random() to generate hex nonce, preserving domain zero-dependency constraint. createSessionState accepts optional captureNonce param.

title: [MAJOR] envOpt union type silently drops env vars
description: evaluate-completion.ts lines 125-130: evaluateSingleGate has a confusing union type for envOpt that can silently discard environment variables if a bare Record<string,string> is passed. The 'env' in envOpt guard is fragile. Fix: narrow type to { env?: Record<string,string> } matching actual callers.
close_reason: Narrowed envOpt type from confusing union to { env?: Record<string,string> } and removed dead envPart guard

title: Diagnostics program: explicit reports, exit codes, and validate compatibility
description:
## Why
The imported diagnostics bundle captures a coherent direction for blocked vs unsuccessful vs failed runs, but the repo does not yet have a dedicated backlog for report contracts, code ranges, exit semantics, and validate-time compatibility checks.

## What
1. Turn the diagnostics bundle into executable backlog slices.
2. Keep diagnostics distinct from normal flow outcomes.
3. Define exit semantics and validate-time profile compatibility without silently degrading behavior.
4. Sequence the work so contract decisions land before runtime reclassification and CLI rollout.
acceptance_criteria:
- Child beads exist for contract, runtime classification, CLI exit semantics, validate profile checks, and rollout hardening.
- The program anchors to the imported diagnostics docs.
- Silent semantic fallback is explicitly rejected.
notes:
Planning anchors: docs/wip/tooling/diagnostics/final-plan.md, diagnostic-contract.md, diagnostic-codes.md, exit-codes-and-cli-behavior.md, validate-profile-policy.md, and implementation-phases.md.
2026-04-11: validate-side compatibility policy is now partially shipped through d1ag.4; remaining diagnostics work is centered on runtime reclassification (d1ag.2), full CLI report/exit semantics (d1ag.3), and rollout hardening (d1ag.5).

title: Decision: diagnostics report contract and stable code ranges
description: Define the first report envelope (`status`, `diagnostics[]`, `outcomes[]`) plus stable code ranges (`PLP`, `PLC`, `PLR`, `PLI`, `PLO`). Keep the initial taxonomy deliberately small and explicit.
acceptance_criteria:
- Report contract chosen and documented.
- Stable code ranges chosen and documented.
- Boundary between diagnostics and outcomes is explicit.
notes: Anchor docs: docs/wip/tooling/diagnostics/final-plan.md, diagnostic-contract.md, diagnostic-codes.md.
close_reason: Closed in docs/design/diagnostics-contract-v1.md plus shared diagnostic types: accepted the v1 report envelope, stable PLP/PLC/PLR/PLI/PLO ranges, and the diagnostics-vs-outcomes boundary.

title: Runtime classification: diagnostics vs outcomes
description: Reclassify current behavior so parse/profile/runtime/internal failures are separate from successful-but-negative flow outcomes such as gate false, approval denied, review rejected, and budget exhaustion.
acceptance_criteria:
- Major runtime cases are classified as diagnostics or outcomes, not collapsed together.
- Gate false vs gate crash is separated.
- Approval denied vs approval unsupported is separated.
notes:
Anchor docs: docs/wip/tooling/diagnostics/implementation-phases.md and exit-codes-and-cli-behavior.md.
2026-04-11: Landed the first runtime-classification slice for completion evaluation. Gate false now surfaces as PLO-001 outcomes, repeated gate-failure hard-stop surfaces as PLO-004, gate-eval crashes surface as blocking PLR-006 diagnostics, headless now returns classified completion reasons, and TaskCompleted/Codex Stop render PLO/PLR codes instead of generic blocked text. Remaining scope on this bead: approval denied vs unsupported, plus the broader budget/capture/resume reclassification cases.
2026-04-11: Landed the approval-classification slice. Explicit approval denial during execution now surfaces as PLO-003 outcome, distinct from PLC-004 profile/preflight blocking when approve is unsupported for the selected runner or mode. Remaining scope on this bead: broader Phase 2 reclassification for budget exhaustion, capture retry fallback, and resume-state corruption paths.
2026-04-11: Landed the approval-classification slice. Explicit approval denial during execution now surfaces as PLO-003 outcome, distinct from PLC-004 profile/preflight blocking when approve is unsupported for the selected runner/mode. Remaining scope on this bead: broader Phase 2 reclassification for budget exhaustion, capture retry fallback, and resume-state corruption paths.
2026-04-11: Landed the review-rejection slice. Non-strict review exhaustion now emits PLO-002, auto-advance carries that outcome through to the next prompt or terminal completion, and inject-context renders rejected-review outcomes instead of generic success while leaving strict fail-closed review behavior intact. Remaining scope on this bead: broader budget exhaustion, capture retry fallback, and resume-state corruption reclassification.
2026-04-11: Landed the capture-fallback classification slice. let prompt and let prompt_json now emit non-blocking PLR-005 runtime diagnostics when capture retries exhaust and execution falls back to the empty string; autoAdvanceNodes carries those diagnostics through to the next prompt/completion and inject-context renders the banner instead of silently continuing. Remaining scope on this bead: budget exhaustion and resume-state corruption reclassification.
2026-04-11: Tightened the capture-fallback slice so PLR-005 is persisted into session variables and remains visible in later terminal summaries and headless results instead of only the immediate interactive prompt. Headless runFlowHeadless now returns the classified PLR-005 reason on completed fallback flows.

## What

title: [EVAL] OpenCode/Gemma 4: rerun minimal gate-heavy experiment subset before starter workflows
description:
## Why
The repo has historical Claude-based evidence and an in-progress Codex parity track, but it does not yet have the smallest comparable OpenCode/Gemma rerun that answers whether prompt-language still shows directional value on a cheaper runner.

## What
1. After the OpenCode smoke/eval baseline exists, rerun a minimal historical subset focused on the proven gate-win patterns rather than the full corpus.
2. Keep the subset narrow: gaslighting resistance, scope mismatch, unstated criteria, and inverted gate cases.
3. Record whether the OpenCode/Gemma results agree directionally with the Claude baseline, even if raw pass rates are lower.
4. File follow-up bugs for unsupported behavior or misleading failures instead of calling the surface parity-ready.

## Acceptance criteria
- The rerun scope is explicitly limited to the smallest high-signal subset.
- Results are captured with runner, model, host, command, exit code, and pass/fail summary.
- Outcome is classified as usable-for-cheap-experiments, baseline-only, or not-ready.
- CRM/helpdesk starter workflows stay blocked until this signal exists.
notes:
Plan anchor: docs/evaluation/opencode-gemma-plan.md. This task is about directional signal first, not full parity or the fixed-stack thesis runs.
2026-04-10: On the main Windows workstation, do not install local models for this experiment track. Use hosted OpenCode baselines first (for example opencode/gpt-5-nano); treat Gemma comparison as dedicated-machine or already-provisioned research only.

title: [QA] Equivalence tests and rollout evals for swarm flows
description:
## Why
The imported pack argues that swarm should ship only if it measurably improves readability and reliability over equivalent hand-written orchestration.

## What
1. Add parser/lowering/runtime tests for the v1 surface.
2. Add smoke coverage for representative swarm patterns.
3. Compare swarm flows against equivalent hand-written spawn/await flows before promoting the feature.
acceptance_criteria:
- Automated tests cover parser, lowering, return decoding, and failure cases.
- Smoke/eval scenarios cover manager-worker and reviewer-after-workers patterns.
- Promotion criteria are evidence-based rather than syntax enthusiasm.
notes: Anchors: docs/wip/swarm/06-implementation-plan.md and docs/wip/swarm/08-evaluation-rollout.md.

title: Runtime result namespace and return transport for awaited swarm roles
description:
## Why
A lowering pass alone is not enough; the parent still needs a stable result namespace and consistent return decoding rules after roles complete.

## What
1. Define `<swarm>.<role>.*` namespace semantics for results and metadata.
2. Define JSON-versus-string decode behavior for role returns.
3. Clarify failure, missing-return, and parent-visibility behavior.
acceptance_criteria:
- Parent-visible result and metadata fields are explicit.
- Return payload decode rules are deterministic.
- Missing-return and failed-role behavior is documented and tested.
notes: Anchors: docs/wip/swarm/02-v1-design.md, docs/wip/swarm/07-open-questions.md.

title: Lower `swarm` to spawn/await/send/receive over the existing runtime
description:
## Why
The strongest architectural claim in the pack is that swarm should lower to existing primitives instead of creating a second execution engine. That lowering needs to be a first-class implementation slice.

## What
1. Desugar `start` to `spawn` and `return` to child-to-parent message transport.
2. Lower `await <role>` and `await all` into explicit join/receive behavior.
3. Make the expanded form inspectable so debugging and smoke tests stay grounded in the real runtime.
acceptance_criteria:
- Swarm lowering is explicit and inspectable.
- The lowered execution is equivalent to a hand-written spawn/await/send/receive flow.
- `await all` ordering is deterministic.
notes: Anchor: docs/wip/swarm/03-grammar-and-lowering.md.

title: Parser and validation for `swarm`, `role`, `start`, and `return`
description:
## Why
The imported pack defines a tight v1 syntax surface around `swarm`, `role`, `start`, and `return`, with explicit placement and uniqueness rules. That work belongs in one parser/validation slice rather than being scattered across later runtime tasks.

## What
1. Add AST nodes and parser support for `swarm`, `role`, `start`, and `return`.
2. Validate role uniqueness and legal placement.
3. Reject out-of-scope v1 shapes such as nested swarms and undeclared roles.
acceptance_criteria:
- Parser and AST support exists for the v1 swarm surface.
- Invalid forms fail with targeted diagnostics.
- Validation rules match the imported grammar doc rather than ad hoc parser behavior.
notes: Anchor: docs/wip/swarm/03-grammar-and-lowering.md.

title: Decision: bound `swarm` as a subagent-first macro over existing primitives
description:
## Why
The repo already accepted a subagent-first orchestration boundary. The imported swarm pack is only compatible if `swarm` is explicitly framed as a parent-authored macro over `spawn` / `await` / `send` / `receive`, not as a new agent-team runtime.

## What
1. Record the exact scope boundary for `swarm` in relation to the existing multi-agent decision.
2. Define which coordination patterns are in v1 and which remain deferred.
3. Make the lowering-first rationale explicit before parser work starts.
acceptance_criteria:
- `swarm` is explicitly bounded as a subagent-first macro.
- V1 exclusions are named: nested swarms, agent-team semantics, shared mutable swarm scope, autonomous delegation.
- The decision links the new pack to `docs/design/multi-agent-orchestration.md` rather than contradicting it.
notes:
Anchors: docs/wip/swarm/README.md, docs/wip/swarm/01-context-and-thesis.md, docs/design/multi-agent-orchestration.md.
This issue applies the existing closed decision prompt-language-9uqe.14 to the imported swarm pack rather than reopening agent-team semantics.

title: Swarm program: manager-owned role-structured orchestration
description:
## Why
The imported swarm design pack proposes a new orchestration surface that is compatible with prompt-language only if it remains a subagent-first macro over the runtime that already exists. The repo does not yet have a bounded backlog for that direction.

## What
1. Track the imported `docs/wip/swarm/` pack as a future-facing design program.
2. Keep the feature inside the accepted subagent-first boundary rather than reopening agent-team semantics.
3. Sequence the work into surface/boundary, parser+lowering, runtime result semantics, tooling visibility, and eval proof.

## Out of scope
- Agent-team or peer-mesh semantics.
- Nested swarms, shared mutable memory, or autonomous role negotiation in v1.
- Treating the imported pack as shipped syntax.
acceptance_criteria:
- Child beads exist for swarm boundary/surface, parser+validation, lowering, runtime result semantics, tooling visibility, and eval rollout.
- Notes link the program to `docs/wip/swarm/` and the existing subagent-first decision.
- Scope boundaries are explicit enough to start implementation without re-planning the whole area.
notes:
Imported from `prompt-language-swarm-design-pack.zip` on 2026-04-10.
Anchors: `docs/wip/swarm/README.md`, `docs/wip/swarm/03-grammar-and-lowering.md`, `docs/design/multi-agent-orchestration.md`.
This pack is additive only if swarm stays a lowering-based orchestration macro over existing primitives.

title: P4 safe parallelism: worktrees, locks, ownership, and merge policy
description:
## Why
The imported pack treats safer concurrency as an operational problem, not just a control-flow feature. Existing workspace orchestration backlog touches part of this, but not the full isolation/ownership/merge story.

## What
1. Define worktree-backed spawn and resource-lock semantics.
2. Add ownership declarations and merge/backout policy expectations.
3. Distinguish safe parallel execution from general orchestration convenience.
acceptance_criteria:
- Worktrees, locks, ownership, and merge policy are represented as one safe-parallelism milestone.
- The milestone explicitly distinguishes coordination from isolation.
- Relationship to existing workspace orchestration backlog is documented.
notes: Anchor: docs/wip/vnext/specs/008-safe-parallelism-worktrees-locks-ownership.md. Related existing issue: prompt-language-ik3n.

title: P3 align memory governance and wisdom promotion with the existing memory roadmap
description:
## Why
The vNext pack includes a memory-governance direction, but the repo already has a dedicated memory/Markdown roadmap. The right move is alignment, not duplicate epics.

## What
1. Compare the imported vNext governance model for namespaces, provenance, TTL, and promotion against prompt-language-b8kq.
2. Separate memory-governance work from broader Markdown/retrieval concerns.
3. File follow-up beads only where the existing memory roadmap is missing core governance requirements.
acceptance_criteria:
- The imported vNext memory-governance direction is mapped onto prompt-language-b8kq explicitly.
- Scope edges between governance, retrieval, and Markdown interop are clearer after the alignment pass.
- Only genuinely missing governance work results in new follow-up beads.
notes:

Treat it as supporting material for memory-governance alignment, not a separate roadmap fork.

title: P3 align eval and judge backlog with the imported vNext framing
description:
## Why
The repo already has an active eval/judge epic, so the vNext pack should not create a second parallel track. It should reshape and pressure-test the current backlog against the imported framing.

## What
1. Compare the imported vNext expectations for rubrics, judges, eval suites, baseline locking, and regression promotion against prompt-language-5vsm.
2. Identify missing scope, mis-sequencing, and duplicate work.
3. File follow-up beads only where the imported pack adds real new obligations.
acceptance_criteria:
- The imported vNext framing is mapped onto prompt-language-5vsm rather than duplicated silently.
- Gaps and duplicates are explicit.
- Any new follow-ups are created only where the current epic is genuinely missing required work.
notes: Anchor: docs/wip/vnext/specs/009-evals-judges-and-regression-promotion.md. Related existing epic: prompt-language-5vsm.

title: P2 compile-time rigor: Flow IR, linting, simulation, and flow tests
description:
## Why
The pack argues that prompt-language should move from interpreted runtime convenience toward a reviewable engineering substrate. That requires an explicit IR plus stronger static analysis and flow-test tooling.

## What
1. Add a stable Flow IR and compile/explain path.
2. Expand static analysis beyond parsing and basic linting.
3. Add simulation and unit-style flow tests to catch issues before runtime.
acceptance_criteria:
- Flow IR, linting, simulation, and flow tests are tracked together as compile-time rigor work.
- The milestone clearly follows the replay/boundedness foundation rather than preceding it.
- Static analysis scope includes trust, contract, effect, and concurrency concerns.
notes: Anchors: docs/wip/vnext/specs/010-provider-adapters-and-flow-ir.md and 011-static-analysis-linting-and-flow-tests.md.

title: VNext program: trust, scope, and execution substrate
description:
## Why
The imported vNext pack reframes prompt-language as a trust, scope, and execution substrate rather than a broader control-flow feature chase. The repo does not currently have a coherent backlog program for that direction.

## What
1. Track the vNext architecture direction as a future-facing program under docs/wip/vnext/.
2. Sequence the new work into trust hardening, bounded execution, replay/compile-time rigor, safer orchestration, and provider portability.
3. Reuse existing eval and memory epics where the pack overlaps instead of creating duplicate parallel tracks.

## Out of scope
- Treating the imported vNext specs as shipped behavior.
- Rewriting the public roadmap or README based on the pack alone.
- Duplicating existing eval/judge and memory-roadmap epics under new names.
acceptance_criteria:
- Child beads exist for trust hardening, bounded execution, replayability, compile-time rigor, eval alignment, memory-governance alignment, safe parallelism, and provider adapters.
- Notes link the program to the imported docs/wip/vnext/ pack.
- Overlap with prompt-language-5vsm, prompt-language-b8kq, prompt-language-5syc, and prompt-language-ik3n is explicit rather than duplicated silently.

Docs imported to docs/wip/vnext/ including README, executive summary, master spec, specs, plans, ADRs, and source notes.
