title: Decision: custom artifact types, review logs, storage, and plugin renderer boundary
description:
## Why
The bundle leaves open questions around custom types, attachment typing, review storage, version scoping, large binaries, and whether renderers live in core or plugins. Those choices affect architecture boundaries and implementation cost.

## What
1. Decide where custom artifact types are declared and how they evolve.
2. Decide where review comments/approvals live and whether they are version-scoped.
3. Decide the storage boundary for large attachments and whether DOCX/PDF renderers are core or plugin-provided.
acceptance_criteria:
- The custom type declaration model is explicit.
- Review/comment storage and versioning semantics are explicit.
- Core-versus-plugin renderer boundaries and large-attachment handling are explicit.
notes: Anchor doc: open-questions.md.

title: Phase 2: artifact registry, validation, and CLI inspection
description:
## Why
A package convention alone is not enough. The runtime needs to validate artifacts, resolve them by id or alias, and let developers inspect them without hand-reading folders.

## What
1. Add artifact validation for manifest shape, required fields, attachment existence, and renderer status.
2. Add registry or lookup semantics for artifact ids and aliases.
3. Add CLI inspection commands so artifacts can be listed, shown, and validated directly.
acceptance_criteria:
- Runtime or CLI validation covers manifest shape and attachment/view expectations.
- Artifacts can be resolved by stable id or alias.
- CLI inspection is sufficient to debug artifact emission without bespoke scripts.
notes: Implements Phase 2 from runtime-semantics.md.

title: Phase 1: artifact package convention, schemas, and sample renderers
description:
## Why
The bundle's recommended first release is the package convention plus manifest and sample renderers. That is the smallest implementation slice that produces a real artifact protocol without overcommitting syntax or UI.

## What
1. Add a runtime-facing artifact package convention under the repo's working directories.
2. Promote the draft manifest/type schemas into repo-owned artifacts or validation fixtures.
3. Provide sample renderer outputs or fixtures that prove the package-plus-views model works.
acceptance_criteria:
- A canonical artifact package convention exists in the repo/runtime.
- Draft schemas are either adopted, adapted, or replaced with repo-owned validation artifacts.
- At least one sample artifact package demonstrates manifest plus multiple views.
notes: Ground on schemas/ and sample-artifact-package/ from the bundle.

title: Decision: artifact package, manifest, and renderer contract
description:
## Why
The strongest concrete proposal in the bundle is that an artifact is a package plus manifest with multiple views, not a single HTML or Markdown file. That contract needs to be explicit before implementation starts.

## What
1. Define the canonical artifact package layout.
2. Set the manifest fields required for identity, lifecycle, provenance, payload, review state, views, and attachments.
3. Define the renderer contract so Markdown, HTML, JSON, and export-oriented views are treated as projections, not separate artifact types.
acceptance_criteria:
- The canonical package layout is defined.
- Required manifest fields are specified with stable semantics.
- Renderer outputs are defined as views over one artifact package rather than separate primary artifacts.
notes: Anchor doc: manifest-and-renderers.md; check draft schemas and sample package folders before implementation.

title: Decision: artifact taxonomy and initial built-ins
description:
## Why
The bundle proposes broad artifact families plus a smaller initial release cut. The repo needs an explicit release cut before runtime work can target concrete artifact types.

## What
1. Evaluate the intent/change/verification/oversight/learning taxonomy.
2. Choose the smallest initial built-in set worth supporting first.
3. Decide how custom artifact types coexist with built-ins without freezing the wrong surface too early.
acceptance_criteria:
- Artifact families are documented with rationale, not just listed.
- An initial built-in set is chosen and justified.
- The custom-type story is separated from the initial built-in release cut.
notes: Anchor doc: artifact-taxonomy.md.

title: Decision: artifact boundary and non-goals
description:
## Why
The bundle's core value is the distinction between artifact, state, memory, log, side effect, and gate. That boundary is not explicit in the current repo, which invites accidental scope creep.

## What
1. Define artifacts as durable, human-reviewable, machine-readable run outputs.
2. Make explicit what artifacts are not: generic files, hidden reasoning dumps, memory, or gate substitutes.
3. Clarify the boundary between the artifact program and existing eval/replay/audit work.
acceptance_criteria:
- Definition explicitly separates artifacts from state, memory, logs, side effects, and gates.
- Non-goals are written down so future artifact work does not silently absorb adjacent concerns.
- Existing related issues are classified as overlapping, blocked by, or out of scope rather than left ambiguous.
notes: Anchor docs: prompt-language-artifact-definition.md and README.md from the artifacts bundle.

title: Artifacts program: durable reviewable machine-readable outputs
description:
## Why
prompt-language-artifacts-bundle.zip captures a coherent artifacts direction, but the repo currently has only partial related work spread across eval artifact capture, replay, audit logging, and context-adaptive output handling. Without a dedicated backlog, artifact work will keep getting conflated with logs, memory, gates, or generic files.

## What
1. Define the artifact boundary and non-goals for prompt-language.
2. Turn the bundle's package, manifest, renderer, runtime, and review ideas into sequenced backlog.
3. Keep broad artifact protocol work separate from existing eval-only artifact capture and replay tasks.
4. Defer user-facing syntax and UI claims until the runtime protocol is shaped and evidence exists.

## Out of scope
- Replacing gates, memory, or logs with artifacts.
- Treating every generated file as a first-class artifact.
- Blindly importing the bundle's proposed syntax as shipped DSL.
acceptance_criteria:
- Child beads exist for artifact boundary, taxonomy, package/manifest contract, runtime semantics, validation/CLI work, artifact-aware gate integration, and deferred syntax/UI rollout.
- The epic notes distinguish this program from existing related issues prompt-language-5vsm.6, prompt-language-67qn, prompt-language-42wb, prompt-language-lrna, and prompt-language-0ovo.4.
- The backlog is sequenced so runtime protocol work precedes user-facing syntax and UI work.
notes:

Program seed issue IDs: Decision: artifact boundary and non-goals=prompt-language-50m6.1, Decision: artifact package, manifest, and renderer contract=prompt-language-50m6.3, Decision: artifact runtime lifecycle and flow semantics=prompt-language-50m6.4, Decision: artifact taxonomy and initial built-ins=prompt-language-50m6.2, Decision: custom artifact types, review logs, storage, and plugin renderer boundary=prompt-language-50m6.8, Future gate: user-facing artifact syntax and review UI rollout=prompt-language-50m6.9, Phase 1: artifact package convention, schemas, and sample renderers=prompt-language-50m6.5, Phase 2: artifact registry, validation, and CLI inspection=prompt-language-50m6.6, Phase 3: artifact-aware gates and approval integration=prompt-language-50m6.7

- 02-design/runtime-semantics.md
- 02-design/open-questions.md
Scoped overlap reminders:

- prompt-language-67qn stays state snapshot/replay, not reviewable artifact packaging

title: G3: Add research plan doc
description:
## Why
The work needs a research framing that makes success and failure conditions explicit.

## What
Add a research document covering hypotheses, metrics, fixture categories, and rejection criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
- doc is committed
- hypotheses and failure conditions are explicit
- relation to eval infrastructure is clear

title: G2: Add roadmap entry
description:
## Why
Tracked work should be visible in roadmap docs without pretending it is already shipped.

## What
Add a roadmap entry for context-adaptive rendering and related instrumentation work, plus an exploratory note for deferred spawn/session work.
acceptance_criteria:
- roadmap entry has a clear status
- entry does not imply the feature is shipped
- deferred exploratory work is separated cleanly
notes: Left open intentionally. docs/roadmap.md already has local user modifications, and roadmap/status wording should remain explicit rather than imported blindly.

title: G1: Write ADR for context-adaptive rendering
description:
## Why
The program needs a stable architectural decision record before implementation spreads across runtime, eval, and docs.

## What
Write the ADR documenting context, decision, alternatives, risks, rollout, and reversal criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
- ADR is committed
- alternatives and risks are documented
- status is marked proposed/accepted/rejected explicitly
notes: Fulfilled by docs/adr/ADR-00XX-context-adaptive-rendering.md imported from the scoped context-adaptive bundle on 2026-04-10.

title: D3: Validate summary safety
description:
## Why
Output summarization might reduce fix quality or recovery quality on large-output tasks.

## What
Run targeted comparisons to test whether summary-based rendering harms correction quality, diagnosis quality, or recovery safety.
acceptance_criteria:
- benchmark comparison completed
- large-output scenarios are covered
- material regressions are documented explicitly

title: D2: Store and reference large outputs as artifacts
description:
## Why
Large outputs should be accessible without bloating every render.

## What
Persist large outputs and reference them from compact renders using stable identifiers and deterministic summary blocks.
acceptance_criteria:
- artifacts use stable identifiers
- runtime preserves exact raw output access
- render output references artifacts instead of dumping full payloads when policy says so

title: D1: Define output summarization policy
description:
## Why
Large stdout/stderr blocks create prompt churn, but summarizing too aggressively can hide critical details.

## What
Define deterministic thresholds and formatting for when output is inlined, summarized, or artifactized.
acceptance_criteria:
- byte thresholds are defined
- summary format is deterministic
- raw output remains accessible

title: C4: Add experimental config flag
description:
## Why
Compact rendering should not become default before evidence exists.

## What
Expose compact mode behind an internal or experimental flag so it can be benchmarked and manually tested without changing default behavior.
acceptance_criteria:
- default remains full mode
- compact mode can be enabled in benchmarks and manual runs
- flag behavior is documented internally

title: C3: Implement compact gate view
description:
## Why
Ordinary turns do not always need the full gate display.

## What
In compact mode, show pending/failing gates and hide already-satisfied irrelevant gate detail while preserving gate truth semantics.
acceptance_criteria:
- compact mode hides irrelevant satisfied-gate detail
- gate semantics remain unchanged
- tests verify gate view parity where required

title: C1: Implement current-path-only rendering
description:
## Why
Full rendering shows more flow state than ordinary turns usually need.

## What
In compact mode, render only the active path, current node, and the minimum supporting runtime context required for safe execution.
acceptance_criteria:
- inactive branches are omitted by default in compact mode
- current node remains explicit and unambiguous
- full mode remains unchanged

title: B3: Remove incidental prompt volatility
description:
## Why
Timestamps, unstable ids, and formatting noise can create churn unrelated to real state changes.

## What
Eliminate incidental volatility from prompt text and verify equivalent states do not churn unnecessarily.
acceptance_criteria:
- diff tests prove no incidental churn for equivalent states
- render output excludes timestamps and other unstable noise by default
- documented exceptions are explicit and tested

title: B2: Implement deterministic ordering
description:
## Why
Without deterministic ordering, repeated identical states can still produce different prompt text.

## What
Ensure sections, variables, gates, and other rendered collections are output in a deterministic order.
acceptance_criteria:
- repeated identical states render byte-identically
- snapshot tests pass
- no nondeterministic map/object ordering remains in renderer output

title: B1: Define canonical render spec
description:
## Why
Equivalent states may currently render with incidental differences, which makes prompt churn analysis and optimization less trustworthy.

## What
Write an internal rendering contract for section order, variable order, whitespace, truncation, and other formatting guarantees.
acceptance_criteria:
- spec is written and reviewed
- test expectations map directly to spec rules
- spec covers all user-visible render sections

title: A4: Build baseline benchmark pack
description:
## Why
There is no baseline benchmark pack dedicated to render cost and prompt volatility across representative flow types.

## What
Select representative fixtures and collect baseline measurements under the current renderer before changing behavior.
acceptance_criteria:
- benchmark categories are documented
- baseline results are committed to a report artifact
- fixtures include gate-heavy, long-flow, large-output, and recovery cases

title: A3: Measure hook/runtime overhead
description:
## Why
Current latency numbers do not isolate render cost from hook startup, state I/O, and gate execution.

## What
Instrument hook startup, state-file read/write, and gate execution separately so runtime cost can be attributed cleanly.
acceptance_criteria:
- timings are recorded independently
- benchmark output separates render cost from non-render cost
- timing collection is covered by tests or fixture validation

title: A2: Measure prompt/render byte composition
description:
## Why
The runtime lacks per-hook visibility into how much of the rendered prompt is stable, dynamic, variable-related, or command-output-related.

## What
Capture per-hook render size, stable-vs-dynamic bytes, variable counts, and command-output byte counts for UserPromptSubmit, Stop, and TaskCompleted.
acceptance_criteria:
- metrics emitted for all three hooks
- tests cover expected metric presence
- output distinguishes stable bytes from dynamic bytes

title: A1: Add render telemetry model
description:
## Why
There is no agreed machine-readable schema for prompt/render/runtime metrics, so measurement is fragmented and hard to compare across runs.

## What
Define and implement a telemetry schema covering prompt bytes, changing bytes, variable counts, output bytes, hook timings, I/O timings, gate timings, turn counts, fallback counts, and recovery incidents.
acceptance_criteria:
- schema covers the agreed metric set
- telemetry output is machine-readable
- telemetry can be disabled or gated behind config

title: EPIC G: Documentation and positioning
description:
## Why
Keep roadmap, ADR, research docs, and product-facing docs aligned with actual implementation status.

## What
Keep roadmap, ADR, research docs, and product-facing docs aligned with actual implementation status.
acceptance_criteria:
- ADR
- roadmap entry
- research doc
- post-results docs discipline

title: EPIC D: Artifactized output handling
description:
## Why
Reduce prompt churn from large stdout/stderr blocks while preserving exact raw access.

## What
Reduce prompt churn from large stdout/stderr blocks while preserving exact raw access.
acceptance_criteria:
- summarization policy
- artifact persistence
- safety validation
notes: Scoped runtime/output-handling track. Related but not identical to existing artifact and replay backlog: prompt-language-5vsm.6, prompt-language-67qn, prompt-language-42wb, prompt-language-lrna.

title: EPIC C: Compact renderer (experimental)
description:
## Why
Add a smaller safe rendering mode behind a flag, with full mode preserved as the baseline.

## What
Add a smaller safe rendering mode behind a flag, with full mode preserved as the baseline.
acceptance_criteria:
- current-path rendering
- variable slicing
- compact gate view
- experimental flag

title: EPIC B: Canonical renderer foundation
description:
## Why
Make equivalent states render byte-identically before trying to shrink the render surface.

## What
Make equivalent states render byte-identically before trying to shrink the render surface.
acceptance_criteria:
- canonical spec
- deterministic ordering
- snapshot tests

title: EPIC A: Instrumentation baseline
description:
## Why
Add measurement before behavior change so render cost, hook cost, state I/O, and gate cost can be separated.

## What
Add measurement before behavior change so render cost, hook cost, state I/O, and gate cost can be separated.
acceptance_criteria:
- telemetry schema
- baseline metrics collection
- benchmark pack
- machine-readable output

title: Context-adaptive rendering program
description:
## Why
The scoped context-adaptive rendering bundle adds a coherent runtime program for measuring and, if justified, introducing compact rendering without weakening prompt-language's deterministic recovery and gate semantics.

## What
1. Keep full-state rendering as the correctness baseline.
2. Sequence instrumentation, canonicalization, compact-mode experiments, fallback policy, evaluation, and docs as one visible program.
3. Keep roadmap and product-facing documentation honest until implementation and evidence exist.
4. Track render-specific output handling separately from the broader artifact/replay backlog already in the repo.
acceptance_criteria:
- Child epics exist for instrumentation, canonical rendering, compact rendering, output handling, fallback policy, evaluation, and documentation.
- The program notes point to the checked-in ADR, research plan, and eval template.
- Documentation tasks already satisfied by imported docs are closed immediately.
- The scoped program remains distinct from the broader artifacts bundle and existing replay backlog.
notes:
Imported from prompt-language-context-adaptive-bundle.zip on 2026-04-10.
Docs added in this import:
- docs/adr/ADR-00XX-context-adaptive-rendering.md
- docs/research/context-adaptive-rendering.md
- docs/evaluation/context-adaptive-rendering-results-template.md
Reviewed but not imported wholesale:
- prompt-language-artifacts-bundle.zip (overlaps existing artifact/replay backlog such as prompt-language-5vsm.6, prompt-language-67qn, prompt-language-42wb, prompt-language-lrna)

title: Decision: checkpoint, handoff, and compaction boundary for memory
description:
## Why
The new memory docs make a stronger claim than the existing backlog: checkpoints, resumability, handoff summaries, and compaction are core runtime behavior, not just extra memory writes. That boundary needs a real product decision before implementation fragments.

## What
1. Define what belongs in execution/session state versus durable memory.
2. Define checkpoint, resumability, handoff-summary, and compaction responsibilities.
3. Reconcile this with existing resume-state backlog so the repo does not grow two overlapping checkpoint models.
4. Create implementation follow-up beads only after the boundary is explicit.
acceptance_criteria:
- Clear boundary documented between execution state and durable memory.
- Checkpoint/handoff/compaction responsibilities are explicit.
- Existing related backlog can be mapped to the chosen model.
- Follow-up implementation beads created only where needed.
notes: Planning anchors: docs/wip/memory/memory-knowledge-positioning.md and docs/wip/memory/memory-scrutiny-and-risks.md.

title: Markdown knowledge interop: knowledge sources and deterministic section lookup
description:
## Why
The checked-in docs propose `knowledge:` declarations and deterministic `section` lookup so Prompt Language can consume Markdown guidance without pretending that docs are the same thing as runtime state.

## What
1. Define the DSL surface for declaring Markdown knowledge sources.
2. Define deterministic section-addressing semantics.
3. Specify how loaded knowledge becomes visible in status/artifacts so it remains inspectable.
4. Update docs/tests together.

## Out of scope
- Semantic/vector retrieval as a default language behavior.
- Treating Markdown guidance as automatically authoritative policy.
acceptance_criteria:
- `knowledge:` and `section` semantics are documented and accepted into backlog-ready form.
- Inspectability requirements are explicit.
- Parser/runtime/docs/tests are identified for implementation.
- Validation path is explicit before code changes start.

title: Scoped memory semantics: strict reads, invalidation, transactional writes
description:
## Why
The current shipped memory surface is useful but too flat. The new planning docs call for scoped memory, strict versus optional reads, TTL/invalidation, and transactional writes so the runtime can stay deterministic instead of quietly trusting stale state.

## What
1. Define the concrete syntax and semantics for memory scopes.
2. Add strict/optional read behavior with explicit failure/default rules.
3. Add TTL/invalidation and transactional write timing.
4. Update parser/runtime/docs/tests together so the behavior is inspectable and not just parser sugar.

## Likely touch points
- `src/application/parse-flow.ts`
- memory store/runtime integration
- docs/reference for `remember` / `memory:`
- parser/runtime tests
acceptance_criteria:
- Scoped memory semantics documented and parser-validated.
- Required memory reads fail loudly; optional reads and defaults are explicit.
- TTL/invalidation and write timing rules are specified and covered by tests.
- Validation evidence includes `npm run test` and `npm run ci`.

title: Disciplined memory + Markdown interop roadmap
description:
## Why
The repo now has a coherent bundle of checked-in docs for disciplined memory, Markdown knowledge interop, retrieval, and evaluation alignment, but there is no executable backlog turning that direction into scoped product work. The older memory bead (`prompt-language-7g58`) covers only part of the surface.

## What
1. Turn the checked-in memory/Markdown docs into an implementation-ready backlog.
2. Keep deterministic runtime state, durable memory, advisory Markdown guidance, and retrieval semantics clearly separated.
3. Sequence the work into the smallest slices that can be validated end to end.
4. Make the trust model, checkpoint boundary, and retrieval boundary explicit before the DSL grows further.
acceptance_criteria:
- Child beads exist for memory semantics, Markdown knowledge interop, checkpoint/handoff boundaries, and retrieval/trust decisions.
- The epic notes reference the checked-in planning docs and the legacy memory bead.
- Scope edges are explicit enough to start implementation without re-planning the whole area.
notes:
Planning anchors: docs/wip/memory/memory-knowledge-positioning.md, docs/wip/memory/memory-scrutiny-and-risks.md, docs/wip/memory/markdown-agent-memory.md, docs/wip/memory/memory-roadmap.md, docs/wip/memory/memory-source-notes.md. Legacy related bead: prompt-language-7g58.
Imported `prompt-language-plan.zip` to `docs/wip/memory/knowledge-plan/` on 2026-04-10.
The pack reinforces the current memory roadmap rather than creating a separate program: explicit `knowledge:`/`section`, scoped memory, checkpoint-first rollout, retrieval as an abstraction layer, and eval above normal execution.
Use it as a concise companion pack to the main anchors already linked here.

title: Decision: `match` first or dedicated `route` for bounded dispatch
description:
## Why
The repo now has a checked-in design note at `docs/wip/runtime/routing-and-dispatch.md`, but there is no backlog item that decides whether bounded dispatch should land as a general `match`/`switch` construct or as a specialized AI-facing `route` feature.

## What
1. Validate the actual pain: branch verbosity versus classification-plus-dispatch semantics.
2. Compare three options: user-space routing only, general symbolic dispatch (`match`/`switch`), and dedicated `route`.
3. Decide whether route metadata, confidence handling, and low-confidence approval belong in the core language or remain a pattern.
4. File follow-up implementation beads only for the winning direction.

## Out of scope
- Implementing parser/runtime changes before the decision is written.
- Adding dynamic path loading or open-ended model-directed dispatch.
acceptance_criteria:
- Explicit decision recorded against `docs/wip/runtime/routing-and-dispatch.md`.
- Clear yes/no on `match` first versus `route`.
- Follow-up implementation beads created only if the product decision says to proceed.
notes: Planning anchor: docs/wip/runtime/routing-and-dispatch.md.

title: [BACKLOG] Helpdesk SDLC starter experiment on OpenCode/free-model baseline
description: Track the helpdesk SDLC starter example, smoke flow, and supporting documentation for the lower-cost runner pass. Revisit it only after the OpenCode runner smoke/eval baseline exists so the experiment uses the intended cheaper surface.

title: [BACKLOG] CRM SDLC starter experiment on OpenCode/free-model baseline
description: Track the CRM SDLC starter example, demo flow, QA flow, and experiment plan for the lower-cost runner pass. Revisit it only after the OpenCode runner smoke/eval baseline exists so the experiment uses the intended cheaper surface.
notes: Checked-in planning docs exist now: examples/crm-sdlc/docs/experiment-plan.md and examples/crm-sdlc/docs/bead-crm-sdlc-experiment.md.

title: Low-cost experiment pass: starter workflows on OpenCode and free-model runners
description:
Why: the next round of starter-workflow experimentation should get cheaper, not wait on Codex-specific stabilization if a runner-based OpenCode path can provide a better cost surface.

What:
- Revisit the starter workflow experiments once the OpenCode runner baseline exists
- Use the cheaper runner surface to validate whether prompt-language workflows remain useful when model quality/cost shifts downward
- Keep the experiment goals concrete: reproducible task runs, comparable evidence, and follow-up product issues for friction

Acceptance criteria:
- The experiment pass is explicitly blocked on the OpenCode runner baseline rather than on Codex stabilization language
- Concrete starter-workflow backlog items exist under this epic
- The experiment output is expected to produce follow-up product/docs beads, not just anecdotes
notes:
This replaces the inaccessible raw JSONL-only starter-experiment backlog phrasing that referenced "after Codex stabilization". The Beads DB version should depend on the OpenCode baseline instead.
Child experiment tasks are individually blocked on prompt-language-9uqe.15.3 because Beads does not allow task blockers on epics.
Execution order clarified in docs/evaluation/opencode-gemma-plan.md: Stage 1 OpenCode/Gemma baseline, Stage 2 minimal gate-heavy rerun subset, then Stage 3 CRM/helpdesk starter workflows. Do not skip directly from prompt-only validation to the starter experiments.

title: Self-hosted meta-layer pilot: prompt-language maintains its own rubrics, judges, evals, and docs first
description:
## Why
Self-hosting should begin at the prompt-language artifact layer, not with an immediate runtime rewrite.

## What
1. Define a bounded pilot where prompt-language maintains its own evaluation artifacts first
2. Scope the pilot to `.flow` files, rubrics, judges, eval suites, dataset docs, and related notes
3. Require locked regression suites before expanding toward runtime-adjacent work
4. State clear stop conditions for the pilot if maintenance cost exceeds value

## Acceptance criteria
- The first self-hosted slice is narrow, testable, and repo-local
- Regression protection is part of the plan before self-modifying work begins
- The pilot excludes parser/executor self-rewrite in the initial phase
- Success metrics are stated before implementation starts

title: Eval artifacts: trace capture, replay, baseline lock, and human annotation hooks
description:
## Why
If judges score traces, diffs, or tool usage, the repo needs durable artifact capture and replay rather than disposable logs.

## What
1. Define the stored artifact set for each eval run: outputs, diffs, test logs, tool transcripts, and metadata
2. Add replay support by run ID so results can be inspected after the fact
3. Add baseline-lock semantics so comparisons use stable references
4. Leave room for human annotation and calibration workflows without forcing them into the happy path on day one

## Acceptance criteria
- Eval runs persist a stable artifact bundle with enough data for debugging and audit
- Replay works from a checked-in or locally persisted run identifier
- Baseline lock behavior is defined and testable
- The design is compatible with later human annotation workflows
notes:
2026-04-10: The runner now persists machine-readable eval reports and compares against locked baseline reports, which partially satisfies the artifact bundle direction. Remaining gap: replay by run id, richer artifact bundles, and annotation hooks are still open here.
2026-04-10: Baseline-lock semantics are now real at the file/report level, including mismatched-dataset rejection in the eval dataset runner tests. Keep this issue focused on replay-by-run-id, richer artifact bundles, and annotation workflows.

title: Experiment 6: Self-healing CI / PR fixer
description: Prove usefulness on a high-value narrow workflow: automatically repair failing CI runs without weakening quality gates. Sequence through tsc, lint, test with fix loops. This is bounded, measurable, repetitive, and already aligned with the repo's current strengths. Strong commercial candidate. Track: repair success rate, false positive rate, time to green, cost, regression introduction rate.

title: Experiment 4: Parallel isolated-module build
description: Test whether multiple coding agents help when seams are truly independent (e.g., packages/api + packages/web + packages/e2e). Spawn workers with cross-directory spawn, await all, then stabilize. Track: merge conflict rate, duplicate work rate, integration bug count, time to green, stabilization loops after await. Only expected to win with real isolation boundaries.

title: Experiment 3: Parallel planning experiment
description: Test where multi-agent helps first — not coding, but parallel specialist artifact production. spawn planner/architect/tester in parallel to produce docs, then implement from those docs. Compare against single-agent on: completeness of plan, missed acceptance criteria, implementation success, token cost, duplicated work, human trust in artifacts. Validates artifact-first multi-agent flows.

title: Experiment 2: Bounded feature implementation benchmark
description: Test whether prompt-language can reliably add one medium feature to an existing app (audit log, notifications, saved filters, export CSV, etc). Compare single-agent with flow+gates vs plain prompt on a rubric: feature works, compiles, tests pass, auth correct, migration safe, UX acceptable, no regressions. Score 0-2 each. Run at least 3 repetitions per condition.

title: Experiment 1: Premature-stop benchmark
description: Run A/B comparison: plain Claude Code prompt vs prompt-language loop+gates on 10-20 small repo-local tasks (fix tests, fix lint, repair builds, add endpoints, patch regressions). Track: task success rate, premature stop rate, time to green, tokens/cost, human cleanup minutes, number of loops. Expected outcome: higher completion rate, lower premature-stop rate, slightly higher cost. This validates the core hypothesis that structured control flow + gates beat freeform prompting.

title: Define 20-task benchmark suite and canonical stack
description: Define the canonical stack/template and 20-task benchmark suite that all experiments will use. The stack should be fixed (one framework, one DB, one auth model, one test runner, one deployment path). The 20 tasks should cover: fix-test scenarios, fix-lint/type scenarios, broken-build repair, single-feature additions with tests, and regression patches. Each task needs a reproducible starting repo state. This is prerequisite infrastructure for all 6 experiments.

title: Set up experimentation folder structure
description: Create the directory structure and organizational scaffolding for scientific experimentation, evaluation, and research. This includes: experiments/ root with subdirectories for each experiment (premature-stop-benchmark, bounded-feature-benchmark, parallel-planning, parallel-isolated-modules, full-saas-factory, self-healing-ci), a shared eval/ directory for rubrics and scoring scripts, a results/ directory for run data, and templates for experiment design docs (hypothesis, methodology, metrics, results). This must be done before any experiments can be run.
close_reason: Created the experimentation scaffold with per-experiment folders, shared eval/results areas, and reusable templates.

title: [RESEARCH] OpenClaw integration spike: first runnable path, constraints, and security model
description:
## Why
OpenClaw is the most interesting non-Claude host in the current research set because it has durable agents, plugins, approvals, and a more always-on operational model. An integration spike would test whether prompt-language should remain session-scoped, become host-adaptable, or stop at advisory integration.

## What
1. Identify the smallest credible OpenClaw integration path: wrapper flow runner, plugin, prompt asset, or host-interop shim
2. Map lifecycle differences versus Claude/Codex: approvals, durable state, scheduled execution, plugin model, and sandbox expectations
3. Define the security posture for any integration proposal, especially around skills/plugins, approvals, and secret handling
4. Produce a recommendation: prototype now, track as future work, or defer

## Acceptance criteria
- Integration options and constraints documented
- Security and product-boundary implications called out explicitly
- Clear next-step recommendation produced

title: [RESEARCH] portriam pilot: validate one real workflow with prompt-language
description:
## Why
`portriam` provides a second external project with different constraints, which is useful for checking whether prompt-language generalizes beyond a single repo shape or workflow style. If the repo name/spelling differs from the chat shorthand, confirm the exact target before implementation.

## What
1. Identify one repetitive `portriam` workflow that would benefit from explicit sequencing and gates
2. Design a small pilot flow for that workflow
3. Run the pilot and capture friction points: context loading, command integration, gate design, and portability
4. Convert any repeated pain points into product/docs beads here

## Acceptance criteria
- One concrete `portriam` workflow selected
- Pilot flow tested on the target repo
- Product/docs follow-ups created for meaningful gaps

title: [RESEARCH] llm-quant pilot: validate one real workflow with prompt-language
description:
## Why
`llm-quant` is a good candidate for real-world dogfooding if it has repeatable workflows such as research synthesis, backtest prep, experiment review, or guardrailed multi-step coding tasks. A real pilot will expose whether prompt-language helps outside controlled fixtures.

## What
1. Pick one concrete `llm-quant` workflow worth encoding as a prompt-language flow
2. Map the workflow steps, required variables, gates, and any repo-specific context files
3. Implement a first pilot flow in that repo (or a scratch branch/worktree if safer)
4. Record what worked, what was awkward, and which missing features or docs gaps appeared

## Acceptance criteria
- One named `llm-quant` workflow selected and scoped
- Pilot flow authored and exercised at least once
- Findings captured as docs or follow-up beads in this repo

title: Dogfood prompt-language on real projects and external hosts
description:
Why:
The next useful proof point is not another synthetic example but real workflows in real projects. Dogfooding prompt-language in external repos should reveal missing primitives, portability issues, prompt ergonomics problems, and where the language actually saves time versus just looking clever in demos.

What:
- Pilot prompt-language in concrete external projects owned by the user, starting with `llm-quant` and `portriam`
- For each pilot, identify one high-value workflow that is repetitive enough to justify a flow, then test whether prompt-language helps in practice
- Explore an OpenClaw integration path as a separate host/runtime spike, with explicit attention to security, approval surfaces, and lifecycle differences
- Capture reusable lessons: what should become docs, examples, features, or product-boundary decisions inside this repo

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- Each target project has a scoped pilot bead with a concrete workflow to test
- OpenClaw exploration yields a documented first-path recommendation or explicit reasons to defer
- Cross-project learnings are captured as follow-up docs/issues

title: [RESEARCH] Codex path: native hooks vs supervised runner integration
description:
Question set:
- Given current upstream maturity, should Codex support begin with native hooks at all?
- Can prompt-language get enough value from AGENTS.md, notifications, and supervised codex exec JSON output to support a useful first integration?
- Which completion and guardrail behaviors can be enforced externally by the prompt-language runner, and which require host-native support?

What:
- Compare two Codex integration paths: native hooks (if/when stable) versus supervised runner integration.
- Identify the minimal honest Codex support story prompt-language could ship without claiming full Claude parity.
- Recommend whether native Codex hooks should be tracked now, tracked later behind an experimental flag, or ignored.

Relevant sources to confirm against current behavior:
- Codex config reference
- Codex AGENTS.md guidance
- Codex notifications / exec JSON docs
acceptance_criteria:
- Recommendation clearly chooses initial Codex path: native hooks, supervised runner, both staged, or no implementation
- Recommendation distinguishes stable current surfaces from experimental ones
- Output includes explicit risks of overpromising parity with Claude

title: [RESEARCH] Product boundary: DSL syntax vs config layer vs host-only integration
description:
Question set:
- If prompt-language manages host lifecycle automation, is that a language feature, a runtime adapter concern, or a repo-level configuration concern?
- Would adding hook syntax to the DSL create false parity promises across hosts?
- Does the existing language already have the right abstraction boundary, with hooks remaining an integration concern outside the core flow language?

What:
- Compare three shapes: inline DSL syntax, separate config file compiled to host integrations, and host-specific installers with no shared abstraction.
- Evaluate each option for user value, conceptual fit, architectural cost, and documentation honesty.
- Make an explicit recommendation for what prompt-language should and should not expose.

Relevant code/docs to inspect:
- docs/research/08-feature-completeness.md
- docs/roadmap.md
- docs/wip/index.md
acceptance_criteria:
- Options compared: DSL, config/platform layer, host-specific only
- Recommendation states whether hook management belongs inside the language surface
- Recommendation names the least-confusing user-facing shape, if any

title: [RESEARCH] Capability matrix: Claude hooks vs Codex lifecycle surfaces
description:
Question set:
- Which lifecycle events are actually available today across Claude Code and Codex CLI?
- Which behaviors are first-class and blocking (for example prompt interception or completion blocking) versus merely advisory or observational?
- How do compaction/resume/session-start semantics differ?
- Which current prompt-language integration points are Claude-specific today?

What:
- Map Claude hook events against Codex capabilities such as AGENTS.md, notifications, JSON exec output, and any experimental hook surface.
- Identify the true lowest-common-denominator model, if one exists.
- Record which semantics are host-specific and should not be normalized away.

Relevant code/docs to inspect:
- bin/cli.mjs
- hooks/hooks.json
- src/infrastructure/adapters/claude-process-spawner.ts
- docs/research/08-feature-completeness.md
acceptance_criteria:
- Capability matrix covers event timing, blocking ability, context injection, completion enforcement, and session restore
- Matrix distinguishes shipped/stable host behavior from experimental behavior
- Recommendation states whether a host-neutral abstraction is viable or misleading

title: Decision: should prompt-language add multi-host lifecycle automation / hook management?
description:
Why:
The recent discussion surfaced a real product question: prompt-language already relies on Claude Code hooks, but expanding toward multi-host hook management risks mixing together three different things: core DSL syntax, host/runtime integration, and cross-host product scope. Claude's lifecycle hooks are stable today, while Codex currently offers AGENTS.md, notifications, JSON event output, and only an experimental native hooks surface.

What:
- Produce a decision note on whether prompt-language should add multi-host lifecycle automation / hook management at all.
- Decide whether this belongs in core DSL syntax, a repo-level config layer, runtime adapters, or should remain host-specific.
- Use Claude Code and Codex CLI as the concrete comparison set.
- Name the near-term recommendation clearly: implement now, track as planned, defer, or decline.
acceptance_criteria:
- Decision note checked into docs/design/ or docs/research/
- Recommendation explicitly answers whether this should exist in prompt-language at all
- Recommendation explicitly answers DSL vs config/platform integration
- Recommendation states whether Codex support should be advisory-only, experimental, or blocked on upstream maturity

title: Decision: MCP scope should be flow-facing, not generic extension management
description: Why: MCP is already on the roadmap as a way to expose prompt-language flow state to other clients, but recent discussion expanded toward managing skills, agents, and MCP servers themselves. That risks product drift unless the scope boundary is explicit. What: write a decision note comparing two directions: (1) flow-facing MCP integration limited to inspecting and controlling prompt-language sessions, and (2) a broader Claude extension management plane for skills/subagents/MCP/plugins. Evaluate user value, implementation cost, coupling to vendor-specific semantics, and overlap with the host client. Acceptance criteria: decision records whether MCP remains limited to flow inspection/control, names any allowed control operations, and lists out-of-scope management features.
notes: 2026-04-11 ecosystem pack: adjacent workflow shells and hosting runtimes (Archon, GitHub Agentic Workflows, AgentScope Runtime) are useful references, but they strengthen the case that prompt-language should stay flow-facing rather than becoming generic extension or platform administration.

title: Decision: profiles and agents vs direct management of skills, hooks, plugins, and MCP
description: Why: the emerging profiles/agents work needs a clean abstraction boundary. If prompt-language starts directly managing Claude Code skills, hooks, plugins, and MCP server lifecycle, the DSL and config model will mirror host-specific features instead of staying declarative. What: decide whether prompt-language should expose its own abstractions (profiles, agents, flow files, harness adapters) while treating host extensions as backend capabilities, or whether direct extension-management UX belongs in scope. Compare portability, terminology clarity, docs burden, and risk of backend lock-in. Acceptance criteria: decision note states the preferred abstraction layer, defines which concepts are first-class in prompt-language, and identifies any host-managed surfaces that must remain outside prompt-language v1.
close_reason: Host-extension boundaries are already defined in docs/design/host-extension-boundary.md.

title: Decision: subagent-first orchestration vs agent-team semantics
description: Why: recent discussion raised whether prompt-language should grow from parent-coordinated spawn/await workers into agent-team style coordination with peer messaging and shared task lists. The product needs an explicit yes/no boundary before more backlog accrues around agents and multi-harness execution. What: write a short decision note comparing subagent-style orchestration, agent-team semantics, and defer/no-build. Evaluate architecture fit, context model, runtime complexity, token cost, and documentation burden. Acceptance criteria: decision note records whether prompt-language stays subagent-first, what is explicitly out of scope, and which existing beads should proceed, pause, or be reframed.
notes: Imported swarm design pack tracked under prompt-language-1wr7 on 2026-04-10. This does not reopen the closed product-category decision; it applies the existing subagent-first boundary to a possible lowering-based swarm surface.
close_reason: Multi-agent orchestration is already bounded as subagent-first in docs/design/multi-agent-orchestration.md.

title: Prompt libraries: namespaced import syntax and symbol resolution
description: Extend import beyond whole-file inclusion so a file can be imported into a namespace for exported symbol lookup. This is the bridge between existing static import and reusable libraries.
acceptance_criteria: Parses top-level import "path" as name syntax; Namespace collisions are lint errors; Missing namespaces or symbols are lint errors; Relative path rules and cycle detection stay aligned with base import
close_reason: Implemented namespaced imports and symbol resolution with the shared-library example.

title: Prompt libraries: parameter binding, defaults, and linting for use calls
description: Reusable library assets need a minimal parameter model so teams can customize commands and targets without drifting into arbitrary functions.
acceptance_criteria: Supports named parameters with defaults; Missing required arguments fail validation; Unknown arguments fail validation; Parameter substitution is deterministic and documented
close_reason: Implemented parameter binding and defaults for use calls and validated the example files.

title: Prompt libraries: done-when expansion for exported gate bundles
description: Allow completion logic to reuse exported gate bundles instead of repeating the same predicates and custom gate commands across flows.
acceptance_criteria: Supports use namespace.symbol(...) inside done when; Exported gates expand into normal completion predicates; Expanded gate commands remain visible in render/validate output; Unknown or invalid gate bundle usage fails validation
close_reason: Implemented done-when expansion for exported gate bundles and validated the example files.

title: Prompt libraries: flow-side use expansion for exported flow and prompt assets
description: Allow flow bodies to invoke exported reusable assets through a use form that expands before runtime. This should cover both exported flow fragments and single prompt templates.
acceptance_criteria: Supports use namespace.symbol(...) inside flow; Exported flow assets expand into normal flow nodes; Exported prompt assets expand into prompt nodes; Expanded render output remains visible for review and dry-run
close_reason: Implemented namespaced use expansion for reusable flow and prompt assets and validated the example files.

title: Prompt libraries: library/export syntax for flow, gates, and prompt assets
description: Define the file-level syntax for reusable prompt-language assets. The first exported asset kinds should cover reusable flow fragments, gate bundles, and prompt templates.
acceptance_criteria: Supports library metadata declaration; Supports export flow, export gates, and export prompt definitions; Exported names are unique within a library; Invalid export forms fail validation clearly
close_reason: Implemented library/export syntax and validated the example files.

title: Prompt libraries: namespaced exported reusable DSL assets
description: Add a first-class prompt-library model on top of parse-time import so flows can reuse exported DSL assets instead of relying on copy-paste or skills. The first version should stay declarative: local or package-style library resolution, required namespaces, static expansion, and visible expanded output for auditability.
acceptance_criteria: Supports a tracked prompt-library feature umbrella; Scopes the feature around export flow, export gates, export prompt, and use; Keeps expansion parse-time and audit-visible; Explicitly excludes remote URL imports and arbitrary runtime functions in v1
close_reason: Prompt libraries are implemented and validated with the shared-library example plus docs coverage.

title: [RESEARCH] Variable observability and UX: inspectability, provenance, and docs gaps
description:
Question set:
- Are variable problems mainly semantic, or are they mostly debugging and observability gaps?
- Today variables are rendered as a flat list with truncation and hidden auto-vars; is that sufficient?
- Do users need provenance, history, source-node visibility, or better docs before new syntax is added?

What:
- Audit current variable visibility in renderFlow, docs, and debugging workflows.
- Identify the highest-friction gaps when understanding variable state during real runs.
- Recommend minimal documentation/tooling improvements versus deeper runtime changes.

Related open bead:
- prompt-language-h2an
acceptance_criteria:
- Highest-friction variable debugging gaps identified
- Recommendation separates doc fixes, tooling fixes, and runtime changes
- Output gives a clear short list of low-risk improvements

title: [RESEARCH] Variable data structures: lists today, maps/objects/types tomorrow
description:
Question set:
- What data structures do variables really support today?
- Lists exist, but they are represented as JSON array strings in the variable store; is that the right long-term model?
- Should the runtime grow native arrays, objects/maps, or typed values, or stay string-first with small escape hatches?

What:
- Audit the current variable store and list behavior.
- Evaluate tradeoffs of: scalar-only plus JSON strings, native arrays only, arrays plus objects/maps, and typed declarations.
- Recommend whether to pursue richer data structures now, later, or not at all.

Related open beads:
- prompt-language-mewj
- prompt-language-s5cx
acceptance_criteria:
- Current list/data representation and constraints documented
- Options compared with implementation cost, UX impact, and compatibility risk
- Recommendation identifies the next reasonable step, if any

title: [RESEARCH] Variable semantics: declaration, undefined values, and scope rules
description:
Question set:
- How are variables defined today: parser forms, runtime initialization paths, built-in auto-vars, foreach vars, and child-variable import?
- Do variables need explicit prior definition, or is current late-bound interpolation acceptable?
- What should happen with undefined variables, fallback syntax, reassignment, and shadowing?
- Should the language remain flat/global or move toward lexical/block scope?

What:
- Document current semantics with code references.
- Compare current flat global model against a lint-only model and a lexical-scope model.
- Recommend whether declaration rules belong in parser, linter, runtime, or docs.

Related open beads:
- prompt-language-cva6
- prompt-language-nm4l
- prompt-language-brwd
acceptance_criteria:
- Current declaration and lookup semantics documented precisely
- Recommendation given for undefined vars, declaration requirements, and shadowing policy
- Recommendation states whether enforcement should be docs-only, lint-only, or runtime/parser-level

title: [RESEARCH] Variable model: semantics, scoping, and data structures
description:
Why:
The language already has real variable machinery (`let`/`var`, built-in auto-vars, foreach loop vars, child vars after `await`), but the design questions are still muddy: what counts as a variable definition, whether undefined variables are acceptable, whether scope should stay flat, and whether richer data structures should exist.

What:
- Inspect the shipped variable model across parser, AST, session state, interpolation, advancement, rendering, and docs.
- Separate current behavior from proposed enhancements.
- Identify which open variable-related beads are true feature gaps versus documentation or tooling gaps.
- Produce a short design/research note that becomes the reference point for future variable work.

Related open beads to consider:
- prompt-language-h2an (/flow-vars skill)
- prompt-language-cva6 (const keyword)
- prompt-language-nm4l and prompt-language-brwd (shadow warnings)
- prompt-language-mewj (structured map variables)
- prompt-language-s5cx (typed variable declarations)
acceptance_criteria:
- Research note checked into docs/research/ or docs/design/
- Note covers current shipped behavior, pain points, options, and recommendation
- Note explicitly links or references affected open beads
notes: QA found a validator gap: examples that use memory-prefetched keys and remember/runtime-introduced vars still emit 'Reference to undefined variable' warnings (for example helpdesk-sdlc/project.flow warns on helpdesk_target_market and preferred_package_manager). The issue is likely lint semantics, not the example flows.

title: Choose canonical prompt-language file extension and migrate file-workflow backlog
description:
Why: The current backlog is inconsistent: some issues assume .flow, others assume .pl. The user also explicitly wants a unique file type and OS integration. The project needs one canonical extension before editor tooling and installers proceed.

What:
- Choose a canonical prompt-language file extension that is unique enough to avoid common ecosystem collisions
- Record rejected options including .pl and .flow if they are deemed too ambiguous or already occupied in adjacent tooling
- Define migration/update plan for docs, CLI commands, editor tooling, and file association work
- Make all file-based workflow issues depend on this decision

Acceptance criteria:
- Decision note records the chosen extension with rationale
- Open issues that assume .pl/.flow are updated or linked behind this decision
- CLI/editor/desktop-integration follow-up work references the chosen extension consistently
close_reason: Canonical extension and migration direction are already reflected in the file-workflow docs and example set.

title: Terminology decision: skill vs profile vs agent vs flow file
description:
Why: The recent design discussion clarified that several concepts were getting conflated: skills, profiles, agents, and flow files. The implementation backlog will stay muddy unless the project records the separation explicitly.

What:
- Write a decision note defining each concept precisely:
  - skill = reusable know-how/instructions for an agent runtime
  - profile = reusable context pack for prompt/ask turns
  - agent = named spawned runtime definition for spawn
  - flow file = executable prompt-language program
- State the governing rule: profiles control what a turn sees; agents control what a spawned session is
- Be explicit about where each concept appears: DSL syntax, config file, runtime adapter, installer UX, docs
- Capture non-goals: avoid OO method syntax and avoid making raw skills a first-class DSL primitive in v1

Acceptance criteria:
- Decision note checked into docs/design/ or docs/decisions/
- Existing epic/design beads reference the terminology
- README/docs can use the terms consistently without overlap
close_reason: Terminology is already canonicalized in docs/design/terminology-skill-profile-agent-flow.md and related docs.

title: Explicit context filesets for prompt/ask turns
description:
Why: The user wants more than flow imports. They also want explicit control over which project files are injected into prompt/ask turns as context, without abusing spawn/agent configuration for normal turns.

What:
- Add a first-class way for profiles (and possibly prompt/ask directly) to declare context files or file sets
- Define safe path rules, ordering, truncation/size limits, and merge behavior
- Ensure file-context injection is separate from source-code import: import composes DSL; context files feed the model
- Make the behavior deterministic so the same profile yields the same injected context

Acceptance criteria:
- Config/design specifies how filesets are declared
- Runtime behavior for loading/injecting files is documented
- Clear diagnostics for missing/unsafe files
- Context budgeting rules documented to avoid silent bloat
notes: 2026-04-11 ecosystem pack: Context Cascade and Agent Loom reinforce selective loading and explicit context-packet thinking, but this should stay deterministic and fileset-oriented rather than becoming a broad plugin hierarchy.

title: First-class flow-file workflow: file-based execution and runner selection
description:
Why: Flows are currently mostly inline strings. A first-class file mode improves authoring, reuse, editor support, and launch ergonomics.

What:
- Add a canonical prompt-language file extension and execution path
- Do not hardcode .pl or .flow until the extension decision is closed
- Support CLI execution like prompt-language run path/to/file
- Allow file-based flows to resolve relative imports and project-local config cleanly
- Define how runner selection works when a flow file is launched directly

Acceptance criteria:
- Canonical extension decision referenced and followed
- CLI supports executing a file-based flow
- Relative import resolution documented for file execution mode
- README/docs show file-first workflow
- Errors include filename/line context where possible
- Smoke/eval coverage includes at least one file-based flow execution path

title: Named agents for spawn: harness + default profile resolution
description:
Why: spawn currently names a child process, but not a reusable agent persona/runtime. Users want sub-agents with stable defaults such as harness, model/profile, and role-specific context.
What:
- Add named agent definitions in config
- Support spawn "name" using agent "reviewer" or equivalent declarative syntax
- Resolve agent definitions to harness + default profile + optional model/runtime options
- Keep spawn declarative; do not add OO method-call syntax
Acceptance criteria:
- Agent definition schema documented
- Parser accepts agent reference syntax on spawn nodes
- Runtime resolves named agent definitions before launch
- Clear precedence between explicit spawn options and agent defaults
- Imported child variables remain backward compatible
notes: Named agents should model spawned-session startup config only. Normal prompt/ask turns should prefer profiles/context packs rather than targeting raw agents by default.

title: Context profiles: reusable bundles for skills, memory, and prompt injection
description:
Why: Users need a way to super-control injected context without copying the same skill/memory/system-prompt setup into every node.

What:
- Add config-level profiles that bundle context sources such as skills, memory files, system preamble, model hints, and tool policy
- Add DSL support for selecting a profile at flow-level and per-node, for example: use profile "repo-map", prompt using profile "planner", and ask ... using profile "reviewer"
- Merge profile context deterministically with existing flow injection rules

Acceptance criteria:
- Profile schema documented and validated
- Parser accepts the chosen profile syntax
- Runtime passes resolved profile context into prompt/ask execution
- Unknown profile names fail loudly with actionable diagnostics
- Tests cover precedence: default profile vs node override vs explicit agent config
notes: Profiles should be able to declare explicit context filesets for prompt/ask turns, but the details are tracked in child bead prompt-language-9uqe.9 for dedicated design and runtime work.

title: Design declarative profiles, named agents, and harness registry
description:
Why: The project needs a clean design before implementation. The key decision is whether skills are a first-class language primitive or part of a wider profile/context-pack abstraction.

What:
- Write a design doc covering declarative profiles, named agents, harness registry, and file-based execution
- Define syntax candidates such as use profile "name", prompt using profile "name", and spawn "name" using agent "reviewer"
- Specify what belongs in language syntax vs config file vs installer UX
- Define how existing Claude Code skills and Codex skills map into a harness-agnostic profile model
- Explicitly separate startup-only agent config (skills, tools, MCP servers, sandbox, model) from per-node prompt context (files, memory snippets, extra instructions)
- Decide whether prompt and ask may target a named agent directly, or only a profile/context pack
- Explicitly reject imperative/OO syntax unless a stronger case emerges

Acceptance criteria:
- Design doc checked into docs/design/
- Separates DSL semantics from harness-specific implementation
- Defines migration path from current claude-specific spawn adapter
- Resolves whether prompt/ask/spawn all support profile modifiers in v1
- Resolves how agent inheritance works by default for skills/context to avoid surprise context bloat
- Lists verification and smoke-test implications
close_reason: Design coverage already exists in docs/design/multi-agent-orchestration.md and docs/design/terminology-skill-profile-agent-flow.md.

title: Profiles, agents, imports, and runner UX for prompt-language
description:
Why: prompt-language currently orchestrates a single hardcoded Claude Code harness with inline flow text. Users want stronger control over injected context, reusable context bundles, named sub-agents, reusable flow imports, and a first-class flow-file workflow that can launch through a preferred agent harness.

Scope:
- Reusable context profiles that can bundle skills, memory/context files, system preamble, model hints, and tool policy
- Named agent definitions that map spawn targets to a harness plus a default profile
- Static composition of flow files via import (reuse existing bead prompt-language-jkfn)
- Harness abstraction so agent execution is not permanently tied to claude -p
- First-class flow-file execution and default-runner configuration
- OS-level installer/UX for file association and launch behavior
- Guided configuration UX (command/skill) for setting up harnesses, profiles, and file associations

Non-goals:
- OO/method-call DSL syntax such as agent.skillLoad(...).prompt(...)
- Arbitrary function imports into the DSL in the first iteration
- Baking Claude-specific skill semantics directly into the core AST

Architecture direction:
Keep the DSL declarative. Treat skills as one possible backend capability inside a broader profile/context-pack model so the same DSL can target multiple harnesses over time.

title: [QA] Release readiness checklist for v0.4.0
description:
## Why
Releases need a systematic checklist to prevent shipping broken or incomplete features. This issue serves as the pre-release gate for v0.4.0.

## What
Before tagging v0.4.0:
1. All P0 and P1 issues closed (or consciously deferred with documented reason)
2. npm run ci passes with 0 warnings
3. npm run eval:smoke passes 3 consecutive runs (all non-flaky tests)
4. npm audit shows 0 high/critical vulnerabilities
5. CHANGELOG.md updated with all changes since v0.3.0
6. DSL cheatsheet updated for any new syntax
7. CLAUDE.md DSL primitives section accurate
8. version bumped in package.json
9. npm publish dry run succeeds
10. At least one human end-to-end test on a real project

## Acceptance criteria
- All 10 checklist items completed
- Sign-off documented in issue comments before tagging

title: [QA] Backlog grooming: close stale issues, reprioritize, identify quick wins
description:
## Why
Issue backlogs accumulate cruft over time — duplicate issues, superseded ideas, issues that became irrelevant after implementation. Regular grooming keeps the backlog actionable and morale high.

## What (recurring — run monthly)
1. Run: bd stale — identify issues with no activity in 60+ days
2. Run: bd orphans — identify issues with broken dependencies
3. Review all P3/P4 issues: are they still relevant? Close anything superseded
4. Identify 3 quick-win issues (high impact, low cost, no blockers) to highlight for the next sprint
5. Review all in_progress issues: are any stalled? Flag for human decision
6. Check for duplicate issues created by separate sessions
7. Update priority on any issues that have become more/less urgent

## Acceptance criteria
- All stale issues resolved (closed, deferred, or commented with status update)
- All orphan dependencies fixed
- 3 quick wins identified and labeled
- Findings documented as comment

## Recurrence
Close and create a new instance monthly.
close_reason: Backlog grooming run complete: stale_open=0, orphan_deps=0, in_progress=0; quick-win candidates tagged: prompt-language-vxii, prompt-language-3713, prompt-language-9xmr.

title: [QA] Verify all 13 node kinds have dedicated smoke test coverage
description:
## Why
The 26 smoke tests cover many scenarios but coverage of individual node kinds may be uneven. Some node kinds (spawn, await, continue) may only appear incidentally in combination tests. Each node kind deserves at least one dedicated smoke test that exercises it as the primary subject.

## What
Audit the 26 smoke tests against all 13 node kinds:
- prompt, run, while, until, retry, if, try, foreach, let, break, continue, spawn, await

For each node kind, identify which smoke test(s) primarily exercise it. If any node kind has no primary smoke test (only incidental coverage), create a new targeted smoke test scenario.

Also verify the ask keyword has a dedicated smoke test (it was added in Phase 4).

## Acceptance criteria
- Coverage matrix documented (node kind → smoke test ID)
- Any gaps have new smoke test scenarios added to smoke-test.mjs
- Total smoke test count updated in CLAUDE.md and MEMORY.md if new tests added
close_reason: Verified dedicated smoke coverage in scripts/eval/smoke-test.mjs for prompt/run/while/until/retry/if/try/foreach/let/break/continue/spawn/await; ask-judge path covered via AK grounded-by while scenario.

title: [QA] Edge case test matrix for control flow: empty bodies, max iterations, nested limits
description:
## Why
Control flow edge cases (empty foreach lists, while with max=1, foreach with max_advances exceeded, break at top level, continue in non-loop) are handled but not systematically tested. A matrix test ensures coverage of the combinatorial space.

## What
Create advance-flow.edge-cases.test.ts covering:
1. foreach with empty item list — should skip body entirely
2. while with max=1 — should execute body exactly once
3. retry with max=1 — should execute body exactly once, no retry
4. break immediately at loop start — exits without executing body
5. continue at loop end — re-enters without double-executing
6. nested foreach 3 levels deep — correct path indices
7. try with no catch and no finally — body failure marks flow failed
8. if with no else and false condition — advances past block
9. spawn with no variables in scope — child state is empty
10. let with empty string value — stored as empty string, not null
11. Flow with 0 nodes — completes immediately
12. Single-node flow (just a prompt) — normal advancement

## Acceptance criteria
- All 12+ cases have unit tests
- All pass
- Added to CI (npm run test)

title: [QA] Cross-platform smoke test run: verify plugin works on Linux and macOS
description:
## Why
The plugin is developed on Windows but runs on Linux (CI) and macOS (most developers). Path separator differences (/ vs \), CRLF vs LF line endings, and shell differences (bash vs zsh vs PowerShell) can cause subtle breakage that only surfaces on non-Windows platforms.

## What
1. Run full npm run eval:smoke on Linux (GitHub Actions runner or WSL)
2. Run full npm run eval:smoke on macOS (if available)
3. Specifically verify: file path construction in all adapters, shell command execution, state file read/write, hook execution
4. Document any platform-specific failures
5. Add CI matrix to run smoke tests on ubuntu-latest and macos-latest if not already present

## Acceptance criteria
- Smoke tests pass on Linux (at minimum)
- Any platform-specific failures have issues created
- CI matrix documented or implemented
notes:
Workspace blocker: live smoke cannot execute here because claude -p returns an org access/login error. The smoke harness should fail fast with a clear blocker message instead of running all scenarios and returning 0/41 passed.
Workspace blocker: claude -p returns an org access/login error in this environment ('Your organization does not have access to Claude. Please login again or contact your administrator.'). The smoke harness has been updated to fail fast on this blocker instead of running 41 empty scenarios, but the live smoke run still needs a valid Claude login to complete.

title: [REVIEW] DSL ergonomics review: footguns, onboarding friction, and missing primitives
description:
## Why
The DSL evolves organically. Without periodic review, footguns accumulate, syntax becomes inconsistent, and the onboarding experience degrades. An experienced PL designer reviewing the DSL from scratch surfaces issues that are invisible to daily contributors.

## What (recurring — run every 6 months or before a major version)
1. Read the DSL cheatsheet cold — does it make sense without prior knowledge?
2. Write 3 non-trivial flows from scratch using only the docs — where did you get stuck?
3. Review the known footguns (stringly-typed, globals, no precedence, no scoping)
4. Are there any primitives that are missing and frequently worked around?
5. Review the lint warnings — are they helpful or noisy?
6. Review the error messages when flows fail — are they actionable?
7. Check: is the 'ask' keyword intuitive or confusing?
8. Review the flow rendering output — does it clearly show current position?

## Acceptance criteria
- At least 3 findings filed as issues or improvements
- DSL cheatsheet updated if any inaccuracies found
- Findings documented as comment

## Recurrence
Close and create a new instance every 6 months.

title: [REVIEW] Test coverage regression check: maintain >90% line coverage
description:
## Why
Coverage can silently erode as features are added without tests, or as tests are removed during refactors. The CI enforces coverage thresholds but only at the point of PR — between PRs, coverage can drift.

## What (recurring — run before each release or when adding a major feature)
1. Run npm run test:coverage
2. Review coverage report: identify any new files below 80% line coverage
3. For domain/ files specifically: target is 95%+ (pure functions are fully testable)
4. For presentation/hooks: 70%+ acceptable (integration-tested via smoke tests)
5. Identify the 3 lowest-coverage files and create targeted test tasks
6. Check that all new node kinds from recent PRs have unit tests in advance-flow.test.ts

## Acceptance criteria
- Overall coverage still >90%
- domain/ files >95%
- Any file below threshold has a tracked issue
- Report filed as comment

## Recurrence
Close and create a new instance before each release.
close_reason: Coverage review run complete via npm run test:coverage: overall line coverage 91.53% (>90) and domain line coverage 93.25%. Lowest files tracked as follow-up tasks: prompt-language-njy7 (mcp-server.ts), prompt-language-vm2a (render-node-to-dsl.ts), prompt-language-r7pk (run-flow-headless.ts).

title: [REVIEW] Smoke test reliability audit: flakiness rates and coverage gaps
description:
## Why
The 26 smoke tests are the only end-to-end signal that the plugin actually works. Some tests (S, T, Y, V) are known-flaky due to Claude nondeterminism. Over time, flakiness rates change as the model updates, capture protocol changes, and new features are added. This review tracks the signal.

## What (recurring — run monthly or after any capture protocol change)
1. Run npm run eval:smoke 3 times and record which tests fail each run
2. Update MEMORY.md with current known-flaky tests
3. For each consistently failing test: create a bug issue with the failure pattern
4. For any new feature not covered by a smoke test: create a coverage gap issue
5. Review test descriptions — are all 13 node kinds covered end-to-end?
6. Check smoke test runtime — still under 8 minutes for full suite?

## Acceptance criteria
- Flakiness rates documented for current known-flaky tests
- Any new consistent failures have bug issues created
- Coverage gaps identified and tracked

## Recurrence
Close and create a new instance monthly.
close_reason: Ran npm run eval:smoke three times (results: smoke-2026-04-10T05-04-12-038Z.json, smoke-2026-04-10T05-05-52-763Z.json, smoke-2026-04-10T05-07-34-154Z.json). All runs were 0/41 with identical failures, indicating a consistent host/harness failure pattern rather than isolated flakiness. Filed follow-up bug prompt-language-k1dg. Existing node-kind coverage matrix already tracked in closed issue prompt-language-9xmr.

title: [REVIEW] Security audit: injection, credentials, nonce, and trust boundary review
description:
## Why
The security model has multiple safeguards (shell injection, capture injection, credential filtering, path traversal, state integrity). New features can introduce gaps. Periodic review ensures no safeguard has been silently weakened.

## What (recurring — run before each release)
1. Review shellInterpolate() — are all run: command substitutions using it?
2. Review filterSpawnVariables() — does it still exclude _key/_token/_secret/_password?
3. Review the path traversal regex in evaluate-completion.ts — still correct?
4. Review the capture nonce — still UUID4, per-session, validated on read?
5. Check for any new user-controlled strings that reach shell execution
6. Run npm audit — any high/critical vulnerabilities?
7. Review file_exists gate: still using the allowlist + safe regex?
8. Check output truncation limits — still applied at all injection points?

## Acceptance criteria
- npm audit shows 0 high/critical issues
- No new unprotected shell interpolation paths found
- All credential filter patterns still active
- Findings documented as comment

## Recurrence
Close and create a new instance before each release.

title: [REVIEW] Quarterly architecture review: layer boundaries and dependency health
description:
## Why
DDD architecture (domain → application → infrastructure → presentation) is only sound if actively maintained. Layer violations and dependency creep accumulate invisibly between releases. This review ensures architectural integrity is maintained.

## What (recurring — run every quarter or before each minor release)
1. Run dependency-cruiser: npm run depcruise — verify 0 violations
2. Run knip: npm run knip — verify 0 unused exports/files
3. Review all domain/ files for external imports (must be zero)
4. Check flow-complexity scores for any new files exceeding 4/5
5. Verify test co-location ratio is still >85%
6. Review any new ports/adapters: do they follow the naming convention?
7. Check for any new singletons or global state
8. Review CLAUDE.md DSL primitives section — is it still accurate?

## Acceptance criteria
- dependency-cruiser passes with 0 violations
- knip reports 0 unused symbols
- All domain files have 0 external imports
- Report filed as a comment on this issue with findings

## Recurrence
Close and create a new instance each quarter.

title: Recency-boost capture instructions: add active-capture reminder to flow rendering
description:
## Why
In long conversations (20+ turns), Claude's attention to early system instructions degrades. The capture format instruction gets buried. Adding a brief reminder in the flow visualization (injected every turn) reinforces the instruction at the right moment.

## What
In renderFlow() (render-flow.ts), when a node is in awaiting_capture state, append a one-liner reminder below the flow visualization:

  [Capture active: write response to .prompt-language/vars/{varName} using Write tool]

This costs ~15 tokens per turn when capture is active and ensures the instruction appears at the end of context (most attended by the model).

Key files: src/domain/render-flow.ts.

## Acceptance criteria
- Reminder line appears in render output when capture is active
- Reminder correctly shows variable name
- Only shown when awaiting_capture (not on every turn)
- Unit tests for render output with/without active capture

title: Add const keyword for immutable variable declarations
description:
## Why
Variables set once (API keys, config values, loop limits) can be accidentally overwritten by Claude in a loop body. A const keyword prevents reassignment and documents intent.

## What
Add const as a third alias alongside let/var. A const-declared variable cannot be reassigned. In updateVariable() (session-state.ts), check if the variable was declared const and emit a warning (or soft error) on reassignment attempt.

The parser recognizes 'const' in the same position as 'let'/'var'. LetNode gains a isConst: boolean field.

Key files: src/domain/flow-node.ts (LetNode), src/application/parse-flow.ts (parseLetLine), src/domain/session-state.ts (updateVariable).

## Acceptance criteria
- const parsed identically to let/var
- Reassignment of const warns at runtime
- Linter warns on const reassignment statically
- const included in flow rendering (shown as 'const' not 'let')
- Backward compatible

title: Add gradual typing for let declarations (let x: int = 0)
description:
## Why
All variables are stringly-typed. The condition evaluator does implicit numeric coercion — if count is 'pending', ${count} > 5 silently returns null and falls through to command execution. Type annotations let the parser and linter catch these mismatches statically.

## What
Add optional type annotations to let/var declarations:
  let count: int = 0
  let name: string = 'alice'
  let done: bool = false
  let items: list = []

Parse the ': type' after variable name in parseLetLine(). Store declaredType on LetNode. At runtime in updateVariable(), validate assigned value matches declared type (warn on mismatch, don't hard-fail for backward compat). In the linter, warn when comparison operators are used on string-typed variables, or when a list variable is used in a numeric comparison.

Key files: src/domain/flow-node.ts (LetNode), src/application/parse-flow.ts (parseLetLine), src/domain/session-state.ts (updateVariable), src/domain/lint-flow.ts.

## Acceptance criteria
- Type annotations parsed and stored on LetNode
- Runtime validation warns on type mismatch
- Linter warns on comparison operators with declared string type
- Fully backward compatible (untyped variables behave as before)
