- Unit tests for parsing and validation

title: Add runtime invariant assertions to catch state machine corruption early
description:
## Why
State corruption manifests as bizarre behavior far from the corruption site. Runtime invariant checks after each state transition would catch corruption immediately when it happens, with a useful error message.

## What
Implement assertStateInvariants(state: SessionState): void as a pure domain function. Invariants to check:
1. path resolves to a valid node or is one-past-end
2. nodeProgress iteration <= maxIterations for all nodes
3. status === 'active' implies path.length >= 1
4. setExitVariables values are consistent (command_failed != command_succeeded)
5. transitionSeq is present and non-negative
6. No variable name contains the capture nonce substring

Call assertStateInvariants() in autoAdvanceNodes() after each node transition. In production: log violations. In test (NODE_ENV=test): throw.

Key files: src/domain/session-state.ts or new src/domain/assert-invariants.ts, src/application/advance-flow.ts.

## Acceptance criteria
- Pure domain function with zero external deps
- All 6 invariants checked
- Called after each transition in autoAdvanceNodes
- Throws in test environment, logs in production
- Unit tests for each invariant violation scenario

title: Add variable shadow warnings to linter for foreach and nested scopes
description:
## Why
Variables in prompt-language are global. A foreach iterator variable named 'item' inside a foreach 'item' silently shadows the outer variable. This causes subtle bugs that are invisible at runtime — the outer loop's iterator gets corrupted. The linter can catch this statically.

## What
In lintFlow() / lintNodes(), track a scope stack as the linter descends into loop bodies and branches. When a let or foreach declares a variable name that already exists in an outer scope, emit a warning: 'Variable X shadows outer definition in foreach/while/if'.

Also: after foreach body exhaustion (handleBodyExhaustion), clean up the foreach iterator variable from SessionState to prevent stale values leaking after the loop.

Key files: src/domain/lint-flow.ts, src/application/advance-flow.ts (handleBodyExhaustion).

## Acceptance criteria
- Linter warns on variable shadowing in nested scopes
- Warning message includes variable name and scope type
- Foreach iterator cleaned from state after loop completes
- No false positives on non-shadowing variable reuse

title: Scan full Claude response text for capture tags (not just tool output)
description:
## Why
The post-tool-use hook currently only scans toolOutput for capture tags. Claude sometimes emits the capture tag content in its regular text response rather than (or in addition to) tool output. This valid capture attempt is silently ignored, causing unnecessary retry.

## What
In post-tool-use.ts scanAndSaveCapturedVars(), also scan the response_text field (if present in stdin JSON) for capture tag patterns. Accept capture from either channel. If both have values, prefer the file-write channel (more structured). This is a low-cost extension of the existing multi-channel approach.

Key files: src/presentation/hooks/post-tool-use.ts (scanAndSaveCapturedVars).

## Acceptance criteria
- Response text scanned for capture tags alongside tool output
- Both channels checked, file-write takes precedence
- No regression on existing capture tests

title: Pre-create capture file with sentinel value to enable targeted retry diagnostics
description:
## Why
Currently FileCaptureReader.read() returns null for both 'file not found' (Claude ignored the Write instruction entirely) and 'file empty' (Claude wrote but wrote nothing). These are different failure modes requiring different retry strategies. Better diagnostics enable smarter retry prompts.

## What
Before emitting a capture prompt (advanceLetPrompt Phase 1), after captureReader.clear(), write a sentinel value __PENDING__ to the file. In Phase 2, distinguish:
- File has __PENDING__: Claude did not write to the capture file → emphasize Write tool instruction in retry
- File exists but empty: Claude wrote but wrote nothing → emphasize content requirement in retry
- File has content: success

Add prime(varName) method to CaptureReader port and FileCaptureReader adapter.

Key files: src/application/ports/capture-reader.ts, src/infrastructure/adapters/file-capture-reader.ts, src/application/advance-flow.ts (advanceLetPrompt).

## Acceptance criteria
- CaptureReader port has prime() method
- Sentinel written before each capture prompt
- Phase 2 distinguishes pending vs empty vs content
- Different retry messages for each failure mode

title: Add capture circuit breaker: detect systemic failures and switch to degraded mode
description:
## Why
When capture fails repeatedly, retrying the same broken format wastes turns and never recovers. Repeated failures indicate a systematic issue (model confusion, context pollution, nonce mismatch). A circuit breaker detects the pattern and adapts rather than looping forever.

## What
Track captureFailureCount in SessionState. After 5 cumulative capture failures in a session:
- Switch to simplified capture prompt (Write-tool-only, minimal text)
- Log a WARNING in the flow visualization

After 10 cumulative failures:
- Mark flow as failed with diagnostic message explaining the capture system is not working

The counter resets on successful capture.

Key files: src/domain/session-state.ts (add counter), src/application/advance-flow.ts (advanceLetPrompt), src/domain/capture-prompt.ts.

## Acceptance criteria
- captureFailureCount field in SessionState
- Degraded mode prompt at threshold 5
- Flow fails with diagnostic at threshold 10
- Counter resets on success

title: Implement tiered escalating retry prompts for capture failures
description:
## Why
The current capture retry (buildCaptureRetryPrompt) sends essentially the same instruction rephrased. Research shows escalating specificity is more effective than repetition. Repeating a broken format won't fix it — the retry needs to change strategy.

## What
Implement a 3-tier retry strategy parameterized by attempt number:
- Tier 1 (current): rephrase the instruction
- Tier 2: include a concrete example of the expected output (show a Write tool call with example content)
- Tier 3 (last resort): strip all other context, issue a minimal focused prompt with only the capture instruction

buildCaptureRetryPrompt() gains an attemptNumber: number parameter. advanceLetPrompt() passes the attempt count.

Key files: src/domain/capture-prompt.ts (buildCaptureRetryPrompt), src/application/advance-flow.ts (advanceLetPrompt).

## Acceptance criteria
- buildCaptureRetryPrompt accepts attempt number 1-3
- Each tier produces meaningfully different prompt text
- Attempt count tracked in NodeProgress or passed through state
- All existing capture tests pass

title: Feature: review block for generator-evaluator debate loops
description:
## Why this issue exists

The Anthropic engineering blog (Mar 2026) demonstrated that generator-evaluator multi-agent architectures produce dramatically better results than solo agents — a working game vs a broken one. The pattern: generator produces work, evaluator critiques against explicit criteria, generator iterates, repeat until approval.

prompt-language's spawn/await supports this in principle but with poor ergonomics: requires separate flow files for each agent, no built-in retry-until-approved, implicit variable naming, verbose orchestration.

## What needs to be done

Add a first-class `review` block (or `evaluate` node) that expresses generator-evaluator loops concisely:

```
flow:
  retry max 5
    prompt: Implement feature X
    review
      criteria: "Has tests? Handles errors? Clean code?"
      grounded-by: npm test
    end
  end
```

The `review` block would:
1. Spawn a skeptical evaluator sub-agent with the criteria as its mandate
2. Give it Playwright/shell access to verify the work
3. Import its verdict (`approved`) and feedback (`critique`) as variables
4. If `approved == false`, inject `critique` into the retry loop's next iteration context
5. If `approved == true`, break out of the retry

Key design decisions:
- Evaluator is intentionally skeptical (prompted to be, unlike self-evaluation)
- Feedback is injected as context for the next generator turn
- The `grounded-by` command gives the evaluator objective ground truth (test results, screenshots)
- Criteria can be inline or referenced from a file

## Acceptance criteria
- Can express a generate-then-review loop in <10 lines of DSL
- Evaluator's critique is visible in flow render output
- Review block works inside retry/while/until loops
- Criteria prompt shapes evaluator behavior (skeptical by default)
- Grounded-by output is provided to evaluator as evidence
- All existing spawn/await tests still pass

title: Flow composition: `import` directive for reusable sub-flow files
description:
Why: Teams have common setup/teardown patterns (install deps, run linter, verify gates) that repeat across flows. Without composition, every flow duplicates boilerplate. Example: flows/setup.flow contains npm install + npm run build; every flow imports it.

What: `import 'path/to/sub.flow'` directive that inlines another flow file's nodes at parse time (static inclusion, source-to-source transform). Relative paths only. Same SAFE_PATH_RE as file_exists gates. Cycle detection required.

Example:
```
flow:
  import "flows/setup.flow"
  prompt: Now implement the feature
  import "flows/verify.flow"
```

Acceptance criteria:
- import directive resolved at parse time
- Cycle detection: A imports B imports A -> lint error
- Path traversal prevented by SAFE_PATH_RE
- Imported nodes get unique IDs (avoid collisions)
- Missing import file: lint error, not silent failure
close_reason: Implemented parse-time import with relative-path checks, cycle detection, and static expansion; validated via the libraries example.

title: MCP server: expose flow state as MCP resources for any MCP-capable AI client
description:
Why: MCP is the emerging standard for AI-tool integration. Any MCP-capable client (Claude Desktop, Cursor, Windsurf) could query and control prompt-language flows without being a Claude Code plugin. This decouples prompt-language from Claude Code's plugin system entirely.

What: Lightweight MCP server (@45ck/prompt-language-mcp) exposing:
- Resources: flow://state, flow://variables, flow://gates, flow://audit
- Tools: flow_reset, flow_set_variable, flow_status

Server reads from .prompt-language/session-state.json — no new state storage.

Note: Requires Programmatic API (public SDK exports) as prerequisite.

Acceptance criteria:
- MCP server installable separately: npm i @45ck/prompt-language-mcp
- flow://state returns current SessionState as JSON
- flow://variables returns variable map with full values
- flow_status tool returns renderFlow() output
- Works in Claude Desktop and any MCP-capable client

title: Webhook notifications: notify Slack/Discord/HTTP on flow completion/failure
description:
Why: Teams need visibility into unattended flow runs. When a developer kicks off a 10-minute flow and switches tasks, they need a Slack ping when it completes or fails.

What: Optional notify: section in DSL or .prompt-language/config.json:
```
notify:
  on_complete: https://hooks.slack.com/services/...
  on_fail: https://hooks.slack.com/services/...
```

In stop.ts hook (which already detects completion/failure), send HTTP POST with payload: {goal, status, duration, gate_results}. Uses node:https (no deps). Slack-compatible format.

Acceptance criteria:
- on_complete fires on flow completion
- on_fail fires on flow failure
- Payload includes goal, status, duration, gate pass/fail summary
- Uses node:https with no new dependencies
- Webhook errors don't fail the flow

title: Flow registry: establish .flow file convention and CLI run/validate commands
description:
Why: Flows are ephemeral — pasted into prompts and lost. A file convention makes flows versionable, shareable, reviewable in PRs, and composable. This is the foundation for a flow ecosystem.

What: .flow file convention with standard header. CLI commands:
- npx @45ck/prompt-language run my-task.flow
- npx @45ck/prompt-language validate my-task.flow (parse + lint + dry-run)
- npx @45ck/prompt-language list (list .flow files in cwd)

Convention: flows/ directory in project root for team-shared flows.

Acceptance criteria:
- .flow files parsed correctly with same parser as inline flows
- validate command exits non-zero on lint errors
- run command executes flow via claude -p
- list command finds all .flow files recursively

title: GitHub Actions integration: 45ck/prompt-language-action
description:
Why: CI/CD is the #1 path to production adoption. Teams want 'every PR automatically runs a prompt-language flow that fixes lint, runs tests, and verifies gates.' Without this, adoption is limited to interactive developer usage.

What: GitHub Action that runs npx @45ck/prompt-language ci --file <flow> inside a workflow step. Inputs: flow-file, api-key (secret), timeout (default 10min). Outputs: status (completed/failed), gate-results (JSON), audit-log (artifact).

Note: The existing cli.mjs ci command handles headless execution — the Action is a wrapper adding Node setup, install, and run.

Example workflow step:
```yaml
- uses: 45ck/prompt-language-action@v1
  with:
    flow-file: flows/fix-and-test.flow
    api-key: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
```

Acceptance criteria:
- action.yml with correct inputs/outputs
- Dockerfile packages Node + claude-code + prompt-language
- Gate failures produce failed check (exit 1)
- Audit log uploaded as artifact

title: State snapshot/replay: /flow-snapshot to save and restore named flow state
description:
Why: When a flow fails at step 10 of 15, developers must re-run the entire flow from scratch. With snapshots, they can save state at step 8, experiment with different approaches for steps 9-10, and restore if needed. Dramatically reduces iteration time for long flows.

What: (a) snapshotState() saves a named copy of current state to .prompt-language/snapshots/. (b) restoreSnapshot() loads a snapshot as current state. (c) /flow-snapshot create|list|restore skill.

Acceptance criteria:
- /flow-snapshot create <name> saves current state
- /flow-snapshot list shows all snapshots with timestamps
- /flow-snapshot restore <name> restores without affecting other state
- SHA-256 checksum verified on restore
- Max 10 snapshots (FIFO eviction)

title: Flow progress notifications: log iteration/branch events to stderr during execution
description:
Why: For long-running flows with loops, the user sees nothing between the initial render and the next prompt pause. If a while tests_fail max 10 loop runs for 5 minutes, there's no feedback about whether progress is being made.

What: Add [PL:progress] stderr messages at key milestones: loop iteration completion ('retry 3/5 completed'), branch taken ('if command_failed: took else branch'), flow percentage ('step 8/12 [67%]'). Controlled by PROMPT_LANGUAGE_PROGRESS=1 env var.

Acceptance criteria:
- Loop iteration completion printed to stderr with iteration count
- Branch decisions printed: which branch was taken and why
- Flow percentage: steps completed / total steps
- No output when PROMPT_LANGUAGE_PROGRESS not set

title: Gate dry-run mode: `--check-gates` flag to verify gates without running the flow
description:
Why: The only way to test if tests_pass works is to run the entire flow. If a gate predicate is misspelled or the command fails for environment reasons, the developer doesn't find out until the flow reaches the end. Example: `done when: pytest_pass` when pytest isn't installed.

What: Add --check-gates flag to the dry-run CLI command. Actually runs all gate commands and reports their current status without starting or affecting the flow.

Acceptance criteria:
- npx @45ck/prompt-language dry-run --check-gates my.flow runs all gates and reports pass/fail
- Shows actual gate command output (stdout/stderr)
- Exits 0 if all gates would pass, 1 if any would fail
- Works without an active session (standalone gate check)

title: Structured debug mode: expand PROMPT_LANGUAGE_DEBUG to 3 verbosity levels
description:
Why: Current debug output is minimal (~5 messages across all hooks). When something goes wrong, developers have zero visibility into why a condition evaluated a certain way, why a node didn't advance, or why a capture failed.

What: Expand PROMPT_LANGUAGE_DEBUG env var to 3 levels:
- Level 1 (current): hook entry/exit
- Level 2: + condition evaluation results, variable mutations, path transitions
- Level 3: + full node resolution, capture tag matching, gate command details

Structured prefixes: [PL:hook], [PL:advance], [PL:condition], [PL:gate], [PL:capture] for grep-based filtering.

Acceptance criteria:
- PROMPT_LANGUAGE_DEBUG=2 shows condition evaluation with intermediate values
- PROMPT_LANGUAGE_DEBUG=3 shows capture tag matching and gate command details
- Structured prefixes enable: 2>&1 | grep PL:condition
- No output when env var not set (no performance regression)

title: Add node execution timing to audit trail and flow render
description:
Why: Individual run: commands have no timing data in the visualization. When a flow takes 10 minutes, there's no way to know which commands are the bottleneck. Existing timingAnnotation() in render-flow.ts only shows timing for loop nodes.

What: Extend AuditEntry with nodeId, nodeKind, nodePath, and durationMs. Log timing for every node advancement. Add renderTimingReport() domain function with slowest-first table. Surface in /flow-status skill and completion summary.

Acceptance criteria:
- Every node advancement logged with durationMs
- /flow-status shows timing summary after flow completion
- renderTimingReport() produces sortable table by duration
- Flow completion banner includes 'slowest node: ...' hint if any node took >30s

title: Add /flow-eval skill: evaluate condition expressions against current variable state
description:
Why: When a while or if condition behaves unexpectedly, the only option is to read state files and mentally evaluate the condition. With compound conditions (a and b or c), left-to-right evaluation is especially confusing. Users need interactive condition testing.

What: New /flow-eval skill that evaluates a condition expression against current variable state. Shows intermediate values and evaluation steps.

Example: /flow-eval "${count} > 3 and command_succeeded" shows count=2, 2 > 3 = false, short-circuit: result = false

Acceptance criteria:
- Evaluates all condition types: comparisons, and/or, not, variable refs
- Shows intermediate values for each operand
- Works without an active flow (uses last session state)
- Clear error if variable is undefined

title: Checkpoint/approval gate: `approve "message"` node for human-in-the-loop hard gates
description:
Why: Human-in-the-loop is essential for high-stakes operations. The only current way to involve humans is via prompt: nodes, but those don't block on explicit approval — Claude can just proceed. An approve node is a hard gate requiring human confirmation.

What: New node `approve "message"` that pauses and requires explicit user approval. Flow blocks until user responds with 'yes'/'approved'/'y'. Rejection ('no'/'reject') enters configurable fallback. Optional timeout.

Example:
```
flow:
  prompt: Generate the migration SQL
  let sql = prompt "Show me the migration"
  approve "Ready to run this migration against prod?"
  run: psql -f migration.sql
```

Acceptance criteria:
- approve node pauses flow and displays message prominently
- Trivial-prompt detection for approval ('yes', 'y', 'approved', 'ok')
- Rejection detected and flow transitions to cancelled or catch block
- Optional timeout: approve ... timeout 5m
- Clear visual indicator in flow render

title: Conditional spawn: `spawn "name" if condition`
description:
Why: In parallel workflows, not all branches are always needed. Wrapping spawn in an if block is verbose and breaks the parallel spawn pattern.

What: Add optional inline condition to spawn nodes. Child is only launched if condition evaluates to true. Uses same evaluateFlowCondition() as if nodes.

Example:
```
spawn "fix-lint" if lint_fail
  prompt: Fix the lint errors
end
spawn "fix-tests" if tests_fail
  prompt: Fix the failing tests
end
await all
```

Acceptance criteria:
- if condition syntax parsed in spawn
- Condition false: spawn node advances past immediately (no child launched)
- Condition true: normal spawn behavior
- await all: only waits for launched children
- Backward compatible: spawn without if always launches

title: Structured output capture: `let x = prompt "..." as json { schema }`
description:
Why: Captured values are opaque strings. Any downstream logic that needs structured data (list of files, severity rating, boolean + explanation) must rely on fragile string parsing. Structured capture is the critical bridge between the 15% AI and 85% deterministic parts.

What: Extend let x = prompt to accept a JSON schema. The capture prompt instructs Claude to respond in JSON matching the schema. Runtime validates response, re-prompts on validation failure (up to DEFAULT_MAX_CAPTURE_RETRIES). Individual fields accessible via ${x.fieldName} dot-notation.

Example:
```
let analysis = prompt "Analyze failures" as json { severity: "low"|"medium"|"high", files: string[], summary: string }
if ${analysis.severity} == "high"
  prompt: Fix all ${analysis.files_length} files immediately
end
```

Acceptance criteria:
- as json schema syntax parsed correctly
- Capture prompt includes JSON instruction + schema
- JSON validation with retry on malformed response
- Dot-notation ${x.fieldName} works in interpolation
- Schema validation errors surface clearly in capture diagnostics

title: Tool-aware post-tool-use context injection: `on-tool` DSL section
description:
Why: The post-tool-use hook fires for all tools but can't inject targeted guidance. Tool-specific context is critical for reliability — after a Bash call, remind the AI to check exit codes; after Write, remind it to validate.

What: New `on-tool:` section in the DSL. When a specified tool fires post-use, inject associated text as additionalContext.

Example:
```
on-tool:
  Bash: "Remember to check exit codes and run tests after shell commands"
  Write: "Verify the written file matches the spec before continuing"
  Edit: "Run the test suite to verify your change didn't break anything"
```

Acceptance criteria:
- on-tool section parsed and stored in FlowSpec
- post-tool-use hook checks toolName against on-tool map
- Matching text injected as additionalContext
- No match: no injection (backward compatible)

title: Structured telemetry: log all state transitions and node lifecycle events to audit.jsonl
description:
Why: The audit log only captures command executions and gate evaluations. When a flow fails, there's no record of the control-flow decisions that led to it. Diagnosing 'why did my flow end up in failed state?' requires reverse-engineering from session-state.json.

What: Extend FileAuditLogger to log events for all state transitions: flow_started, flow_completed, flow_failed, node_entered {nodeId, kind}, node_completed {nodeId, durationMs}, loop_iteration {nodeId, iteration, max}, spawn_started, spawn_completed, capture_attempt, capture_success, capture_failed. Each event includes monotonic sequence number and timestamp.

Acceptance criteria:
- All state transitions produce audit events
- Events are structured JSONL with consistent schema
- Performance overhead is acceptable (<1ms per event)
- Existing audit log format is backward compatible

title: Spawn await: exponential backoff + jitter, wall-clock timeout
description:
Why: Current adaptive polling (1s/2s/5s/10s steps) has two issues: (1) multiple concurrent await nodes create synchronized polling storms, (2) total timeout is derived from poll count (150 * interval) making it hard to predict. With jitter, concurrent polls naturally desynchronize. With wall-clock timeout, behavior is predictable.

What: Replace computePollInterval step function with exponential backoff + jitter: min(base * 2^attempt + random(0, base), maxInterval). Add configurable wall-clock total timeout for await (default: 25 min).

Acceptance criteria:
- Multiple concurrent awaits don't poll in lockstep
- Total timeout is wall-clock based, not poll-count based
- Configurable via PROMPT_LANGUAGE_AWAIT_TIMEOUT env var
- Existing await tests still pass

title: State migration framework: version-based SessionState schema migrations
description:
Why: SessionState.version exists (always 1) but has no migration logic. Any future change to SessionState shape causes silent data loss or parse failures for users who upgrade the plugin mid-flow. This is critical for a plugin that persists state to disk.

What: Add migrateState(raw: unknown): SessionState in the domain layer. Pattern-match on version field, apply transformations. Call in FileStateStore.parseStateJson() after JSON.parse. Register as { 1: identity, 2: addCaptureNonce, ... }.

Acceptance criteria:
- Old version-1 state files load correctly via identity migration
- Any future schema change ships with a migration function
- Parse failures on unknown versions produce clear error with version info
- Domain-pure function (no external deps)

title: Dead flow reaper: auto-fail flows idle >30min or running >4h
description:
Why: A user might start a complex flow, walk away, and come back hours later. The flow is still 'active' but all context is lost. The stop hook blocks the user from completing any work. Users must manually run 'abort flow'. Stale flows should self-clean.

What: Add lastActivityAt timestamp to SessionState (updated on every state save). Add flowStartedAt. In inject-context.ts load path, check: if flow active and lastActivityAt > 30min ago — mark failed with reason 'Flow timed out: no activity for 30 minutes'. Configurable via PROMPT_LANGUAGE_IDLE_TIMEOUT env var.

Acceptance criteria:
- Idle flow (no activity 30min) auto-fails with clear message
- Total duration limit (4h default) also triggers auto-fail
- Both timeouts configurable via env vars
- Timeout does NOT trigger on compaction/resume (update lastActivityAt there)

title: Spawn PID liveness check: fail fast when spawned child crashes without updating state
description:
Why: A spawned claude -p process can crash (OOM, SIGKILL) without updating its state file. The parent then polls 150 times over ~25 minutes waiting for a dead process. With PID liveness checking, the parent detects the crash within one poll cycle.

What: In advanceAwaitNode(), before polling state file, check if child PID is still alive using process.kill(pid, 0). If PID is dead but state shows 'running' or state doesn't exist, immediately mark child as failed.

Acceptance criteria:
- Dead child process detected within one poll cycle
- Parent flow proceeds to catch/fail path immediately
- On Windows: skip PID check (same pattern as isLockStale)
- ~15 LOC change
close_reason: Closed: await-node PID liveness fast-fail behavior was already implemented and validated by regression tests.

title: Fix: generalize ${var} interpolation in condition expressions
description:
Why: Users naturally write `if ${flag}` expecting it to interpolate the variable value and evaluate truthiness. Instead it's treated as a literal variable name lookup for '${flag}' (which doesn't exist). The asymmetry — interpolation works in prompt/run but not in if/while/until conditions — is persistent confusion.

What: Add an interpolate() call at the top of evaluateCondition() before any parsing, so `if ${flag}` interpolates to the flag's value before condition evaluation. ~5 LOC, but needs careful testing to avoid double-resolution with resolveOperand.

Acceptance criteria:
- `if ${ready}` where ready='true' evaluates as truthy
- `while ${count} > 0` correctly interpolates count before comparison
- No regression on existing conditions that use ${var} in comparisons
close_reason: Fixed: evaluateCondition now interpolates ${var} directly, with regression tests for truthiness and numeric comparison.

title: Add multi-line string literals (triple-quote syntax)
description:
Why: There is no way to express a multi-line template as an inline literal. The only workarounds are file includes or let prompt (which requires AI participation). This is the most-requested missing syntax for prompt text, email templates, and configuration.

What: Triple-quote syntax for multi-line strings with dedent support.

Example:
```
let template = """
  Dear ${name},
  Your order ${order_id} is confirmed.
  """
```

Acceptance criteria:
- Triple-quote strings work in let, prompt, and run contexts
- Indentation is stripped to the level of the closing quotes
- Variable interpolation works inside triple-quote strings
- No existing DSL syntax uses triple quotes — pure addition

title: Add sub-flows / parameterized macros (`define` blocks)
description:
Why: Any repeated pattern must be copy-pasted. The include directive provides file-level reuse but is not parameterized. As flows grow, boilerplate duplication becomes painful and error-prone.

What: New `define` block that creates a reusable, parameterized flow fragment. Textual expansion (macro), not runtime call stack — parameters substituted at parse time before parseBlock.

Example:
```
define fix-and-test(file, test_cmd)
  prompt: Fix ${file}
  run: ${test_cmd}
  if command_failed
    prompt: Try again — tests still failing
  end
end

flow:
  fix-and-test("app.js", "npm test")
  fix-and-test("utils.js", "npm test")
```

Acceptance criteria:
- define blocks expand inline at parse time
- Parameters are substituted correctly
- Cycle detection prevents recursive macros
- Undefined macro invocation produces lint error

title: Add pattern match / switch construct (desugars to if/elif/else)
description:
Why: Multi-way branching with if/elif/elif/.../else is verbose and error-prone when switching on a single variable value. This is common after `let status = run '...'`.

What: Parser sugar only — desugar to nested if/else at parse time. ~60-80 LOC.

Example:
```
match ${status}
  case "success"
    prompt: Great, it worked!
  case "retry"
    prompt: Let's try again
  default
    prompt: Unexpected status: ${status}
end
```

Acceptance criteria:
- match/case/default desugar to if/elif/else correctly
- match and case are not existing keywords — pure addition
- Works with variable and literal value matching
- Lint warns on unreachable cases

title: Attach source line numbers to runtime errors (node ID -> line number mapping)
description:
Why: Runtime errors from advance-flow.ts reference node IDs like n7 which are meaningless to users. When a flow fails at node n7, users have to mentally map it back to their DSL source. Source locations are table stakes for any language with real complexity.

What: Add `line: number` to BaseNode. Parser already tracks ctx.pos — propagate it to each created node. Surface line numbers in markFailed(), addWarning(), and the flow completion banner. Add describePosition() function that translates path arrays to human-readable breadcrumbs like 'retry(2/3) > if command_failed > prompt'.

Acceptance criteria:
- Error messages include source line number
- Flow completion banner shows human-readable position, not path indices
- describePosition() works for all node kinds including nested

title: Add `return` node for early flow completion
description:
Why: The only way to end a flow early is to let the path advance past all nodes. There is no way to express 'if the precondition is already met, skip the rest.' Users work around this with deeply nested if blocks. `return` is the control-flow complement to `break` (which exits loops).

What: New node kind `return` that immediately marks the flow as completed and exits the auto-advance loop. Parser recognizes the keyword, advanceSingleNode calls markCompleted(). ~40-60 LOC.

Example:
```
flow:
  let result = run "check.sh"
  if command_succeeded
    return
  end
  prompt: Fix the issue
```

Acceptance criteria:
- return immediately completes the flow
- Works inside if blocks, try bodies, foreach loops
- Lint warns if return appears outside a flow body

title: Lint: warn on variable shadowing in nested scopes (foreach loop var shadows outer let)
description:
Why: Without lexical scoping, nested foreach loops that use the same iterator name silently clobber each other. The linter already checks for undefined variables — it should also warn on unintentional redefinition. Example: `let x = 'hello'` followed by `foreach x in '1 2 3'` should warn that x shadows an outer variable.

What: Extend collectDefinedVariables() in lint-flow.ts to track first-definition site. Warn on re-definition in nested scope context. ~30-50 LOC.

Acceptance criteria:
- foreach loop variable that shadows an outer let produces a lint warning
- Warning includes both definition sites for context
- No false positives on intentional top-level reassignment

title: Parameterize eval smoke tests for multiple AI harnesses
description: The smoke test runner (scripts/eval/smoke-test.mjs) is hardcoded to claude -p. To support Gemini CLI (gemini -p --yolo), Codex CLI (codex exec --full-auto), and OpenCode (opencode run), the runner needs an --harness flag or AI_CMD env var that swaps out the AI invocation command. Output parsing may also differ. This enables cross-harness evals to measure how well each AI follows the DSL before committing to a full port.

Complexity: Medium. Impact: High.

title: Tooling visibility for lowered swarm execution and active role state
description:
## Why
A macro feature is only maintainable if developers can inspect the expanded flow and understand live role state from CLI output.

## What
1. Add lowered-flow preview support such as `validate --expand` or equivalent.
2. Expose active role and failure state clearly in CLI/status output.
3. Keep debugging surfaces aligned with the lowered runtime, not a hidden swarm abstraction.
acceptance_criteria:
- There is an inspectable lowered-flow view for swarm programs.
- CLI/status output makes active roles and failures understandable.
- Tooling docs explain swarm as lowered orchestration, not a separate engine.
notes: Anchor: docs/wip/swarm/06-implementation-plan.md.

title: P5 provider adapters over shared Flow IR
description:
## Why
The imported pack argues that the architecture should outlive any single host integration. Provider adapters and typed tool boundaries are the portability layer on top of the shared IR.

## What
1. Define a provider-agnostic runtime boundary over Flow IR.
2. Separate provider-specific behavior from the language core.
3. Clarify how existing MCP, LSP, and playground backlog should relate to this portability layer rather than lead it.
acceptance_criteria:
- Provider adapters are explicitly blocked on the shared IR and earlier trust/replay work.
- The portability goal is architectural, not just another integration surface.
- Relationship to MCP/LSP/playground backlog is documented.
notes:
Anchors: docs/wip/vnext/specs/010-provider-adapters-and-flow-ir.md and adrs/ADR-006-provider-agnostic-architecture.md. Related existing issues: prompt-language-folr, prompt-language-idbc, prompt-language-528q.
2026-04-11 ecosystem pack: compile-target and source-vs-runtime separation ideas are reinforced by GitHub Agentic Workflows and Doctrine. Treat these as architectural portability inputs, not a reason to widen the current shipped surface.

3. Treat README/guide/UI changes as post-evidence rollout work, not the first implementation slice.
acceptance_criteria:

- Any future user-facing claims are gated on shipped behavior and evidence.
- Host/plugin alternatives are considered before expanding the DSL surface.

title: Decision: retrieval boundary and trust model for Markdown-backed knowledge
description:
## Why
The new docs argue for a split between deterministic retrieval for control paths and softer grounding retrieval for context gathering, plus an explicit trust model for Markdown-backed knowledge. There is no current backlog item that resolves that boundary.

## What
1. Define deterministic retrieval modes versus grounding retrieval modes.
2. Define the trust levels for policy, project, user, and run-scoped knowledge/memory sources.
3. Decide what, if anything, should become first-class DSL surface now versus adapter/config work later.
4. File follow-up implementation beads only for the bounded first slice.
acceptance_criteria:
- Retrieval split is explicit: deterministic versus grounding.
- Trust model for Markdown-backed knowledge is documented.
- The first bounded implementation slice is named, and deferred items stay deferred.
- Follow-up beads exist only for approved near-term work.
notes: Planning anchors: docs/wip/memory/markdown-agent-memory.md and docs/wip/memory/memory-source-notes.md.

Keep this issue open only for the narrower question of whether true external child-session lifecycle via codex exec is still needed beyond the now-working headless path.

title: Codex CLI: SessionStart and Bash-only tool hook adapters
description: Add the remaining Codex hook adapters that are practical today: `SessionStart` for startup/resume context, `PostToolUse` for Bash-only flow rendering and diagnostics, and optional `PreToolUse` for shell-policy enforcement. The implementation must explicitly degrade features that currently rely on Claude `Write`/`Edit`/`NotebookEdit` events or `PreCompact`, because Codex only emits `Bash` for tool hooks today and has no compaction hook event.
notes: Backlog host integration work. Revisit after the core Claude-first prompt-language surface is complete.
close_reason: Practical Codex SessionStart and Bash-only PostToolUse adapters are now implemented and wired.

title: Codex CLI: Stop hook merges flow blocking and gate evaluation
description: Implement the Codex `Stop` hook as the enforcement engine. This hook must combine `evaluateStop()` and `evaluateCompletion()` so it can both prevent premature flow exit and run completion gates when the agent attempts to finish. When work remains or gates fail, return a continuation/block decision with the next-step reason so Codex keeps going. Preserve the existing stop-loop guard (`stop_hook_active`) and completion/failure banners where possible. This task replaces Claude''s separate `TaskCompleted` hard gate with Codex''s continuation semantics.
notes: Backlog host integration work. Revisit after the core Claude-first prompt-language surface is complete.
close_reason: Codex Stop hook shipped as the enforcement surface that merges flow blocking and completion evaluation.

title: Codex CLI: UserPromptSubmit adapter via additionalContext
description: Write the Codex `UserPromptSubmit` adapter. Reuse `injectContext()` but translate its output into Codex hook output. Because Codex does not currently support prompt replacement, the adapter must emit `hookSpecificOutput.additionalContext` (and block when needed) instead of returning a rewritten prompt object. If the current application contract assumes prompt mutation, refactor it so the shared logic can produce either a prompt replacement (Claude) or developer-context injection (Codex) without forking flow semantics.
notes: Backlog host integration work. Revisit after the core Claude-first prompt-language surface is complete.
close_reason: Codex UserPromptSubmit adapter shipped through additionalContext output on the Codex hook surface.

title: Codex CLI: scaffold plugin, marketplace, hooks.json, and installer
description: Create the Codex packaging surface alongside the existing Claude plugin: `.codex-plugin/plugin.json`, marketplace metadata under `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json`, repo-local or personal install path decisions, and CLI install/status/uninstall support. Also add `.codex/hooks.json` registration and feature-flag enablement guidance for `codex_hooks`. The goal is a repeatable local install story that mirrors the current Claude installer as closely as Codex allows.
notes: Backlog host integration work. Revisit after the core Claude-first prompt-language surface is complete.
close_reason: Codex scaffold/install surface shipped with plugin metadata, hooks.json, marketplace wiring, and CLI install/status/uninstall support.

title: Port prompt-language to Codex CLI
description: Codex CLI now has hooks, plugins, skills, and a non-interactive `codex exec` mode, so prompt-language can be ported with high reuse across domain and application layers. The realistic target is near-Claude parity on macOS/Linux/WSL, not exact parity. Codex-specific gaps from the current March 28, 2026 docs: no `TaskCompleted` event, no `PreCompact` event, `UserPromptSubmit` adds `additionalContext` instead of replacing the prompt, and `PreToolUse`/`PostToolUse` currently only emit `Bash`. Hooks are still experimental and currently disabled on native Windows. Gate enforcement therefore needs to move into `Stop`, prompt injection needs a Codex-specific adapter, and write/edit-driven UX needs a Bash-only fallback. Parent epic tracking the full Codex CLI port.
notes:
Backlog for later host integration work. Not core prompt-language runtime work until the Claude-first surface is complete.
2026-04-10: Codex integration is no longer just later host-integration planning. Scaffold/install support, hook adapters, and headless `run/ci --runner codex` flow execution are now real. Remaining open scope is Codex-backed spawn/await and supported-host smoke/support-matrix evidence.

title: Guided configuration UX for harnesses, profiles, agents, and flow-file launch
description:
Why: Manually editing config files for harnesses, profiles, agents, and file-association settings is too much friction. There should be a guided setup path.
What:
- Add a guided configuration command or skill that helps users declare preferred harness, profiles, named agents, and file-launch behavior in natural language
- Generate or update project config safely rather than asking users to hand-edit JSON/YAML
- Decide whether this belongs as a CLI command, a skill, or both
Acceptance criteria:
- Guided setup path proposed and implemented in one supported entry point
- Resulting config is validated before write
- Existing config is merged safely, not overwritten blindly
- Docs show both guided setup and manual config path
- UX works for first-run and reconfiguration scenarios

title: Desktop integration: installer and OS file association for flow-file launch
description:
Why: If .pl becomes a first-class artifact, users will want frictionless OS-level launch behavior instead of manually pasting flows into a harness every time.
What:
- Installer support for file association / launcher registration on Windows, macOS, and Linux where practical
- Define safe launch behavior: double-click should run through the configured harness/runner, not bypass project guardrails
- Provide uninstall/rollback path for file association changes
- Document platform-specific constraints and consent prompts
Acceptance criteria:
- Cross-platform installer plan documented
- Windows file association path specified and tested
- macOS/Linux behavior documented, with implementation where feasible
- User can opt in/out of file association changes during install
- No weakening of safety hooks or quality-gate enforcement during launcher execution

title: [QA] Flakiness regression tracking: instrument smoke tests with pass/fail history
description:
## Why
Known-flaky smoke tests (S, T, Y, V) are tracked in MEMORY.md but with no historical data. Is T getting more or less flaky over time? Did the capture protocol changes in Phase 4 improve or worsen flakiness? Without tracking, we can't measure progress.

## What
Extend smoke-test.mjs to:
1. Append results to scripts/eval/results/history.jsonl (already gitignored via scripts/eval/results/)
2. Format: {date, testId, testName, passed, durationMs, attempt}
3. Add a summarize command: node scripts/eval/smoke-test.mjs --history to show pass rates over last N runs
4. Track consecutive failure streaks per test
5. Optionally: generate a markdown report showing flakiness trends

This is local-only (gitignored). Helps maintainer track whether reliability is improving.

## Acceptance criteria
- History appended to results/history.jsonl on each run
- --history flag shows per-test pass rates
- No change to existing smoke test behavior or output
close_reason: Implemented history.jsonl tracking and --history stability reporting for smoke tests; verified with local report commands and repo QA.

title: [QA] Add property-based tests for the DSL parser (fuzz parseFlow)
description:
## Why
The parser (parse-flow.ts) is an ad-hoc regex + state machine. It handles malformed input via soft-fail (warns not errors). Property-based testing with random/adversarial inputs will surface edge cases that unit tests miss — especially around indentation handling, special characters in strings, and deeply nested structures.

## What
Use fast-check (already a common vitest companion) to generate:
1. Random valid flow strings constructed from grammar rules — parse must not throw
2. Random indentation variations — parse must produce consistent structure
3. Strings with shell special chars in prompts (quotes, backticks, $, !) — parse must not corrupt
4. Deeply nested flows (10+ levels) — parse must not stack overflow
5. Very long variable names and values — parse must truncate or warn gracefully
6. Unicode in prompt text — parse must handle correctly

Add as parse-flow.property.test.ts alongside existing parse-flow tests.

## Acceptance criteria
- At least 5 property-based test suites implemented
- All pass with 1000 generated examples
- Any failures become regression unit tests

title: [REVIEW] Hook performance baseline: measure and track latency per hook
description:
## Why
Hooks add latency to every Claude Code turn. UserPromptSubmit blocks the response until it completes (60s timeout). If hook processing time grows, the developer experience degrades. Performance regressions should be caught before users notice.

## What (recurring — run before each release)
1. Instrument each hook's wall-clock time (hook entry to exit)
2. Baseline measurements: UserPromptSubmit (state load + autoAdvanceNodes + save), TaskCompleted (gate evaluation), Stop (gate status check)
3. Acceptable thresholds: UserPromptSubmit <500ms for typical 10-node flow, TaskCompleted <2s per gate
4. If any hook exceeds threshold, profile and identify bottleneck
5. State file load/save should be <50ms on typical hardware

## Acceptance criteria
- Baseline measurements documented
- Any above-threshold hooks have profiling results
- Findings documented as comment

## Recurrence
Close and create a new instance before each release.

title: Add ask-condition progressive backoff: inject evidence and simplify on retry
description:
## Why
When an ask condition gives an inconsistent verdict (the main ask flakiness source), the system currently has no adaptive behavior — it immediately re-asks with the same prompt. Progressive backoff with evidence injection improves accuracy on retry.

## What
Track consecutive ask failures for a node in NodeProgress. On retry:
- Attempt 1: Re-ask with previous verdict and grounded-by output as additional context
- Attempt 2: Switch to simplified yes/no forced-choice format with explicit binary instructions
- Attempt 3: Default to conservative answer (false for while-ask, true for until-ask), emit WARNING

Key files: src/application/advance-flow.ts (advanceConditionLoop), src/domain/judge-prompt.ts (new retry prompt builders).

## Acceptance criteria
- Consecutive failure count tracked in NodeProgress
- Three retry tiers implemented with distinct prompts
- Conservative default applied at tier 3 with logged warning
- Unit tests for each retry tier
- Smoke tests with ask conditions pass

title: Add opt-in multi-sample voting for ask conditions (ask '...' vote 3)
description:
## Why
For high-stakes ask conditions (e.g., 'is this safe to deploy?'), a single Claude judgment has ~10-15% error rate. Multi-sample voting reduces error rate from p to 3p^2 - 2p^3 — for 10% base rate, drops to ~2.8%.

## What
Add optional 'vote N' suffix to ask conditions:
  while ask 'is the code production-ready?' vote 3

This emits N separate judge prompts in sequence, each writing to __judge_{nodeId}_1__, _2, _3. Takes majority vote. N must be odd. Default behavior (no vote) unchanged — single judgment.

Key files: src/application/parse-flow.ts (parse 'vote N'), src/domain/flow-node.ts (add voteCount to ConditionLoopNode/IfNode), src/application/advance-flow.ts (advanceConditionLoop), src/domain/judge-prompt.ts.

## Acceptance criteria
- 'vote N' syntax parsed and stored
- N judge prompts emitted sequentially
- Majority vote taken correctly
- N must be odd (lint error if even)
- Adds N turns overhead (documented tradeoff)

title: Extend audit logger with capture, condition, and judgment events for replay debugging
Add Write-Ahead Log (WAL) for state transitions to enable crash recovery
description:
## Why
autoAdvanceNodes() can execute multiple run: commands (with real side effects) before saving state. If the process crashes mid-loop, side effects happened but state reverts to pre-advance. This is the classic split-brain: reality and state diverge. The WAL makes each transition individually recoverable.

## What
Before any state mutation in autoAdvanceNodes(), append a WAL entry to .prompt-language/wal.jsonl with: {seq, timestamp, prevPath, newPath, nodeKind, variables_delta, status}. On loadCurrent(), if a WAL entry exists without a corresponding committed state (seq > last committed seq), replay it to reconstruct the correct state.

New domain type: WalEntry. New port: WalWriter. New adapter: FileWalWriter. Integration in advanceSingleNode() (write entry) and FileStateStore.readCurrent() (check and replay).

Key files: new src/application/ports/wal-writer.ts, new src/infrastructure/adapters/file-wal-writer.ts, src/application/advance-flow.ts, src/infrastructure/adapters/file-state-store.ts.

## Acceptance criteria
- WAL entry written before each node transition
- On load, uncommitted WAL entries replayed
- WAL compacted after successful save
- No regression on existing state persistence tests
- Crash simulation test: state recovers correctly after mid-loop crash

title: Implement foreach capture batching to fix let-append-prompt inside loops
description:
## Why
'let x += prompt text' inside foreach is the highest-flakiness pattern. If each capture has 90% reliability, 5 iterations drops overall reliability to 59% (0.9^5). Each iteration requires a full capture cycle.

## What
When the system detects 'let x += prompt' inside a foreach body, batch the capture: emit a single prompt listing all N items, ask Claude to respond to each, write all responses as a newline-separated list to the capture file. This converts N sequential capture cycles into 1.

Implementation requires:
- Detecting the foreach+append-capture pattern at advancement time
- Building a batched prompt from all remaining iteration items
- Parsing the multi-line response and splitting into individual list entries
- Advancing past all iterations in one step

This is a significant change to advanceForeachEntry() and capture semantics. Only viable when prompts are independent across iterations (no per-item state dependency).

Key files: src/application/advance-flow.ts (advanceForeachEntry, advanceLetPrompt), src/domain/capture-prompt.ts.

## Acceptance criteria
- Foreach+append-capture pattern detected
- Batched prompt emitted for independent iterations
- Multi-line response correctly split and appended
- Falls back to sequential capture when iterations have dependencies
- Smoke test T (list accumulation in foreach) passes consistently

title: Add condition type checking to linter (warn on comparison with wrong types)
description:
## Why
Conditions like ${name} > 5 fail silently when name is a string. The condition evaluator returns null (unknown) and falls through to command execution which also fails. This is one of the most confusing runtime behaviors — it silently does nothing.

## What
In lintFlow(), add a condition type checker:
- Parse condition expressions at lint time
- If a ${var} reference with >, <, >=, <= has a declared string type (from let name: string), warn
- If a variable initialized with a string literal (let name = 'alice') is used with comparison operators, warn
- If both sides of == are string literals, info-level note about string equality

Reuse extractVarRefs() and collectDefinedVariables() from the existing linter.

Key files: src/domain/lint-flow.ts.

## Acceptance criteria
- Warns when string-typed var used with >, <, >=, <=
- Warns when string-literal-initialized var used with numeric comparison
- No false positives on numeric-initialized vars
- Works with gradual typing annotations when implemented

title: Recipe: multi-agent debate pattern using spawn/await
description:
## Why this issue exists

The generator-evaluator pattern from the Anthropic harness blog is achievable today with spawn/await, but there's no documentation or formula showing users how to do it. This creates friction for the most powerful multi-agent use case.

## What needs to be done

Add a `bd formula` template and a docs/examples entry showing the debate pattern with current DSL:

```
# generator.flow
flow:
  let feedback = ${feedback:-No feedback yet}
  prompt: Build feature X. Previous feedback: ${feedback}

# evaluator.flow  
flow:
  let work_summary = ${work_summary}
  run: npm test
  ask "Is the implementation complete and correct?"
    grounded-by: npm test
  let approved = ${__judge_....__}
  let critique = prompt "What specifically needs improvement?"

# orchestrator.flow
flow:
  let approved = false
  while not ${approved} == true max 5
    spawn "generator"
    await "generator"
    spawn "evaluator"
    await "evaluator"
    let approved = ${evaluator.approved}
    let feedback = ${evaluator.critique}
  end
```

Key things to document:
- Variables flow parent→child automatically on spawn
- Child output variables are imported with name prefix (evaluator.approved)
- Feedback variable from evaluator becomes input to next generator spawn
- Skeptical evaluator prompting (via evaluator's flow prompt text)

Also document the limitations today vs what prompt-language-s6zz would add.

title: PR status checks: post flow gate results as GitHub Checks API entry
description:
Why: PR checks are the standard way teams enforce quality gates. A prompt-language check showing 'tests_pass [pass], lint_pass [fail]' directly on the PR is immediately actionable.

What: After flow runs in CI (via GitHub Action), post a GitHub Check with: name (prompt-language / flow-name), status (success/failure based on gates), details (rendered flow visualization + gate diagnostics). Uses gh CLI or @octokit/rest.

Acceptance criteria:
- Check posted to correct commit SHA
- Gate results shown in check details
- Failed gates: check fails, PR blocked
- Passed gates: check passes, PR unblocked
- Depends on GitHub Actions integration

title: Secrets management: resolve credentials from env/1Password/Vault at flow start
description:
Why: Production flows need credentials (API keys, database passwords). Current approach (env vars) works but is fragile and unauditable. Vault integration adds security and auditability.

What: secrets: section in flow spec or .prompt-language/config.json:
```yaml
secrets:
  API_KEY:
    source: env  # from environment variable
  DB_PASSWORD:
    source: op   # 1Password: op read 'op://vault/item/field'
```

Resolved secrets injected into run: node environments (NOT into variables — excluded from state and audit logs).

Acceptance criteria:
- env source reads from process environment
- op source spawns 1Password CLI (op read)
- Secrets never written to state file or audit log
- filterSpawnVariables() prevents credential leak to children
- 1Password CLI not required (only if op source used)

title: Docker containerized execution: Dockerfile + `npx @45ck/prompt-language docker` command
description:
Why: Containerized execution provides reproducibility and isolation. CI/CD pipelines and team environments benefit from a hermetic execution environment.

What: Dockerfile packaging Node + claude-code + prompt-language. CLI command: npx @45ck/prompt-language docker --file my.flow that builds and runs the container.

Example:
```
docker run -e ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=... -v ./:/app prompt-language ci /app/fix-tests.flow
```

Acceptance criteria:
- Dockerfile in repository root
- docker command builds and runs hermetically
- API key passed via environment variable
- Published to Docker Hub as 45ck/prompt-language:latest

title: CI status badge for README: `[![flow: passing]` via shields.io
description:
Why: Badges are viral social proof. Every README with a 'flow: passing' badge advertises prompt-language to visitors. Low effort, outsized awareness.

What: The GitHub Action writes gate results to a gist or repo file after each run. Badge URL uses shields.io endpoint JSON format. README snippet generated by the Action.

Example:
[![prompt-language](https://img.shields.io/badge/prompt--language-passing-brightgreen)](https://github.com/45ck/prompt-language)

Acceptance criteria:
- GitHub Action writes badge JSON to configurable location
- Badge reflects last gate evaluation result
- README snippet auto-generated with correct URL
- Depends on GitHub Actions integration

title: Metrics & telemetry port: aggregate flow execution analytics across runs
description:
Why: Teams running flows at scale need to answer 'which gates are flaky?', 'how long do flows take on average?', 'which nodes retry most?' Without metrics, prompt-language is a black box.

What: MetricsCollector port (application layer) aggregating: flow duration, gate pass/fail rates, node execution counts, retry frequency, capture success rate. Default adapter: .prompt-language/metrics.jsonl. Optional: CLI command npx @45ck/prompt-language metrics --format prometheus.

Acceptance criteria:
- Per-run metrics written to metrics.jsonl after each flow completion
- npx @45ck/prompt-language metrics shows aggregated stats across runs
- --format prometheus outputs Prometheus-compatible metrics
- Port/adapter pattern: easy to swap for external collectors

title: Interactive state editor: /flow-set skill to modify variables and gates without editing JSON
description:
Why: Debugging often requires tweaking state: set a variable to test a different code path, mark a gate as passed to unblock completion, jump to a specific node. Currently requires manually editing session-state.json with exact JSON structure and correct SHA-256 checksum.

What: /flow-set skill: /flow-set count 5, /flow-set --gate tests_pass true, /flow-set --path 0.1. Safety checks: warn before overwriting, validate path exists, update checksum automatically.

Acceptance criteria:
- /flow-set varname value sets a variable and saves state
- /flow-set --gate gatename true/false overrides a gate result
- /flow-set --path index validates path before setting
- State saved with correct SHA-256 checksum automatically
- Confirmation prompt before overwriting (bypass with --force)

title: Breakpoint/step-through: pause before specific nodes and advance one at a time
description:
Why: There's no way to pause a flow at a specific point. Auto-advance processes all deterministic nodes in one shot. Developers can't inspect state before a node executes or step through execution interactively.

What: Add breakpoints array to SessionState containing node IDs. When autoAdvanceNodes() reaches a breakpointed node, emit detailed state dump and pause instead of advancing. Add /flow-break skill to set/clear breakpoints. Add /flow-step to advance exactly one node.

Acceptance criteria:
- /flow-break add <nodeId> sets a breakpoint
- /flow-step advances exactly one deterministic node
- State dump at breakpoint shows: current variables, last 10 trace entries, node about to execute
- /flow-break list shows all active breakpoints with node descriptions

title: Execution trace: record state transitions in SessionState for post-hoc debugging
description:
Why: There is no record of how the flow got to its current state. When a while loop ran 4 times, there's no way to see what happened in each iteration. 'Why did my flow skip the else branch?' currently requires guessing.

What: Add trace array to SessionState recording every state transition: {timestamp, fromPath, toPath, nodeKind, event, detail}. Events: advance, branch_taken, loop_enter, loop_exit, condition_eval(result), variable_set(name, value), gate_eval(predicate, result), error. Cap at 200 entries (FIFO).

Acceptance criteria:
- All state transitions recorded in trace
- 200-entry FIFO cap prevents state file bloat
- /flow-trace skill renders trace in readable format
- Trace survives compaction (pre-compact hook preserves last 50 entries)

title: Flow diff: /flow-diff skill to compare two flow versions structurally
description:
Why: When iterating on a flow definition, there's no way to see what changed between versions. Especially painful when flows are AI-generated — the developer can't easily see what the AI changed.

What: Add flowFingerprint() domain function producing normalized text representation of FlowSpec. Add /flow-diff skill comparing two flow definitions (from files, clipboard, or previous session state). Shows: added/removed/changed nodes, changed conditions, changed gate predicates.

Acceptance criteria:
- /flow-diff file1.flow file2.flow shows structural differences
- /flow-diff --session shows diff between active flow and current session definition
- Output highlights changed node kinds, conditions, and gate predicates
- Pure domain function (no external deps)

title: Flow documentation generator: /flow-explain skill
description:
Why: Complex flows (complexity 4-5) are hard to understand, especially for team members who didn't write them. The existing flowComplexityScore() gives a number but no explanation of what makes it complex or how to simplify.

What: New /flow-explain skill that generates: (a) step-by-step narrative of what the flow does, (b) variable dependency graph (which nodes set/use which variables), (c) gate requirements summary, (d) complexity analysis with specific simplification recommendations.

Acceptance criteria:
- Narrative accurately describes flow behavior in plain English
- Variable dependencies show producer -> consumer relationships
- Works for flows of all complexity levels
- Output is concise enough to be useful (not a wall of text)

title: Spawn/await child visibility: show child flow progress in parent render
description:
Why: Spawned children are opaque black boxes. The parent sees `spawn 'fix-auth' [running]` with zero visibility into what the child is doing, whether it's stuck, or how far along it is. `await all` can block for 5 minutes with no progress indication.

What: In renderFlow(), when a spawn node shows [running], also show child's current step and progress (read from child's .prompt-language-{name}/session-state.json). Add /flow-children skill showing all spawned children with full visualizations.

Acceptance criteria:
- spawn [running] shows child's current node position
- /flow-children shows full renderFlow() output for each child
- Handles child state file missing (show 'starting...')
- Performance: child state reads are cached for 1s to avoid excessive I/O

title: Fan-out/reduce pattern: `foreach-spawn` + parallel child aggregation
description:
Why: Parallel fan-out (process each file, test each module, review each PR) is the most common multi-agent pattern. Currently requires verbose manual spawn/await boilerplate. A dedicated construct makes it idiomatic.

What: `foreach-spawn item in ${list} model "..."` spawns one child per item (all in parallel), implicitly awaits all, aggregates child results into parent list variable. Desugars to existing foreach + spawn + await all.

Example:
```
let files = run "find src -name '*.test.ts'"
foreach-spawn file in ${files} model "haiku"
  run: npx vitest ${file}
  let result = prompt "Summarize test results"
end
# ${results} is a JSON array of all child result variables
```

Acceptance criteria:
- foreach-spawn desugars to spawn per item + await all
- Child results aggregated into parent list variable
- Bounded by existing DEFAULT_MAX_FOREACH limit
- model parameter optional

title: Inter-child messaging: `send` / `receive` nodes for parent-child and child-child communication
description:
Why: spawn/await is pure fire-and-forget. Children can't coordinate, share intermediate findings, or request help. This enables debate/critique patterns, pipeline patterns, and supervisor patterns.

What: New nodes: `send "target" "message"` emits to target's inbox (.prompt-language-{name}/inbox.jsonl). `receive` blocks until a message arrives. Parent can send to children and receive from them.

Example:
```
spawn "researcher"
  prompt: Research the auth bug
  send "fixer" "${last_stdout}"
end
spawn "fixer"
  receive
  prompt: Fix the bug described in: ${message}
end
await all
```

Acceptance criteria:
- send/receive implement file-based inbox protocol
- Deadlock prevention: receive has configurable timeout
- Message content filtered for security (same as spawn variable filtering)
- receive sets ${message} and ${message_from} auto-variables

title: Persistent memory across flows: `remember` / `memory:` section
description:
Why: Each flow starts from scratch. For iterative development, there's no way to carry forward what the AI learned. Memory enables: learning from failures, cross-session context, progressive refinement across multiple runs.

What: `memory:` section that persists key-value pairs across flow executions. Stored in .prompt-language/memory.json. New `remember "key" = ${value}` node saves to memory. ${memory.key} reads from memory.

Example:
```
flow:
  prompt: Fix the bug. Previously we tried: ${memory.last_approach}
  let approach = prompt "What approach are you taking?"
  remember "last_approach" = ${approach}
```

Acceptance criteria:
- memory.json persists across flow completions and session restarts
- remember node saves at advancement time
- ${memory.key} resolves in interpolation
- Clear mechanism: bd flow-memory clear or manual delete
- TTL option to prevent stale memory misleading AI

title: Reflection/self-critique: `review max N` composite node
description:
Why: Self-reflection dramatically improves AI output quality. Studies show a review pass catches 30-50% of errors. Currently users must manually construct while/ask loops for this pattern. A dedicated review node makes it first-class and idiomatic.

What: New composite node that implements a built-in reflection loop: (1) execute body, (2) inject self-critique prompt, (3) if critique identifies issues, re-execute with critique as context, (4) repeat up to N times. Desugars to existing until + ask + grounded-by primitives.

Example:
```
review max 3
  prompt: Implement the caching layer
  run: npm test
end
```

Acceptance criteria:
- review keyword parsed and desugared to existing primitives
- Self-critique prompt auto-generated (configurable via prompt: override)
- Iteration count tracked and displayed in flow render
- max default: 2 (one review pass)

title: Checkpoint/resume granularity: track lastCompletedNodePath to prevent partial re-execution
description:
Why: On compaction/resume, autoAdvanceNodes restarts from currentNodePath which may point to a node that was in progress but not completed. This can cause partial re-execution of the node the path points to.

What: Add lastCompletedNodePath to SessionState, updated atomically after each successful node advancement. On resume, start from lastCompletedNodePath + 1 rather than currentNodePath.

Acceptance criteria:
- lastCompletedNodePath always points to a fully-completed node
- Resume starts at the correct next node, never replays a completed one
- Handles edge case: first node has never completed (start from beginning)
- Atomic update prevents split-brain between currentNodePath and lastCompletedNodePath

title: Idempotent run nodes: skip re-execution of successfully-completed nodes on resume
description:
Why: After compaction or session resume, autoAdvanceNodes may re-run a run: node that already executed. Destructive or non-idempotent commands (database migrations, deployments, file deletions) get double-executed.

What: Before executing a run: node, check nodeProgress[node.id] for a completed status with stored exitCode. If node already ran successfully and we're outside a retry loop, skip re-execution and use cached exit variables.

Note: Must NOT skip nodes inside retry/foreach loops (re-execution is intentional there). Scope to top-level and if-branch contexts only.

Acceptance criteria:
- Non-loop run: nodes skip on resume if already completed
- Loop-body run: nodes always re-execute
- Cached last_stdout/last_exit_code restored from nodeProgress
- Deploy/migration style commands benefit immediately

title: Graceful hook timeout escalation: SIGTERM handler saves state before dying
description:
Why: If a hook times out (hung run: command), Claude Code kills the hook process. The state may be partially written — lock acquired, temp file written, but rename not completed. Recovery requires the next hook to detect corruption. A SIGTERM handler can save a consistent snapshot before dying.

What: Add SIGTERM signal handler in each hook entry point that: (1) attempts to save current state, (2) releases lock file, (3) exits cleanly with code 0. Note: SIGTERM is not reliably delivered on Windows (document this limitation).

Acceptance criteria:
- SIGTERM during autoAdvanceNodes saves state and exits cleanly
- Lock file is released (not left as stale)
- Tested on Linux/macOS; Windows limitation documented
- State saved is consistent (not mid-write)

title: Gate result caching: skip re-evaluation of recently-passed gates (TTL: 30s)
description:
Why: Gates like npm test or cargo test take 30-60s. With 5 gates, each task-completed hook takes up to 5 minutes. If the AI makes a small change and re-attempts, all gates re-run even those that already passed. Caching passed gates for a short TTL dramatically reduces gate latency.

What: Add gateEvaluatedAt timestamp to each gate result in gateDiagnostics. In evaluateCompletion, skip re-evaluation of gates that passed within the last N seconds (default 30s). Failed gates are always re-evaluated.

Acceptance criteria:
- Passed gate within TTL: skip evaluation, use cached result
- Failed gate: always re-evaluate
- TTL configurable via PROMPT_LANGUAGE_GATE_CACHE_TTL env var
- Cache miss on first evaluation (no stale false-pass)

title: Write formal grammar spec (EBNF) + railroad diagrams for the DSL
description:
Why: The parser is 773 lines of ad-hoc regex + state machine with no formal grammar spec. This makes it impossible to reason about ambiguities, ensure completeness, communicate the language to contributors, or build alternative tooling (LSP, syntax highlighter, alternative parsers). Every language improvement adds complexity that a formal spec would make manageable.

What: Write an EBNF grammar for the full DSL, validate it against the parser's actual behavior with test cases, generate railroad diagrams, and document edge cases. ~30 productions. The process will likely reveal latent ambiguities requiring parser fixes.

Acceptance criteria:
- EBNF covers all 13 node kinds, all gate forms, all let sources
- Railroad diagrams generated and added to docs/
- Any ambiguities discovered are filed as separate bugs
- New contributors can use the spec to understand parser behavior

title: Add structured data / map variables: `let config = {}`, `let config.host = 'x'`
description:
Why: Related config values are stored as separate flat variables (config_host, config_port), which is fragile and doesn't compose well with spawn (variable passing uses name-based filtering). Map variables would enable passing structured config as a single unit.

What: Support key-value map variables stored as JSON objects internally, accessed via dot notation.

Example:
```
let config = {}
let config.host = "localhost"
let config.port = "8080"
run: curl http://${config.host}:${config.port}/health
```

Note: Dot notation in ${config.host} requires parser and interpolation regex changes. Best done after expression language work.

Acceptance criteria:
- {} initializer creates empty map
- Dot-path assignment and read work
- Maps survive state serialization/deserialization
- filterSpawnVariables() handles map variables correctly

title: Add expression language for let: string concat, ternary, dot-access
description:
Why: Only integer arithmetic is supported in let right-hand-side. String manipulation requires shelling out to echo or using a prompt. Simple operations like concatenating variables, converting case, or getting list length need workarounds.

What: Extend let RHS to support: string concatenation (`let x = ${a} + ' ' + ${b}`), ternary (`let x = ${flag} ? 'yes' : 'no'`), basic string methods (`let x = ${name}.upper`, `let x = ${list}.length`).

Note: The + operator for strings needs to be distinguished from numeric addition — best done after typed variables are available.

Acceptance criteria:
- String concatenation works: let greeting = ${first} + ' ' + ${last}
- Ternary works for simple true/false conditions
- .upper, .lower, .trim, .length work on string/list variables

title: Add typed variable declarations (opt-in): `let count: int = 0`
description:
Why: Everything is stored as strings and coerced ad-hoc. `let x = run 'echo hello world'` stores 'hello world', then `if ${x} > 5` silently returns null. Typed declarations catch errors at assignment rather than at unpredictable downstream use.

What: Optional type annotations: `let count: int = 0`, `let name: string = 'hello'`, `let items: list = []`. Runtime enforces coercion at assignment. Untyped let remains default (fully backward compatible).

Acceptance criteria:
- `let count: int = 'foo'` produces a lint/runtime warning
- Typed variables flow through evaluateCondition and evaluateArithmetic correctly
- Type annotations are purely opt-in — no existing flow breaks

title: Add loop guard clauses (`while ... where condition`)
description:
Why: If a while condition depends on state that hasn't been set up yet (file doesn't exist, variable not set), the loop either enters incorrectly or returns null. A guard clause provides a clean way to express 'only attempt this loop if the precondition holds.'

What: Optional `where` clause on while/until/retry loops. If the where condition is false at loop entry, the loop is skipped entirely.

Example:
```
while tests_fail max 5 where file_exists "app.js"
  prompt: Fix app.js
end
```

Acceptance criteria:
- where condition false: loop body never entered
- where condition true: normal loop behavior
- where is not an existing loop-context keyword — pure addition
- ~40 LOC change

title: VS Code extension: syntax highlighting + inline lint for .pl files
description:
Why: Authoring prompt-language flows in VS Code has no syntax highlighting, autocomplete, or inline error markers. This creates friction for new users and makes editing larger flows error-prone.

What: Build a VS Code extension that provides:
- TextMate grammar for .pl file extension and flow: blocks in markdown
- Inline lint warnings reusing lintFlow() pure function
- 'Did you mean?' suggestions via fuzzyMatch()
- Basic autocomplete for DSL keywords (prompt, run, while, until, if, foreach, let, try, break, continue, spawn, await, done when, etc.)

Acceptance criteria:
- .pl files get syntax coloring in VS Code
- lint warnings show as yellow squiggles
- Extension installable from VS Code marketplace
- Extension installable locally via .vsix

title: Gemini CLI: AfterAgent hook (gate blocking via retry)
description: Write src/hooks/after-agent.mjs — the Gemini CLI equivalent of task-completed.ts. Evaluates gates after each agent response. Gate blocking works differently: instead of exit code 2 hard-blocking, output decision:deny with reason describing what gates failed. Gemini CLI will feed the reason as a new prompt to the agent, creating a retry loop. If all gates pass, output decision:allow. Input includes prompt_response and stop_hook_active fields.
notes: Backlog host integration work. Revisit after the core Claude-first prompt-language surface is complete.

title: Gemini CLI: BeforeAgent hook (user-prompt-submit equivalent)

Reads stdin JSON (session_id, cwd, prompt), loads session state, calls autoAdvanceNodes(), injects flow context via hookSpecificOutput.additionalContext. Blocks the turn with decision:deny if flow is in a bad state. Output: JSON to stdout. Input payload includes prompt field. This is the core hook that drives deterministic flow advancement.
notes: Backlog host integration work. Revisit after the core Claude-first prompt-language surface is complete.

Architecture nearly identical to Claude Code: stdin JSON, exit code 2 blocking, additionalContext injection, config via config.toml. Feature flag required: features.codex_hooks=true. 67k stars, bundled in ChatGPT. Defer until hooks stabilize out of experimental (~2 months). title: Port prompt-language to Gemini CLI

BeforeAgent=user-prompt-submit, AfterAgent=task-completed, AfterTool=post-tool-use, SessionStart=session-start. additionalContext injection is identical. Extension distribution is superior: users install via gemini extensions install from GitHub. Domain and application layer code is 100% reusable; only 4 hook adapter files need rewriting. Key gaps vs Claude Code: no stop hook (cannot block mid-flow interruption), PreCompress is advisory-only (use SessionStart with source=resume as workaround), gate blocking is retry-based via AfterAgent decision:deny instead of hard exit code 2. Parent epic tracking the full Gemini CLI port.

title: Multi-project orchestration: workspace-aware flow execution across monorepo packages
description:
Why: Monorepos are the dominant project structure for teams. Running 'fix all tests across all packages' requires orchestrating flows per package. Without workspace support, users manually spawn per-package flows.

What: Detect pnpm-workspace.yaml, lerna.json, or workspaces in package.json. New CLI command: npx @45ck/prompt-language workspace --flow fix-tests.flow runs flow across all workspace packages using spawn 'pkg' in 'path' internally.

Acceptance criteria:

title: Speculative execution: `race` block spawns multiple approaches, uses first success
description:
Why: Enables speculative execution where multiple approaches are tried in parallel — the AI equivalent of Promise.race(). Multiple fix strategies, redundancy, competitive agents.

What: New `race` node that spawns multiple children in parallel and uses the first successful result, cancelling/ignoring the rest.

Example:
```
race
  spawn "approach-a"
    prompt: Fix by refactoring auth module
    run: npm test
  end
  spawn "approach-b"
    prompt: Fix by adding input validation
    run: npm test
  end
end
# Variables from winner are available
```

Acceptance criteria:
- First successfully-completing child wins
- Losing children: SIGTERM sent (best-effort cleanup)
- Winner variables imported with no prefix
- ${race_winner} auto-variable set to winning spawn name

title: Web playground: try prompt-language DSL in the browser without installing
description:
Why: New users face a cold-start problem — they must install Claude Code, install the plugin, and write a flow before seeing any value. A web playground removes all friction and serves as interactive documentation.

What: Browser-based playground where users can:
- Write a flow in a Monaco editor (with syntax highlighting)

title: Add captureFilePath() deduplication between domain and infrastructure
description: capture-prompt.ts:14 exports captureFilePath() but file-capture-reader.ts builds its own path independently (join(this.baseDir, '.prompt-language/vars', varName)). If the path format diverges, capture writes and reads would break silently. Wire file-capture-reader to use the domain function, or extract a shared constant.
close_reason: Already implemented: file-capture-reader imports CAPTURE_VARS_DIR + captureFilePath from domain/capture-prompt and uses them for read/clear/ensureDir paths.

title: Consolidate or differentiate overlapping fix-and-test/tdd/refactor skills
description: Three skills (fix-and-test, tdd, refactor) all use essentially the same pattern: retry max 5 + npm test + if command_failed + prompt: fix it. The differentiation is subtle (tdd: write test first, refactor: one change at a time). Users may not know which to choose. Either consolidate into one skill with argument-based mode selection, or clearly differentiate their descriptions and flows.
close_reason: Differentiated fix-and-test/tdd/refactor skill guidance with explicit use boundaries and distinct execution rules.

title: Standardize stderr prefix on stop.ts and task-completed.ts
description: stop.ts line 22 and task-completed.ts line 24 write raw result.reason to stderr without the [prompt-language] prefix and without a trailing newline, unlike other hook error messages. Add consistent prefix: '[prompt-language] ' + result.reason + '\n'.
close_reason: Completed in pl-docs-sprint multi-agent team

title: Add hooks.json description field
description: The hooks.json wrapper format supports an optional description field for documentation. The prompt-language hooks.json omits this. Adding 'description: prompt-language flow engine hooks for context injection, gate enforcement, and flow visualization' improves debugging and self-documentation.
notes: Closed during backlog cleanup. Already implemented in the current tree and/or this batch.

This would improve maintainability without changing behavior.
